subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Explain with the help of a labelled ray diagram the formation of final image by an astronomical telescope at infinity. Write the expression for its magnifying power.
  2. The total magnification produced by a compound microscope is 20. The magnification produced by the eyepiece is 5. When the microscope is focussed on a certain object, the distance between the objective and eyepiece is observed to be 14 cm. Calculate the focal lengths of the objective and the eyepiece. (Given that the least distance of distinct vision {tex}=25 {~cm}{/tex})
question_text: Explain with the help of a labelled ray diagram the formation of final image by an astronomical telescope at infinity. Write the expression for its magnifying power. The total magnification produced by a compound microscope is 20. The magnification produced by the eyepiece is 5. When the microscope is focussed on a certain object, the distance between the objective and eyepiece is observed to be 14 cm. Calculate the focal lengths of the objective and the eyepiece. (Given that the least distance of distinct vision {tex}=25 {~cm}{/tex} )
explanation: Light from distant object enters the objective lens \& forms a real image A'B' at {tex}f_o{/tex} . This image {tex}A^{\prime} B^{\prime}{/tex} acts as an object for eye piece and eye piece forms a magnified image at infinity. {tex} \text { Magnifying Power }=f_o / f_e {/tex} Image is formed at least distance of distinct vision {tex} 20=m_o \times m_e {/tex} {tex} m_o=\frac{20}{5}=4 {/tex} {tex} m_e=1+\frac{D}{f_e} {/tex} {tex} f_e=\frac{25}{4} {~cm} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_{{e}}}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{-25}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{4}{25} {/tex} {tex} u_e=-5 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} L={v}_0+\left|u_e\right| {/tex} {tex} {v}_0=9 {~cm} {/tex} Given, {tex}\frac{{v}_0}{u_0}=4{/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}=\frac{1}{f_0} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{f_0}=\frac{1}{9}-\left(-\frac{4}{9}\right) {/tex} {tex} f_0=\frac{9}{5} {~cm} {/tex}
solution: Light from distant object enters the objective lens \& forms a real image A'B' at {tex}f_o{/tex} . This image {tex}A^{\prime} B^{\prime}{/tex} acts as an object for eye piece and eye piece forms a magnified image at infinity. {tex} \text { Magnifying Power }=f_o / f_e {/tex} Image is formed at least distance of distinct vision {tex} 20=m_o \times m_e {/tex} {tex} m_o=\frac{20}{5}=4 {/tex} {tex} m_e=1+\frac{D}{f_e} {/tex} {tex} f_e=\frac{25}{4} {~cm} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_{{e}}}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{-25}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{4}{25} {/tex} {tex} u_e=-5 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} L={v}_0+\left|u_e\right| {/tex} {tex} {v}_0=9 {~cm} {/tex} Given, {tex}\frac{{v}_0}{u_0}=4{/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}=\frac{1}{f_0} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{f_0}=\frac{1}{9}-\left(-\frac{4}{9}\right) {/tex} {tex} f_0=\frac{9}{5} {~cm} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Light from distant object enters the objective lens \& forms a real image A'B' at {tex}f_o{/tex}.
    This image {tex}A^{\prime} B^{\prime}{/tex} acts as an object for eye piece and eye piece forms a magnified image at infinity.
    {tex} \text { Magnifying Power }=f_o / f_e {/tex}
  2. Image is formed at least distance of distinct vision
    {tex} 20=m_o \times m_e {/tex}
    {tex} m_o=\frac{20}{5}=4 {/tex}
    {tex} m_e=1+\frac{D}{f_e} {/tex}
    {tex} f_e=\frac{25}{4} {~cm} {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_{{e}}}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e} {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{1}{-25}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{4}{25} {/tex}
    {tex} u_e=-5 {~cm} {/tex}
    {tex} L={v}_0+\left|u_e\right| {/tex}
    {tex} {v}_0=9 {~cm} {/tex}
    Given, {tex}\frac{{v}_0}{u_0}=4{/tex}
    {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}=\frac{1}{f_0} {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{1}{f_0}=\frac{1}{9}-\left(-\frac{4}{9}\right) {/tex}
    {tex} f_0=\frac{9}{5} {~cm} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a ray diagram to show the image formation by a compound microscope. Obtain the expression for the total magnification of the microscope when the final image is formed at infinity.
  2. In a compound microscope, an object is placed at a distance of 1.5 cm from the objective of focal length 1.25 cm. The eyepiece has a focal length of 5 cm. The final image is formed at infinity. Calculate the distance between the objective and the eyepiece.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram to show the image formation by a compound microscope. Obtain the expression for the total magnification of the microscope when the final image is formed at infinity. In a compound microscope, an object is placed at a distance of 1.5 cm from the objective of focal length 1.25 cm. The eyepiece has a focal length of 5 cm. The final image is formed at infinity. Calculate the distance between the objective and the eyepiece.
explanation: Magnification produced by objective {tex}{m}_{0}=\frac{{h}^{\prime}}{{h}}=\frac{{L}}{{f}_{{o}}}{/tex} Magnification produced by eye-piece {tex}{m}_{{e}}=1+\frac{{D}}{{f}_{{e}}}{/tex} If the final image is formed at infinity {tex}{m}_{{e}}=\frac{{D}}{{f}_{{e}}}{/tex} Total magnification {tex}{m}={m}_{0} \times {m}_{{e}}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{L}{f_{o}}\right)\left(\frac{D}{f_{e}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}_{{o}}}-\frac{1}{{u}_{{o}}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}_{{o}}}-\frac{1}{(-1.5)}=\frac{1}{1.25}{/tex} {tex}{v}_{{o}}=7.5 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}{L}=\left|{v}_{{o}}\right|+\left|{f}_{{e}}\right| {/tex} {tex}\text { as final image is formed at infinity }{/tex} {tex}\left({v}_{e}=\infty, u_{e}=f_{e}\right){/tex} {tex}{L}=7.5+5{/tex} {tex}{~L}=12.5 {~cm}{/tex}
solution: Magnification produced by objective {tex}{m}_{0}=\frac{{h}^{\prime}}{{h}}=\frac{{L}}{{f}_{{o}}}{/tex} Magnification produced by eye-piece {tex}{m}_{{e}}=1+\frac{{D}}{{f}_{{e}}}{/tex} If the final image is formed at infinity {tex}{m}_{{e}}=\frac{{D}}{{f}_{{e}}}{/tex} Total magnification {tex}{m}={m}_{0} \times {m}_{{e}}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{L}{f_{o}}\right)\left(\frac{D}{f_{e}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}_{{o}}}-\frac{1}{{u}_{{o}}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}_{{o}}}-\frac{1}{(-1.5)}=\frac{1}{1.25}{/tex} {tex}{v}_{{o}}=7.5 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}{L}=\left|{v}_{{o}}\right|+\left|{f}_{{e}}\right| {/tex} {tex}\text { as final image is formed at infinity }{/tex} {tex}\left({v}_{e}=\infty, u_{e}=f_{e}\right){/tex} {tex}{L}=7.5+5{/tex} {tex}{~L}=12.5 {~cm}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Magnification produced by objective
    {tex}{m}_{0}=\frac{{h}^{\prime}}{{h}}=\frac{{L}}{{f}_{{o}}}{/tex}
    Magnification produced by eye-piece
    {tex}{m}_{{e}}=1+\frac{{D}}{{f}_{{e}}}{/tex}
    If the final image is formed at infinity
    {tex}{m}_{{e}}=\frac{{D}}{{f}_{{e}}}{/tex}
    Total magnification
    {tex}{m}={m}_{0} \times {m}_{{e}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\left(\frac{L}{f_{o}}\right)\left(\frac{D}{f_{e}}\right){/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{1}{{v}_{{o}}}-\frac{1}{{u}_{{o}}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{0}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{{v}_{{o}}}-\frac{1}{(-1.5)}=\frac{1}{1.25}{/tex}
    {tex}{v}_{{o}}=7.5 {~cm}{/tex}
    {tex}{L}=\left|{v}_{{o}}\right|+\left|{f}_{{e}}\right| {/tex}{tex}\text { as final image is formed at infinity }{/tex}{tex}\left({v}_{e}=\infty, u_{e}=f_{e}\right){/tex}
    {tex}{L}=7.5+5{/tex}
    {tex}{~L}=12.5 {~cm}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: The two surfaces of a biconvex lens are of radius of curvature '{tex}R{/tex}' each. Obtain the condition under which its focal length '{tex}f{/tex}' be equal to '{tex}R{/tex}'. If one of the two surfaces of this lens is made plane, what will be the new focal length of the lens?
question_text: The two surfaces of a biconvex lens are of radius of curvature ' {tex}R{/tex} ' each. Obtain the condition under which its focal length ' {tex}f{/tex} ' be equal to ' {tex}R{/tex} '. If one of the two surfaces of this lens is made plane, what will be the new focal length of the lens?
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=({n}-1)\left(\frac{1}{{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{R}_{2}}\right){/tex} For {tex}{f}={R}, {R}_{1}={R}, {R}_{2}=-{R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{R}}=({n}-1)\left(\frac{1}{{R}}+\frac{1}{{R}}\right){/tex} {tex}{n}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} For convex lens {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=({n}-1)\left(\frac{2}{{R}}\right){/tex} For plano Convex lens {tex}{R}_{1}={R} {R}_{2}=\infty{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{p}}=({n}-1)\left(\frac{1}{{R}}\right){/tex} {tex}f_{{p}}=2 {f}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=({n}-1)\left(\frac{1}{{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{R}_{2}}\right){/tex} For {tex}{f}={R}, {R}_{1}={R}, {R}_{2}=-{R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{R}}=({n}-1)\left(\frac{1}{{R}}+\frac{1}{{R}}\right){/tex} {tex}{n}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} For convex lens {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=({n}-1)\left(\frac{2}{{R}}\right){/tex} For plano Convex lens {tex}{R}_{1}={R} {R}_{2}=\infty{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{p}}=({n}-1)\left(\frac{1}{{R}}\right){/tex} {tex}f_{{p}}=2 {f}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=({n}-1)\left(\frac{1}{{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{R}_{2}}\right){/tex}
For {tex}{f}={R}, {R}_{1}={R}, {R}_{2}=-{R}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{{R}}=({n}-1)\left(\frac{1}{{R}}+\frac{1}{{R}}\right){/tex}
{tex}{n}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
For convex lens
{tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=({n}-1)\left(\frac{2}{{R}}\right){/tex}
For plano Convex lens {tex}{R}_{1}={R} {R}_{2}=\infty{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{p}}=({n}-1)\left(\frac{1}{{R}}\right){/tex}
{tex}f_{{p}}=2 {f}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A convex lens of focal length 10 cm, a concave lens of focal length 15 cm and a third lens of unknown focal length are placed coaxially in contact. If the focal length of the combination is +12 cm, find the nature and focal length of the third lens, if all lenses are thin. Will the answer change if the lenses were thick?
question_text: A convex lens of focal length 10 cm, a concave lens of focal length 15 cm and a third lens of unknown focal length are placed coaxially in contact. If the focal length of the combination is +12 cm, find the nature and focal length of the third lens, if all lenses are thin. Will the answer change if the lenses were thick?
explanation: {tex} \frac{1}{{f}} =\frac{1}{{f}_1}+\frac{1}{{f}_2}+\frac{1}{{f}_3} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{12} =\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{{f}_3} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{f}_3} =\frac{5-6+4}{60} {/tex} {tex} {f}_3 =20 {~cm} {/tex} Nature: Convex Yes
solution: {tex} \frac{1}{{f}} =\frac{1}{{f}_1}+\frac{1}{{f}_2}+\frac{1}{{f}_3} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{12} =\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{{f}_3} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{f}_3} =\frac{5-6+4}{60} {/tex} {tex} {f}_3 =20 {~cm} {/tex} Nature: Convex Yes
solution_html:
{tex} \frac{1}{{f}} =\frac{1}{{f}_1}+\frac{1}{{f}_2}+\frac{1}{{f}_3} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{12} =\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{{f}_3} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{{f}_3} =\frac{5-6+4}{60} {/tex}
{tex} {f}_3 =20 {~cm} {/tex}
Nature: Convex
Yes

subject: Physics
question_html: Using the mirror equation and the formula of magnification, deduce that "the virtual image produced by a convex mirror is always diminished in size and is located between the pole and the focus."
question_text: Using the mirror equation and the formula of magnification, deduce that "the virtual image produced by a convex mirror is always diminished in size and is located between the pole and the focus."
explanation: Let, {tex}{u}={nf}{/tex} From the Mirror formula, {tex} \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{n f} {/tex} {tex} v =\frac{n f}{n+1} {/tex} {tex} n =+v e \therefore v<f {/tex} {tex} m =\frac{-v}{u}=\frac{1}{n+1} {/tex} m is always positive & less than 1.
solution: Let, {tex}{u}={nf}{/tex} From the Mirror formula, {tex} \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{n f} {/tex} {tex} v =\frac{n f}{n+1} {/tex} {tex} n =+v e \therefore v<f {/tex} {tex} m =\frac{-v}{u}=\frac{1}{n+1} {/tex} m is always positive & less than 1.
solution_html:
Let, {tex}{u}={nf}{/tex}
From the Mirror formula,
{tex} \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{n f} {/tex}
{tex} v =\frac{n f}{n+1} {/tex}
{tex} n =+v e \therefore v<f {/tex}
{tex} m =\frac{-v}{u}=\frac{1}{n+1} {/tex}
m is always positive & less than 1.

question_html: A rectangular glass slab {tex}A B C D{/tex} (refractive index 1.5) is surrounded by a transparent liquid (refractive index 1.25) as shown in the figure. A ray of light is incident on face AB at an angle i such that it is refracted out grazing the face AD. Find the value of angle i.
question_text: A rectangular glass slab {tex}A B C D{/tex} (refractive index 1.5) is surrounded by a transparent liquid (refractive index 1.25) as shown in the figure. A ray of light is incident on face AB at an angle i such that it is refracted out grazing the face AD. Find the value of angle i.
explanation: For glass- liquid interface {tex} \sin {i}_c =\frac{1}{{n}_{21}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1.25}{1.5} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{5}{6} {/tex} {tex} i_c+r =90^{\circ} {/tex} {tex} \sin r=\sqrt{1-\cos ^2 r} =\frac{\sqrt{11}}{6} {/tex} Since {tex} \frac{\operatorname{Sin} i}{\operatorname{Sin} r}=n {/tex} Therefore, {tex}\operatorname{Sin} i=\frac{\sqrt{11}}{4}{/tex} or {tex}i=\operatorname{Sin}^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{11}}{4}{/tex}
solution: For glass- liquid interface {tex} \sin {i}_c =\frac{1}{{n}_{21}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1.25}{1.5} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{5}{6} {/tex} {tex} i_c+r =90^{\circ} {/tex} {tex} \sin r=\sqrt{1-\cos ^2 r} =\frac{\sqrt{11}}{6} {/tex} Since {tex} \frac{\operatorname{Sin} i}{\operatorname{Sin} r}=n {/tex} Therefore, {tex}\operatorname{Sin} i=\frac{\sqrt{11}}{4}{/tex} or {tex}i=\operatorname{Sin}^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{11}}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
For glass- liquid interface
{tex} \sin {i}_c =\frac{1}{{n}_{21}} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1.25}{1.5} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{5}{6} {/tex}
{tex} i_c+r =90^{\circ} {/tex}
{tex} \sin r=\sqrt{1-\cos ^2 r} =\frac{\sqrt{11}}{6} {/tex}
Since
{tex} \frac{\operatorname{Sin} i}{\operatorname{Sin} r}=n {/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\operatorname{Sin} i=\frac{\sqrt{11}}{4}{/tex} or {tex}i=\operatorname{Sin}^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{11}}{4}{/tex}

question_html: An equiconvex lens is made of glass of refractive index 1.55. If the focal length of the lens is 15.0 cm, calculate the radius of curvature of its surfaces.
question_text: An equiconvex lens is made of glass of refractive index 1.55. If the focal length of the lens is 15.0 cm, calculate the radius of curvature of its surfaces.
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\left({n}_{21}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{R}_{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}{R}_{1}={R} \text { and } {R}_{2}=-{R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{15}=(1.55-1)\left[\frac{2}{{R}}\right]{/tex} {tex}{R}=16.5 {~cm}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\left({n}_{21}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{R}_{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}{R}_{1}={R} \text { and } {R}_{2}=-{R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{15}=(1.55-1)\left[\frac{2}{{R}}\right]{/tex} {tex}{R}=16.5 {~cm}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\left({n}_{21}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{R}_{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}{R}_{1}={R} \text { and } {R}_{2}=-{R}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{15}=(1.55-1)\left[\frac{2}{{R}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}{R}=16.5 {~cm}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A point object is placed in air at a distance {tex}{R} / 3{/tex} in front of a convex surface of radius of curvature R , separating air from a medium of refractive index {tex}{n}( \lt 4){/tex}. Find the nature and position of the image formed.
question_text: A point object is placed in air at a distance {tex}{R} / 3{/tex} in front of a convex surface of radius of curvature R , separating air from a medium of refractive index {tex}{n}( \lt 4){/tex} . Find the nature and position of the image formed.
explanation: For refraction at convex surface {tex} \frac{{n}_1}{-{u}}+\frac{{n}_2}{{v}}=\frac{{n}_2-{n}_1}{{R}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{n}{v} =\frac{[n-1-3]}{R} {/tex} {tex} v =\frac{n R}{n-4} {/tex} For all values of {tex}{n}<4{/tex} , the value of v is negative and greater than R Therefore the nature of image is virtual and is formed in front of convex surface.
solution: For refraction at convex surface {tex} \frac{{n}_1}{-{u}}+\frac{{n}_2}{{v}}=\frac{{n}_2-{n}_1}{{R}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{n}{v} =\frac{[n-1-3]}{R} {/tex} {tex} v =\frac{n R}{n-4} {/tex} For all values of {tex}{n}<4{/tex} , the value of v is negative and greater than R Therefore the nature of image is virtual and is formed in front of convex surface.
solution_html:
For refraction at convex surface
{tex} \frac{{n}_1}{-{u}}+\frac{{n}_2}{{v}}=\frac{{n}_2-{n}_1}{{R}} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{n}{v} =\frac{[n-1-3]}{R} {/tex}
{tex} v =\frac{n R}{n-4} {/tex}
For all values of {tex}{n}<4{/tex}, the value of v is negative and greater than R
Therefore the nature of image is virtual and is formed in front of convex surface.

subject: Physics
question_html: Draw a ray diagram showing the image formation when a concave mirror produces a real, inverted and magnified image of an object and hence obtain the mirror formula.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram showing the image formation when a concave mirror produces a real, inverted and magnified image of an object and hence obtain the mirror formula.
explanation: In similar triangles {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F{/tex} and {tex}\triangle M P F{/tex} {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{M P}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P} (\because M P=A B){/tex} ...(1) In similar triangles {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} P{/tex} and {tex}\triangle A B P{/tex} {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{P B^{\prime}}{P B}{/tex} ...(2) from equation (1) and (2) {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{P B^{\prime}}{P B}{/tex} {tex}\frac{P B^{\prime}-P F}{F P}=\frac{P B^{\prime}}{P B}{/tex} {tex}\frac{(-{v})-(-{f})}{(-{f})}=\frac{(-{v})}{(-{u})}{/tex} on solving we get {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
solution: In similar triangles {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F{/tex} and {tex}\triangle M P F{/tex} {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{M P}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P} (\because M P=A B){/tex} ...(1) In similar triangles {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} P{/tex} and {tex}\triangle A B P{/tex} {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{P B^{\prime}}{P B}{/tex} ...(2) from equation (1) and (2) {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{P B^{\prime}}{P B}{/tex} {tex}\frac{P B^{\prime}-P F}{F P}=\frac{P B^{\prime}}{P B}{/tex} {tex}\frac{(-{v})-(-{f})}{(-{f})}=\frac{(-{v})}{(-{u})}{/tex} on solving we get {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
solution_html:
In similar triangles
{tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F{/tex} and {tex}\triangle M P F{/tex}
{tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{M P}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P} (\because M P=A B){/tex} ...(1)
In similar triangles {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} P{/tex} and {tex}\triangle A B P{/tex}
{tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{P B^{\prime}}{P B}{/tex} ...(2)
from equation (1) and (2)
{tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{P B^{\prime}}{P B}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{P B^{\prime}-P F}{F P}=\frac{P B^{\prime}}{P B}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{(-{v})-(-{f})}{(-{f})}=\frac{(-{v})}{(-{u})}{/tex}
on solving we get
{tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}

question_html: Find the focal length of plano-convex lens of refractive index 1.5 and radius of curvature 10 cm when it is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.25.
question_text: Find the focal length of plano-convex lens of refractive index 1.5 and radius of curvature 10 cm when it is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.25.
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\left(\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{R}_{2}}\right]{/tex} For plano convex lens {tex}{R}_{1}={R}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{2}=\infty{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\left(\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}-1\right) \frac{1}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1.5}{1.25}-1\right) \times \frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{50}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {f}=50 {~cm}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\left(\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{R}_{2}}\right]{/tex} For plano convex lens {tex}{R}_{1}={R}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{2}=\infty{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\left(\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}-1\right) \frac{1}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1.5}{1.25}-1\right) \times \frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{50}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {f}=50 {~cm}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\left(\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{R}_{2}}\right]{/tex}
For plano convex lens
{tex}{R}_{1}={R}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{2}=\infty{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\left(\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}-1\right) \frac{1}{{R}}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{1.5}{1.25}-1\right) \times \frac{1}{10}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{50}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {f}=50 {~cm}{/tex}

question_html: A point source of light in air is kept at a distance of 12 cm in front of a convex spherical surface of glass of refractive index 1.5 and radius of curvature 30 cm. Find the nature and position of the image formed.
question_text: A point source of light in air is kept at a distance of 12 cm in front of a convex spherical surface of glass of refractive index 1.5 and radius of curvature 30 cm. Find the nature and position of the image formed.
explanation: {tex} \frac{{n}_2}{{v}}-\frac{{n}_1}{{u}}=\frac{{n}_2-{n}_1}{{R}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1.5}{{v}}-\frac{1}{(-12)}=\frac{1.5-1}{30} {/tex} {tex} {v}=-22.5 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} \text { Image is virtual and erect. } {/tex}
solution: {tex} \frac{{n}_2}{{v}}-\frac{{n}_1}{{u}}=\frac{{n}_2-{n}_1}{{R}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1.5}{{v}}-\frac{1}{(-12)}=\frac{1.5-1}{30} {/tex} {tex} {v}=-22.5 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} \text { Image is virtual and erect. } {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \frac{{n}_2}{{v}}-\frac{{n}_1}{{u}}=\frac{{n}_2-{n}_1}{{R}} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1.5}{{v}}-\frac{1}{(-12)}=\frac{1.5-1}{30} {/tex}
{tex} {v}=-22.5 {~cm} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Image is virtual and erect. } {/tex}

question_html: Two concave lenses A and B , each of focal length 8.0 cm are arranged coaxially 16 cm apart as shown in figure. An object {tex}P{/tex} is placed at a distance of 4.0 cm from A. Find the position and nature of the final image formed.
question_text: Two concave lenses A and B , each of focal length 8.0 cm are arranged coaxially 16 cm apart as shown in figure. An object {tex}P{/tex} is placed at a distance of 4.0 cm from A. Find the position and nature of the final image formed.
explanation: For the first lens:- {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_1}-\frac{1}{{u}_1}=\frac{1}{{f}_1} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_1}+\frac{1}{4}=-\frac{1}{8} {/tex} {tex} {v}_1=-\frac{8}{3} {~cm} {/tex} For the second lens: - {tex} {u}_2=-16-\frac{8}{3}=-\frac{56}{3} {~cm} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_2}-\left(-\frac{3}{56}\right)=-\frac{1}{8} {/tex} {tex} {v}_2=-5.6 {~cm} {/tex}
solution: For the first lens:- {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_1}-\frac{1}{{u}_1}=\frac{1}{{f}_1} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_1}+\frac{1}{4}=-\frac{1}{8} {/tex} {tex} {v}_1=-\frac{8}{3} {~cm} {/tex} For the second lens: - {tex} {u}_2=-16-\frac{8}{3}=-\frac{56}{3} {~cm} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_2}-\left(-\frac{3}{56}\right)=-\frac{1}{8} {/tex} {tex} {v}_2=-5.6 {~cm} {/tex}
solution_html:
For the first lens:-
{tex} \frac{1}{{v}_1}-\frac{1}{{u}_1}=\frac{1}{{f}_1} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{{v}_1}+\frac{1}{4}=-\frac{1}{8} {/tex}
{tex} {v}_1=-\frac{8}{3} {~cm} {/tex}
For the second lens: -
{tex} {u}_2=-16-\frac{8}{3}=-\frac{56}{3} {~cm} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{{v}_2}-\left(-\frac{3}{56}\right)=-\frac{1}{8} {/tex}
{tex} {v}_2=-5.6 {~cm} {/tex}

question_html: A double convex lens of glass has both faces of the same radius of curvature 17 cm. Find its focal length if it is immersed in water. The refractive indices of glass and water are 1.5 and 1.33 respectively.
question_text: A double convex lens of glass has both faces of the same radius of curvature 17 cm. Find its focal length if it is immersed in water. The refractive indices of glass and water are 1.5 and 1.33 respectively.
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{n_{g}}{n_{w}}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex} For double convex lens {tex}{R}_{1}={R}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{2}=-{R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{1.5}{1.33}-1\right)\left(\frac{2}{R}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1.5-1.33}{1.33}\right)\left(\frac{2}{17}\right){/tex} {tex}f=66.5 {~cm}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{n_{g}}{n_{w}}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex} For double convex lens {tex}{R}_{1}={R}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{2}=-{R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{1.5}{1.33}-1\right)\left(\frac{2}{R}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1.5-1.33}{1.33}\right)\left(\frac{2}{17}\right){/tex} {tex}f=66.5 {~cm}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{n_{g}}{n_{w}}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex}
For double convex lens {tex}{R}_{1}={R}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{2}=-{R}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{1.5}{1.33}-1\right)\left(\frac{2}{R}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{1.5-1.33}{1.33}\right)\left(\frac{2}{17}\right){/tex}
{tex}f=66.5 {~cm}{/tex}

question_text: A light beam converges at a point O. In the path of this beam, a concave lens of focal length 15 cm is placed at a distance of 10 cm before point O. The beam now converges at a point {tex}{O}^{\prime}{/tex} . Find the magnitude and the direction of shift OO'.
explanation: {tex} \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{10} {/tex} On solving:- {tex} {v}=30 {~cm} {/tex} Magnitude of {tex}{OO}^{\prime}=30-10=20 {~cm}{/tex} Direction is away from the lens.
solution: {tex} \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{10} {/tex} On solving:- {tex} {v}=30 {~cm} {/tex} Magnitude of {tex}{OO}^{\prime}=30-10=20 {~cm}{/tex} Direction is away from the lens.
solution_html:
{tex} \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{10} {/tex}
On solving:-
{tex} {v}=30 {~cm} {/tex}
Magnitude of {tex}{OO}^{\prime}=30-10=20 {~cm}{/tex}
Direction is away from the lens.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. An object is placed 30 cm from a thin convex lens of focal length 10 cm. The lens forms a sharp image on a screen. If a thin concave lens is placed in contact with the convex lens, the sharp image on the screen is formed when the screen is moved by 45 cm from its initial position. Calculate the focal length of the concave lens.
  2. Calculate the angle of minimum deviation of an equilateral prism. The refractive index of the prism is {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}. Calculate the angle of incidence for this case of minimum deviation also.
question_text: An object is placed 30 cm from a thin convex lens of focal length 10 cm. The lens forms a sharp image on a screen. If a thin concave lens is placed in contact with the convex lens, the sharp image on the screen is formed when the screen is moved by 45 cm from its initial position. Calculate the focal length of the concave lens. Calculate the angle of minimum deviation of an equilateral prism. The refractive index of the prism is {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} . Calculate the angle of incidence for this case of minimum deviation also.
explanation: For real image form by Convex lens {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}=\frac{1}{{v}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{u}_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{10}=\frac{1}{{v}_{1}}-\frac{1}{(-30)}{/tex} {tex}{v}_{1}=15 {~cm}{/tex} For Combination of lenses, let the focal length of combination of lens is {tex}f_{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{3}}=\frac{1}{{v}_{3}}-\frac{1}{{u}_{3}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{3}}=\frac{1}{(15+45)}+\frac{1}{30}{/tex} {tex}{f}_{3}=20 {~cm}{/tex} Let the focal length of concave lens is {tex}f_{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{3}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}+\frac{1}{{f}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{2}}=\frac{1}{20}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}{f}_{2}=-20 {~cm}{/tex} Angle of minimum deviation {tex}\mu=\frac{\operatorname{Sin} \frac{\left({A}+\delta_{{m}}\right)}{2}}{\operatorname{Sin} \frac{{~A}}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3}=\frac{\operatorname{Sin} \frac{\left(60^{\circ}+\delta_{{m}}\right)}{2}}{\operatorname{Sin} 30}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\operatorname{Sin} \frac{\left(A+\delta_{m}\right)}{2}{/tex} {tex}60^{\circ}=\frac{\left(A+\delta_{m}\right)}{2}{/tex} {tex}\delta_{{m}}=60^{\circ}{/tex} Angle of incidence {tex}i+e=A+\delta{/tex} {tex}2 i=A+\delta_{m}{/tex} {tex}i=\frac{A+\delta_{m}}{2}{/tex} {tex}i=60^{\circ}{/tex}
solution: For real image form by Convex lens {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}=\frac{1}{{v}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{u}_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{10}=\frac{1}{{v}_{1}}-\frac{1}{(-30)}{/tex} {tex}{v}_{1}=15 {~cm}{/tex} For Combination of lenses, let the focal length of combination of lens is {tex}f_{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{3}}=\frac{1}{{v}_{3}}-\frac{1}{{u}_{3}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{3}}=\frac{1}{(15+45)}+\frac{1}{30}{/tex} {tex}{f}_{3}=20 {~cm}{/tex} Let the focal length of concave lens is {tex}f_{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{3}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}+\frac{1}{{f}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{2}}=\frac{1}{20}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}{f}_{2}=-20 {~cm}{/tex} Angle of minimum deviation {tex}\mu=\frac{\operatorname{Sin} \frac{\left({A}+\delta_{{m}}\right)}{2}}{\operatorname{Sin} \frac{{~A}}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3}=\frac{\operatorname{Sin} \frac{\left(60^{\circ}+\delta_{{m}}\right)}{2}}{\operatorname{Sin} 30}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\operatorname{Sin} \frac{\left(A+\delta_{m}\right)}{2}{/tex} {tex}60^{\circ}=\frac{\left(A+\delta_{m}\right)}{2}{/tex} {tex}\delta_{{m}}=60^{\circ}{/tex} Angle of incidence {tex}i+e=A+\delta{/tex} {tex}2 i=A+\delta_{m}{/tex} {tex}i=\frac{A+\delta_{m}}{2}{/tex} {tex}i=60^{\circ}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. For real image form by Convex lens
    {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}=\frac{1}{{v}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{u}_{1}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{10}=\frac{1}{{v}_{1}}-\frac{1}{(-30)}{/tex}
    {tex}{v}_{1}=15 {~cm}{/tex}
    For Combination of lenses, let the focal length of combination of lens is {tex}f_{3}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{3}}=\frac{1}{{v}_{3}}-\frac{1}{{u}_{3}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{3}}=\frac{1}{(15+45)}+\frac{1}{30}{/tex}
    {tex}{f}_{3}=20 {~cm}{/tex}
    Let the focal length of concave lens is {tex}f_{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{3}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}+\frac{1}{{f}_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{2}}=\frac{1}{20}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
    {tex}{f}_{2}=-20 {~cm}{/tex}
  2. Angle of minimum deviation
    {tex}\mu=\frac{\operatorname{Sin} \frac{\left({A}+\delta_{{m}}\right)}{2}}{\operatorname{Sin} \frac{{~A}}{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\sqrt{3}=\frac{\operatorname{Sin} \frac{\left(60^{\circ}+\delta_{{m}}\right)}{2}}{\operatorname{Sin} 30}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\operatorname{Sin} \frac{\left(A+\delta_{m}\right)}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}60^{\circ}=\frac{\left(A+\delta_{m}\right)}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\delta_{{m}}=60^{\circ}{/tex}
    Angle of incidence
    {tex}i+e=A+\delta{/tex}
    {tex}2 i=A+\delta_{m}{/tex}
    {tex}i=\frac{A+\delta_{m}}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}i=60^{\circ}{/tex}

Find the nature and position of the image when viewed from side {tex}B{/tex} . Show the image formation by drawing a ray diagram.
explanation: For light going from denser to rarer medium {tex} \frac{{n}_2}{-{u}}+\frac{{n}_1}{{v}}=\frac{{n}_1-{n}_2}{{R}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1.5}{8.75}+\frac{1}{{v}}=\frac{(1-1.5)}{-7} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{v}=\frac{0.5}{7}-\frac{1.5}{8.75} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{-1}{10} {/tex} {tex} {v}=-10 {~cm} {/tex} Nature of image is virtual
solution: For light going from denser to rarer medium {tex} \frac{{n}_2}{-{u}}+\frac{{n}_1}{{v}}=\frac{{n}_1-{n}_2}{{R}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1.5}{8.75}+\frac{1}{{v}}=\frac{(1-1.5)}{-7} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{v}=\frac{0.5}{7}-\frac{1.5}{8.75} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{-1}{10} {/tex} {tex} {v}=-10 {~cm} {/tex} Nature of image is virtual
solution_html:
For light going from denser to rarer medium
{tex} \frac{{n}_2}{-{u}}+\frac{{n}_1}{{v}}=\frac{{n}_1-{n}_2}{{R}} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1.5}{8.75}+\frac{1}{{v}}=\frac{(1-1.5)}{-7} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{v}=\frac{0.5}{7}-\frac{1.5}{8.75} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{-1}{10} {/tex}
{tex} {v}=-10 {~cm} {/tex}
Nature of image is virtual

It is placed coaxially in contact with a thin equiconvex lens of same radius of curvature of refractive index {tex}{n}_{2}{/tex}. Obtain the power of the combination lens.
question_text: Write any two advantages of a compound microscope over a simple microscope. Draw a ray diagram for the image formation at the near point by a compound microscope and A thin planoconcave lens with its curved face of radius of curvature R is made of glass of refractive index {tex}{n}_{1}{/tex} . It is placed coaxially in contact with a thin equiconvex lens of same radius of curvature of refractive index {tex}{n}_{2}{/tex} . Obtain the power of the combination lens.
explanation: Advantages Larger magnification Brighter image Any other valid advantage The lens nearest the object, called the objective, forms a real, inverted, magnified image of the object. This serves as the object for the second lens, the eye piece, functions like a simple microscope and produces final image which is enlarged and virtual. Power of plano concave lens {tex}={P}_1=\frac{-\left({n}_1-1\right)}{R}{/tex} Power of convex lens {tex} ={P}_2 =\left({n}_2-1\right)\left(\frac{2}{R}\right) {/tex} {tex} {P} ={P}_1+{P}_2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\left(2 {n}_2-{n}_1-1\right)}{R} {/tex}
solution: Advantages Larger magnification Brighter image Any other valid advantage The lens nearest the object, called the objective, forms a real, inverted, magnified image of the object. This serves as the object for the second lens, the eye piece, functions like a simple microscope and produces final image which is enlarged and virtual. Power of plano concave lens {tex}={P}_1=\frac{-\left({n}_1-1\right)}{R}{/tex} Power of convex lens {tex} ={P}_2 =\left({n}_2-1\right)\left(\frac{2}{R}\right) {/tex} {tex} {P} ={P}_1+{P}_2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\left(2 {n}_2-{n}_1-1\right)}{R} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Advantages
    1. Larger magnification
    2. Brighter image
      Any other valid advantage

      The lens nearest the object, called the objective, forms a real, inverted, magnified image of the object. This serves as the object for the second lens, the eye piece, functions like a simple microscope and produces final image which is enlarged and virtual.
  2. Power of plano concave lens {tex}={P}_1=\frac{-\left({n}_1-1\right)}{R}{/tex}
    Power of convex lens {tex} ={P}_2 =\left({n}_2-1\right)\left(\frac{2}{R}\right) {/tex}
    {tex} {P} ={P}_1+{P}_2 {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{\left(2 {n}_2-{n}_1-1\right)}{R} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a ray diagram of a reflecting telescope (Cassegrain) and explain the formation of image. State two important advantages that a reflecting telescope has over a refracting telescope.
  2. In a refracting telescope, the focal length of the objective is 50 times the focal length of the eyepiece. When the final image is formed at infinity, the length of the tube is 102 cm.
    explain it. power of the combination lens. Find the focal lengths of the two lenses.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram of a reflecting telescope (Cassegrain) and explain the formation of image. State two important advantages that a reflecting telescope has over a refracting telescope. In a refracting telescope, the focal length of the objective is 50 times the focal length of the eyepiece. When the final image is formed at infinity, the length of the tube is 102 cm. explain it. power of the combination lens. Find the focal lengths of the two lenses.
explanation: The parallel rays from a distant object are reflected by a large concave mirror. These rays are then reflected by a convex mirror placed just before the focus of concave mirror and are converged to a point outside the hole. The final image is viewed through eye piece. No chromatic aberration. Less spherical aberration Less mechanical support required Brighter Image High resolving power. High magnifying power For image at infinity {tex} \left|{f}_0\right|+\left|{f}_{{e}}\right|={L} {/tex} According to question {tex} {f}_0 =50 \times {f}_{{e}} {/tex} {tex} {f}_{{e}} +50 {fe}=102 {/tex} {tex} {f}_{{e}} =2 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} {f}_0 =100 {~cm} {/tex}
solution: The parallel rays from a distant object are reflected by a large concave mirror. These rays are then reflected by a convex mirror placed just before the focus of concave mirror and are converged to a point outside the hole. The final image is viewed through eye piece. No chromatic aberration. Less spherical aberration Less mechanical support required Brighter Image High resolving power. High magnifying power For image at infinity {tex} \left|{f}_0\right|+\left|{f}_{{e}}\right|={L} {/tex} According to question {tex} {f}_0 =50 \times {f}_{{e}} {/tex} {tex} {f}_{{e}} +50 {fe}=102 {/tex} {tex} {f}_{{e}} =2 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} {f}_0 =100 {~cm} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. The parallel rays from a distant object are reflected by a large concave mirror. These rays are then reflected by a convex mirror placed just before the focus of concave mirror and are converged to a point outside the hole. The final image is viewed through eye piece.
    1. No chromatic aberration.
    2. Less spherical aberration
    3. Less mechanical support required
    4. Brighter Image
    5. High resolving power.
    6. High magnifying power
  2. For image at infinity
    {tex} \left|{f}_0\right|+\left|{f}_{{e}}\right|={L} {/tex}
    According to question
    {tex} {f}_0 =50 \times {f}_{{e}} {/tex}
    {tex} {f}_{{e}} +50 {fe}=102 {/tex}
    {tex} {f}_{{e}} =2 {~cm} {/tex}
    {tex} {f}_0 =100 {~cm} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A spherical convex surface of radius of curvature {tex}R{/tex} separates glass (refractive index 1.5) from air. Light from a point source placed in air at distance R/2 from the surface falls on it. Find the position and nature of the image formed.
question_text: A spherical convex surface of radius of curvature {tex}R{/tex} separates glass (refractive index 1.5) from air. Light from a point source placed in air at distance R/2 from the surface falls on it. Find the position and nature of the image formed.
explanation: Refraction from rarer to denser medium {tex}\frac{n_{1}}{-{u}}+\frac{n_{2}}{{v}}=\frac{n_{2}-{n}_{1}}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}{u}=-\frac{{R}}{2}, n_{1}=1, {n}_{2}=1.5{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{{R}}+\frac{1.5}{{v}}=\frac{1.5-1}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1.5}{{v}}=\frac{0.5}{{R}}-\frac{2}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1.5}{{v}}=-\frac{1.5}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}{v}=-{R}{/tex} The image is virtual in air at distance {tex}R{/tex} .
solution: Refraction from rarer to denser medium {tex}\frac{n_{1}}{-{u}}+\frac{n_{2}}{{v}}=\frac{n_{2}-{n}_{1}}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}{u}=-\frac{{R}}{2}, n_{1}=1, {n}_{2}=1.5{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{{R}}+\frac{1.5}{{v}}=\frac{1.5-1}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1.5}{{v}}=\frac{0.5}{{R}}-\frac{2}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1.5}{{v}}=-\frac{1.5}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}{v}=-{R}{/tex} The image is virtual in air at distance {tex}R{/tex} .
solution_html:
Refraction from rarer to denser medium
{tex}\frac{n_{1}}{-{u}}+\frac{n_{2}}{{v}}=\frac{n_{2}-{n}_{1}}{{R}}{/tex}
{tex}{u}=-\frac{{R}}{2}, n_{1}=1, {n}_{2}=1.5{/tex}
{tex}\frac{2}{{R}}+\frac{1.5}{{v}}=\frac{1.5-1}{{R}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1.5}{{v}}=\frac{0.5}{{R}}-\frac{2}{{R}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1.5}{{v}}=-\frac{1.5}{{R}}{/tex}
{tex}{v}=-{R}{/tex}
The image is virtual in air at distance {tex}R{/tex}.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A thin pencil of length ({tex}{f} / 4{/tex}) is placed coinciding with the principal axis of a mirror of focal length {tex}f{/tex}. The image of the pencil is real and enlarged, just touches the pencil. Calculate the magnification produced by the mirror.
  2. A ray of light is incident on a refracting face AB of a prism ABC at an angle of {tex}45^{\circ}{/tex}. The ray emerges from face AC and the angle of deviation is {tex}15^{\circ}{/tex}. The angle of prism is {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}. Show that the emergent ray is normal to the face AC from which it emerges out. Find the refraction index of the material of the prism.
question_text: A thin pencil of length ( {tex}{f} / 4{/tex} ) is placed coinciding with the principal axis of a mirror of focal length {tex}f{/tex} . The image of the pencil is real and enlarged, just touches the pencil. Calculate the magnification produced by the mirror. A ray of light is incident on a refracting face AB of a prism ABC at an angle of {tex}45^{\circ}{/tex} . The ray emerges from face AC and the angle of deviation is {tex}15^{\circ}{/tex} . The angle of prism is {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} . Show that the emergent ray is normal to the face AC from which it emerges out. Find the refraction index of the material of the prism.
explanation: As the pencil lies between {tex}f{/tex} and {tex}2 f{/tex} such that one end of the pencil coincides with 2f. Position of the other end {tex}(u)=-\left(2 f-\frac{f}{4}\right)=-\frac{7 f}{4}{/tex} {tex} \operatorname{Magnification}(m) =\frac{f}{f-u} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{-f}{-f-\left(-\frac{7 f}{4}\right)} {/tex} {tex} m =-\frac{4}{3} {/tex} For prism; {tex} i+e=A+\delta {/tex} {tex} 45^o+e=30^o+15^o {/tex} {tex} \therefore e=0^o {/tex} Hence, {tex}{r}_2=0^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Emergent ray is perpendicular to face AC.
solution: As the pencil lies between {tex}f{/tex} and {tex}2 f{/tex} such that one end of the pencil coincides with 2f. Position of the other end {tex}(u)=-\left(2 f-\frac{f}{4}\right)=-\frac{7 f}{4}{/tex} {tex} \operatorname{Magnification}(m) =\frac{f}{f-u} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{-f}{-f-\left(-\frac{7 f}{4}\right)} {/tex} {tex} m =-\frac{4}{3} {/tex} For prism; {tex} i+e=A+\delta {/tex} {tex} 45^o+e=30^o+15^o {/tex} {tex} \therefore e=0^o {/tex} Hence, {tex}{r}_2=0^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Emergent ray is perpendicular to face AC.
solution_html:
1. As the pencil lies between {tex}f{/tex} and {tex}2 f{/tex} such that one end of the pencil coincides with 2f.
    Position of the other end {tex}(u)=-\left(2 f-\frac{f}{4}\right)=-\frac{7 f}{4}{/tex}
    {tex} \operatorname{Magnification}(m) =\frac{f}{f-u} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{-f}{-f-\left(-\frac{7 f}{4}\right)} {/tex}
    {tex} m =-\frac{4}{3} {/tex}
  2. For prism;
    {tex} i+e=A+\delta {/tex}
    {tex} 45^o+e=30^o+15^o {/tex}
    {tex} \therefore e=0^o {/tex}
    Hence, {tex}{r}_2=0^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Emergent ray is perpendicular to face AC.

subject: Physics
question_html: A transparent solid cylindrical rod (refractive index {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}) is kept in air. A ray of light incident on its face travels along the surface of the rod, as shown in figure. Calculate the angle {tex}\theta{/tex}.
question_text: A transparent solid cylindrical rod (refractive index {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} ) is kept in air. A ray of light incident on its face travels along the surface of the rod, as shown in figure. Calculate the angle {tex}\theta{/tex} .
explanation: For critical Angle {tex}\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}=\frac{1}{\sin \theta_{c}}{/tex} {tex}{n}_{1}=1 {/tex} , {tex}{n}_{2}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \text { (given) }{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{1}{\sin \theta_{c}}{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta_{c}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\theta_{c}=60^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}r=90-\theta_{c}{/tex} {tex}=30^{\circ}{/tex} From Snell's law at air rod interface {tex}n_{1} \sin i=n_{2} \sin r{/tex} {tex} n_2=\frac{\sin \theta}{\sin r} {/tex} {tex} \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{\sin \theta}{\sin 30^{\circ}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \times \frac{1}{2}=\sin \theta {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\sin \theta {/tex} {tex} \theta=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) {/tex}
solution: For critical Angle {tex}\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}=\frac{1}{\sin \theta_{c}}{/tex} {tex}{n}_{1}=1 {/tex} , {tex}{n}_{2}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \text { (given) }{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{1}{\sin \theta_{c}}{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta_{c}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\theta_{c}=60^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}r=90-\theta_{c}{/tex} {tex}=30^{\circ}{/tex} From Snell's law at air rod interface {tex}n_{1} \sin i=n_{2} \sin r{/tex} {tex} n_2=\frac{\sin \theta}{\sin r} {/tex} {tex} \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{\sin \theta}{\sin 30^{\circ}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \times \frac{1}{2}=\sin \theta {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\sin \theta {/tex} {tex} \theta=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) {/tex}
solution_html:
For critical Angle
{tex}\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}=\frac{1}{\sin \theta_{c}}{/tex}
{tex}{n}_{1}=1 {/tex}, {tex}{n}_{2}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \text { (given) }{/tex}
{tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{1}{\sin \theta_{c}}{/tex}
{tex}\sin \theta_{c}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\theta_{c}=60^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}r=90-\theta_{c}{/tex}
{tex}=30^{\circ}{/tex}
From Snell's law at air rod interface
{tex}n_{1} \sin i=n_{2} \sin r{/tex}
{tex} n_2=\frac{\sin \theta}{\sin r} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{\sin \theta}{\sin 30^{\circ}} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \times \frac{1}{2}=\sin \theta {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\sin \theta {/tex}
{tex} \theta=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
A thin lens is a transparent optical medium bounded by two surfaces, at least one of which should be spherical. Applying the formula for image formation by a single spherical surface successively at the two surfaces of a lens, one can obtain the 'lens maker formula' and then the 'lens formula'. A lens has two foci - called 'first focal point' and 'second focal point' of the lens, one on each side.


  1. Consider the arrangement shown in figure. A black vertical arrow and a horizontal thick line with a ball are painted on a glass plate. It serves as the object. When the plate is illuminated, its real image is formed on the screen.
    Which of the following correctly represents the image formed on the screen?
  2. Which of the following statements is incorrect?
    1. For a convex mirror magnification is always negative.
    2. For all virtual images formed by a mirror magnification is positive.
    3. For a concave lens magnification is always positive.
    4. For real and inverted images, magnification is always negative.
    1. A convex lens of focal length '{tex}f{/tex}' is cut into two equal parts perpendicular to the principal axis. The focal length of each part will be:
      1. f
      2. 2 f
      3. {tex}\frac{{f}}{2}{/tex}
      4. {tex}\frac{{f}}{4}{/tex}

        OR

    2. If an object in case (i) above is 20 cm from the lens and the screen is 50 cm away from the object, the focal length of the lens used is
      1. 10 cm
      2. 12 cm
      3. 16 cm
      4. 20 cm
  4. The distance of an object from first focal point of a biconvex lens is {tex}{X}_1{/tex} and distance of the image from second focal point is {tex}{X}_2{/tex}. The focal length of the lens is
    1. {tex}{X}_1 {X}_2{/tex}
    2. {tex}\sqrt{{X}_1+{X}_2}{/tex}
    3. {tex}\sqrt{{X}_1 {X}_2}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\sqrt{\frac{{X}_2}{{X}_1}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A ray of light is incident on one face of a triangular glass prism of refractive index n and is refracted out from the opposite face. Deduce the expression for refractive index of glass prism in terms of the angle of minimum deviation and angle of the prism. Write the condition on {tex}n{/tex} for refraction to take place on the opposite face.
question_text: A ray of light is incident on one face of a triangular glass prism of refractive index n and is refracted out from the opposite face. Deduce the expression for refractive index of glass prism in terms of the angle of minimum deviation and angle of the prism. Write the condition on {tex}n{/tex} for refraction to take place on the opposite face.
explanation: {tex} {A}+\delta={i}+{e} {/tex} {tex} \text { and } {A}={r}_1+{r}_2 {/tex} at angle of minimum deviation, {tex}\delta={D}_{{m}}{/tex} {tex} i=e, r_1=r_2=r {/tex} {tex} A+D_m=2 i {/tex} {tex} i=\frac{A+D_m}{2} {/tex} {tex} A=2 r \Rightarrow r=\frac{A}{2} {/tex} From Snell's law {tex} n =\frac{\sin i}{\sin r} {/tex} {tex} n =\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+D_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)} {/tex} For refraction to take place on opposite face {tex} r_2<i_c {/tex} {tex} n=\frac{1}{\sin i_c} {/tex} {tex} i_c=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{n}\right) {/tex} {tex} r_2<\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{n}\right) {/tex}
solution: {tex} {A}+\delta={i}+{e} {/tex} {tex} \text { and } {A}={r}_1+{r}_2 {/tex} at angle of minimum deviation, {tex}\delta={D}_{{m}}{/tex} {tex} i=e, r_1=r_2=r {/tex} {tex} A+D_m=2 i {/tex} {tex} i=\frac{A+D_m}{2} {/tex} {tex} A=2 r \Rightarrow r=\frac{A}{2} {/tex} From Snell's law {tex} n =\frac{\sin i}{\sin r} {/tex} {tex} n =\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+D_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)} {/tex} For refraction to take place on opposite face {tex} r_2<i_c {/tex} {tex} n=\frac{1}{\sin i_c} {/tex} {tex} i_c=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{n}\right) {/tex} {tex} r_2<\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{n}\right) {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} {A}+\delta={i}+{e} {/tex}
{tex} \text { and } {A}={r}_1+{r}_2 {/tex}
at angle of minimum deviation, {tex}\delta={D}_{{m}}{/tex}
{tex} i=e, r_1=r_2=r {/tex}
{tex} A+D_m=2 i {/tex}
{tex} i=\frac{A+D_m}{2} {/tex}
{tex} A=2 r \Rightarrow r=\frac{A}{2} {/tex}
From Snell's law
{tex} n =\frac{\sin i}{\sin r} {/tex}
{tex} n =\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+D_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)} {/tex}
For refraction to take place on opposite face
{tex} r_2<i_c {/tex}
{tex} n=\frac{1}{\sin i_c} {/tex}
{tex} i_c=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{n}\right) {/tex}
{tex} r_2<\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{n}\right) {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A convex lens is kept coaxially on the left side of a concave mirror at a distance of 5f from it where f is focal length of each of them. An object is kept at a distance of 2f on the left side of the convex lens. Draw the ray diagram showing the formation of the image by the combination. Find the distance of the final image from the mirror.
question_text: A convex lens is kept coaxially on the left side of a concave mirror at a distance of 5f from it where f is focal length of each of them. An object is kept at a distance of 2f on the left side of the convex lens. Draw the ray diagram showing the formation of the image by the combination. Find the distance of the final image from the mirror.
explanation: {tex} u=-3 f {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} {/tex} {tex} \frac{-1}{f}=-\frac{1}{3 f}+\frac{1}{v} {/tex} {tex} v=\frac{-3}{2} f {/tex}
solution: {tex} u=-3 f {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} {/tex} {tex} \frac{-1}{f}=-\frac{1}{3 f}+\frac{1}{v} {/tex} {tex} v=\frac{-3}{2} f {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} u=-3 f {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{-1}{f}=-\frac{1}{3 f}+\frac{1}{v} {/tex}
{tex} v=\frac{-3}{2} f {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A plano-convex lens of refractive index {tex}\mu_{1}{/tex} is placed coaxially in contact with a biconcave lens of refractive index {tex}\mu_{2}{/tex} as shown in the figure. All curved faces are of radius of curvature {tex}R{/tex} each. Obtain the expression for the focal length of the combined lens.
question_text: A plano-convex lens of refractive index {tex}\mu_{1}{/tex} is placed coaxially in contact with a biconcave lens of refractive index {tex}\mu_{2}{/tex} as shown in the figure. All curved faces are of radius of curvature {tex}R{/tex} each. Obtain the expression for the focal length of the combined lens.
explanation: {tex} \frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right) {/tex} For plano convex lens {tex} \frac{1}{f_1}=\left(\mu_1-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{-R}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{f_1}=\frac{\left(\mu_1-1\right)}{R} {/tex} For concave lens {tex} \frac{1}{f_2} =\left(\mu_2-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{-R}-\frac{1}{R}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{f_2} =-2 \frac{\left(\mu_2-1\right)}{R} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{f} =\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2} {/tex} {tex} f =\frac{R}{\mu_1-2 \mu_2+1} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right) {/tex} For plano convex lens {tex} \frac{1}{f_1}=\left(\mu_1-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{-R}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{f_1}=\frac{\left(\mu_1-1\right)}{R} {/tex} For concave lens {tex} \frac{1}{f_2} =\left(\mu_2-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{-R}-\frac{1}{R}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{f_2} =-2 \frac{\left(\mu_2-1\right)}{R} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{f} =\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2} {/tex} {tex} f =\frac{R}{\mu_1-2 \mu_2+1} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right) {/tex}
For plano convex lens
{tex} \frac{1}{f_1}=\left(\mu_1-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{-R}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{f_1}=\frac{\left(\mu_1-1\right)}{R} {/tex}
For concave lens
{tex} \frac{1}{f_2} =\left(\mu_2-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{-R}-\frac{1}{R}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{f_2} =-2 \frac{\left(\mu_2-1\right)}{R} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{f} =\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2} {/tex}
{tex} f =\frac{R}{\mu_1-2 \mu_2+1} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
When light travels from an optically denser medium to an optically rarer medium, at the interface it is partly reflected back into the same medium and partly refracted to the second medium. The angle of incidence corresponding to an angle of refraction {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex} is called the critical angle ({tex}{i}_{{c}}{/tex}) for the given pair of media. This angle is related to the refractive index of medium 1 with respect to medium 2.
Refraction of light through a prism involves refraction at two plane interfaces. A relation for the refractive index of the material of the prism can be obtained in terms of the refracting angle of the prism and the angle of minimum deviation. For a thin prism, this relation reduces to a simple equation.
Laws of refraction are also valid for refraction of light at a spherical interface. When an object is placed in front of a spherical surface separating two media, its image is formed. A relation between object and image distance, in terms of refractive indices of two media and the radius of curvature of the spherical surface can be obtained. Using this relation for two surfaces of a lens, 'lens maker formula' is obtained.

  1. A small bulb is placed at the bottom of a tank containing a transparent liquid (refractive index n) to a depth H. The radius of the circular area of the surface of liquid, through which light from the bulb can emerge out, is R. Then {tex}\left(\frac{{R}}{{H}}\right){/tex} is: (1)
    1. {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{{n}^2-1}}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\sqrt{{n}^2-1}{/tex}
    3. {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{{n}^2+1}}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\sqrt{{n}^2+1}{/tex}
    1. A parallel beam of light is incident on a face of a prism with refracting angle {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}. The angle of minimum deviation is found to be {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}. The refractive index of the material of the prism is close to: (1)
      1. {tex}1.3{/tex}
      2. {tex}1.4{/tex}
      3. 1.5
      4. 1.6

        OR

    2. The angle of minimum deviation for a ray of light incident on a thin prism, made of crown glass {tex}(n=1.52){/tex} is {tex}D_m{/tex}. If the prism was made of dense flint glass ({tex}{n}=1.62{/tex}) instead of crown glass, the angle of minimum deviation will: (1)
      1. decrease by {tex}4 \%{/tex}
      2. increase by {tex}4 \%{/tex}
      3. decrease by 19%
      4. increase by 19%
  3. An object is placed in front of a convex spherical glass surface ({tex}{n}=1.5{/tex} and radius of curvature R) at a distance of 4 R from it. As the object is moved slowly close to the surface, the image formed is: (1)
    1. always real
    2. always virtual
    3. first real and then virtual
    4. first virtual and then real
  4. A double-convex lens, made of glass of refractive index {tex}1.5{/tex}, has focal length 10 cm. The radius of curvature of its each face, is: (1)
    1. 10 cm
    2. 15 cm
    3. 20 cm
    4. 40 cm

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A concave mirror has radius of curvature 20 cm. Calculate the distance of an object from the mirror so as to form an image of magnification -2. Also find the location of the image.
  2. If the silver coating around the centre of a concave mirror is removed, will the mirror still form the image of an object? Justify your answer.
question_text: A concave mirror has radius of curvature 20 cm. Calculate the distance of an object from the mirror so as to form an image of magnification -2. Also find the location of the image. If the silver coating around the centre of a concave mirror is removed, will the mirror still form the image of an object? Justify your answer.
explanation: {tex}m=-\frac{{v}}{u}{/tex} {tex}-2=-\frac{{v}}{u}{/tex} {tex}{v}=2 u{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{{v}}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{-10}=\frac{1}{2 u}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}u=-15 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}v=-30 {~cm}{/tex} Yes, same image is formed with reduced intensity, because reflecting area get reduced and laws of reflection still hold good for remaining part of the mirror.
solution: {tex}m=-\frac{{v}}{u}{/tex} {tex}-2=-\frac{{v}}{u}{/tex} {tex}{v}=2 u{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{{v}}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{-10}=\frac{1}{2 u}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}u=-15 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}v=-30 {~cm}{/tex} Yes, same image is formed with reduced intensity, because reflecting area get reduced and laws of reflection still hold good for remaining part of the mirror.
solution_html:
1. {tex}m=-\frac{{v}}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}-2=-\frac{{v}}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}{v}=2 u{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{{v}}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{-10}=\frac{1}{2 u}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}u=-15 {~cm}{/tex}
    {tex}v=-30 {~cm}{/tex}
  2. Yes, same image is formed with reduced intensity, because reflecting area get reduced and laws of reflection still hold good for remaining part of the mirror.

question_html: A tank is filled with a liquid of refractive index {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}, up to a height of 30 cm. A tiny bulb is glowing at the bottom of the tank. Calculate the diameter of an opaque disc floating symmetrically on the liquid surface that can cut off completely the light from the bulb that comes out of the liquid surface.
question_text: A tank is filled with a liquid of refractive index {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} , up to a height of 30 cm. A tiny bulb is glowing at the bottom of the tank. Calculate the diameter of an opaque disc floating symmetrically on the liquid surface that can cut off completely the light from the bulb that comes out of the liquid surface.
explanation: {tex}\sin i_{c}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}i_{c}=45^{0}{/tex} {tex}\tan i_{c}=\frac{r}{d}{/tex} {tex}r=d \tan 45^{0}{/tex} {tex}r=30 \times 1 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}D=2 r=60 {~cm}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\sin i_{c}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}i_{c}=45^{0}{/tex} {tex}\tan i_{c}=\frac{r}{d}{/tex} {tex}r=d \tan 45^{0}{/tex} {tex}r=30 \times 1 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}D=2 r=60 {~cm}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\sin i_{c}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}i_{c}=45^{0}{/tex}
{tex}\tan i_{c}=\frac{r}{d}{/tex}
{tex}r=d \tan 45^{0}{/tex}
{tex}r=30 \times 1 {~cm}{/tex}
{tex}D=2 r=60 {~cm}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A ray of light is incident at an angle {tex}i{/tex} on a parallel sided glass slab of thickness 'd' and gets refracted into the slab at angle r. Draw a ray diagram to show its path as its emerges out of the slab. Hence, obtain an expression for the lateral shift of the ray. Under what condition will the shift be minimum?
question_text: A ray of light is incident at an angle {tex}i{/tex} on a parallel sided glass slab of thickness 'd' and gets refracted into the slab at angle r. Draw a ray diagram to show its path as its emerges out of the slab. Hence, obtain an expression for the lateral shift of the ray. Under what condition will the shift be minimum?
explanation: Ray diagram {tex}\sin ({i}-{r})=\frac{C D}{B C}{/tex} ...(1) {tex}\cos r=\frac{B E}{B C}=\frac{d}{B C}{/tex} Putting in equation (1) {tex}C D=\frac{d \sin (i-r)}{\cos r}{/tex} Shift will be minimum for minimum angle of incidence.
solution: Ray diagram {tex}\sin ({i}-{r})=\frac{C D}{B C}{/tex} ...(1) {tex}\cos r=\frac{B E}{B C}=\frac{d}{B C}{/tex} Putting in equation (1) {tex}C D=\frac{d \sin (i-r)}{\cos r}{/tex} Shift will be minimum for minimum angle of incidence.
solution_html:
Ray diagram

{tex}\sin ({i}-{r})=\frac{C D}{B C}{/tex} ...(1)
{tex}\cos r=\frac{B E}{B C}=\frac{d}{B C}{/tex}
Putting in equation (1)
{tex}C D=\frac{d \sin (i-r)}{\cos r}{/tex}
Shift will be minimum for minimum angle of incidence.

subject: Physics
question_html: A ray of light is incident on face {tex}A B{/tex} of a prism {tex}A B C{/tex} with angle of prism {tex}A{/tex} and emerges out from face AC. The prism is set in the position of minimum deviation with angle of deviation {tex}\delta{/tex}. Find (i) the angle of incidence and (ii) the angle of refraction on face AB.
question_text: A ray of light is incident on face {tex}A B{/tex} of a prism {tex}A B C{/tex} with angle of prism {tex}A{/tex} and emerges out from face AC. The prism is set in the position of minimum deviation with angle of deviation {tex}\delta{/tex} . Find (i) the angle of incidence and (ii) the angle of refraction on face AB.
explanation: In a prism we know {tex}A+\delta=i+e{/tex} and {tex}A=r_{1}+r_{2}{/tex} At minimum deviation {tex}i=e{/tex} and {tex}r_{1}=r_{2}=r{/tex} {tex}A+D_{m}=2 i{/tex} {tex}i=\frac{A+D_{m}}{2}{/tex} {tex}A=2 r{/tex} {tex}r=A / 2{/tex}
solution: In a prism we know {tex}A+\delta=i+e{/tex} and {tex}A=r_{1}+r_{2}{/tex} At minimum deviation {tex}i=e{/tex} and {tex}r_{1}=r_{2}=r{/tex} {tex}A+D_{m}=2 i{/tex} {tex}i=\frac{A+D_{m}}{2}{/tex} {tex}A=2 r{/tex} {tex}r=A / 2{/tex}
solution_html:
In a prism we know
{tex}A+\delta=i+e{/tex} and {tex}A=r_{1}+r_{2}{/tex}
At minimum deviation
{tex}i=e{/tex} and {tex}r_{1}=r_{2}=r{/tex}
{tex}A+D_{m}=2 i{/tex}
{tex}i=\frac{A+D_{m}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}A=2 r{/tex}
{tex}r=A / 2{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
A compound microscope is an optical instrument used for observing highly magnified images of tiny objects. It consists of two convex lenses. The lens near the object is called the objective and the lens near the eye is called the eyepiece. The magnifying power of a compound microscope is given by {tex}m=m_0 m_e{/tex}, where {tex}m_0{/tex} is the magnification produced by objective lens and {tex}m_e{/tex} is the magnification produced by eyepiece. The expression for {tex}{m}_{{e}}{/tex} depends on whether the final image is formed at the near point or at infinity (normal adjustment).

    1. A compound microscope consists of an objective lens of focal length {tex}f_o=2.0 {~cm}{/tex} and an eyepiece of focal length {tex}{f}_{{e}}=6.25 {~cm}{/tex} separated by a distance of 15 cm . How far should an object be placed from the objective so that the final image is formed at infinity? (1)
      1. 3.45 cm
      2. 5 cm
      3. 1.29 cm
      4. 2.59 cm

        OR

    2. A person with normal near point 25 cm, using a compound microscope with an objective of focal length 0.8 cm and eyepiece of focal length 2.5 cm can bring an object placed at 0.9 cm from the objective in sharp focus. If the image formed by the objective is at a distance of 2.27 cm from the eyepiece, the separation between the lenses is: (1)
      1. {tex} 7.47 {~cm}{/tex}
      2. 8.47 cm
      3. 9.47 cm
      4. {tex}10 .47 {~cm}{/tex}
  2. A compound microscope consists of an objective of focal length 2 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 10 cm. The eyepiece is kept 20 cm away from the objective. Find the magnifying power of the microscope if the final image is formed at infinity. (1)
    1. 7
    2. 10
    3. 14
    4. 16
  3. The intermediate image formed in a compound microscope is: (1)
    1. real, inverted and magnified
    2. real, erect and magnified
    3. virtual, erect and magnified
    4. virtual, inverted and magnified
  4. The focal lengths of the objective and the eyepiece of a compound microscope are {tex}f_o{/tex} and {tex}f_e{/tex} respectively. Which of the following statements is correct, in normal adjustment? (1)
    1. The object is at a distance less than {tex}{f}_{{o}}{/tex} from the objective.
    2. The object is at a distance more than {tex}f_0{/tex} and less than {tex}2 f_0{/tex} from the objective.
    3. The intermediate image is formed at a distance slightly more than {tex}f_e{/tex} from the eyepiece.
    4. The intermediate image is formed at {tex}2 {f}_{{e}}{/tex} from the eyepiece.

The eyepiece is kept 20 cm away from the objective. Find the magnifying power of the microscope if the final image is formed at infinity. (1) 7 10 14 16 The intermediate image formed in a compound microscope is: (1) real, inverted and magnified real, erect and magnified virtual, erect and magnified virtual, inverted and magnified The focal lengths of the objective and the eyepiece of a compound microscope are {tex}f_o{/tex} and {tex}f_e{/tex} respectively. The object is at a distance more than {tex}f_0{/tex} and less than {tex}2 f_0{/tex} from the objective.       OR

  2. (B) 10

subject: Physics
question_html: A convex lens (n = 1.52) has a focal length of 15.0 cm in air. Find its focal length when it is immersed in liquid of refractive index 1.65. What will be the nature of the lens?
question_text: A convex lens (n = 1.52) has a focal length of 15.0 cm in air. Find its focal length when it is immersed in liquid of refractive index 1.65. What will be the nature of the lens?
explanation: For convex lens in air {tex} \frac{1}{f_a}=\left(\frac{n_g}{n_a}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} For convex lens in liquid. {tex} \frac{1}{f_l} =\left(\frac{n_g}{n_t}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{f_l}{f_a} =\frac{\frac{1.52-1}{1}}{\frac{1.52-1.65}{1.65}} {/tex} {tex} =-6.6 {/tex} {tex} f_l =-6.6 f_a {/tex} {tex} =-99 {~cm} {/tex} Nature of the lens: Diverging/ behaves like a concave lens.
solution: For convex lens in air {tex} \frac{1}{f_a}=\left(\frac{n_g}{n_a}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} For convex lens in liquid. {tex} \frac{1}{f_l} =\left(\frac{n_g}{n_t}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{f_l}{f_a} =\frac{\frac{1.52-1}{1}}{\frac{1.52-1.65}{1.65}} {/tex} {tex} =-6.6 {/tex} {tex} f_l =-6.6 f_a {/tex} {tex} =-99 {~cm} {/tex} Nature of the lens: Diverging/ behaves like a concave lens.
solution_html:
For convex lens in air
{tex} \frac{1}{f_a}=\left(\frac{n_g}{n_a}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
For convex lens in liquid.
{tex} \frac{1}{f_l} =\left(\frac{n_g}{n_t}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \frac{f_l}{f_a} =\frac{\frac{1.52-1}{1}}{\frac{1.52-1.65}{1.65}} {/tex}
{tex} =-6.6 {/tex}
{tex} f_l =-6.6 f_a {/tex}
{tex} =-99 {~cm} {/tex}
Nature of the lens: Diverging/ behaves like a concave lens.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a labelled ray diagram of a compound microscope showing image formation at least distance of distinct vision. Derive an expression for its magnifying power.
  2. A telescope consists of two lenses of focal length 100 cm and 5 cm. Find the magnifying power when the final image is formed at infinity.
question_text: Draw a labelled ray diagram of a compound microscope showing image formation at least distance of distinct vision. Derive an expression for its magnifying power. A telescope consists of two lenses of focal length 100 cm and 5 cm. Find the magnifying power when the final image is formed at infinity.
explanation: The magnification obtained by eye-piece lens {tex}m_e=\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex} Hence the total magnifying power is {tex} m=m_0 \times m_e {/tex} {tex} =\frac{v_0}{-u_0}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right) {/tex} {tex}{m}=\left|\frac{f_0}{f_e}\right|{/tex} Identification of focal length of objective and eyepiece {tex} f_0=100 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} f_e=5 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} m=\left|\frac{100}{5}\right|=20 {/tex}
solution: The magnification obtained by eye-piece lens {tex}m_e=\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex} Hence the total magnifying power is {tex} m=m_0 \times m_e {/tex} {tex} =\frac{v_0}{-u_0}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right) {/tex} {tex}{m}=\left|\frac{f_0}{f_e}\right|{/tex} Identification of focal length of objective and eyepiece {tex} f_0=100 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} f_e=5 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} m=\left|\frac{100}{5}\right|=20 {/tex}
solution_html:
1.  
    The magnification obtained by eye-piece lens {tex}m_e=\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex}
    Hence the total magnifying power is
    {tex} m=m_0 \times m_e {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{v_0}{-u_0}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right) {/tex}
  2. {tex}{m}=\left|\frac{f_0}{f_e}\right|{/tex}
    Identification of focal length of objective and eyepiece
    {tex} f_0=100 {~cm} {/tex}
    {tex} f_e=5 {~cm} {/tex}
    {tex} m=\left|\frac{100}{5}\right|=20 {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Explain the following:

  1. For a simple microscope, the angular size of the object equals the angular size of the image. Yet it offers magnification.
  2. Both plane and convex mirrors produce virtual images of objects. Can they produce real images under some circumstances?
question_text: Explain the following: For a simple microscope, the angular size of the object equals the angular size of the image. Yet it offers magnification. Both plane and convex mirrors produce virtual images of objects. Can they produce real images under some circumstances?
explanation: Yes, it offers magnification. We can keep the small object much closer to the eye than 25 cm and hence have it subtend a large angle. Yes, Rays converging to a point behind a plane or convex mirror are reflected to a point in front of the mirror on a screen.
solution: Yes, it offers magnification. We can keep the small object much closer to the eye than 25 cm and hence have it subtend a large angle. Yes, Rays converging to a point behind a plane or convex mirror are reflected to a point in front of the mirror on a screen.
solution_html:
1. Yes, it offers magnification.
    We can keep the small object much closer to the eye than 25 cm and hence have it subtend a large angle.
  2. Yes, Rays converging to a point behind a plane or convex mirror are reflected to a point in front of the mirror on a screen.

subject: Physics
question_html: The magnifying power of an astronomical telescope is 24. In normal adjustment, distance between its two lenses is 150 cm. Find the focal length of the objective lens.
question_text: The magnifying power of an astronomical telescope is 24. In normal adjustment, distance between its two lenses is 150 cm. Find the focal length of the objective lens.
explanation: Magnifying power = 24, Distance between lenses = 150 cm {tex} \frac{f_o}{f_e}=24 {/tex} {tex} f_o+f_e=150 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} f_e=6 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} f_o=144 {~cm} {/tex}
solution: Magnifying power = 24, Distance between lenses = 150 cm {tex} \frac{f_o}{f_e}=24 {/tex} {tex} f_o+f_e=150 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} f_e=6 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} f_o=144 {~cm} {/tex}
solution_html:
Magnifying power = 24, Distance between lenses = 150 cm
{tex} \frac{f_o}{f_e}=24 {/tex}
{tex} f_o+f_e=150 {~cm} {/tex}
{tex} f_e=6 {~cm} {/tex}
{tex} f_o=144 {~cm} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Trace the path of a ray of light showing refraction through a triangular prism and hence obtain an expression for angle of deviation ({tex}\delta{/tex}) in terms of A , i and e , where symbols have their usual meanings. Draw a graph showing the variation of angle of deviation with the angle of incidence.
  2. In the figure, a ray of light is incident on a transparent liquid contained in a thin glass box at an angle of {tex}45^{\circ}{/tex} with its one face. The emergent ray passes along the face {tex}A B{/tex}. Find the refractive index of the liquid.
question_text: Trace the path of a ray of light showing refraction through a triangular prism and hence obtain an expression for angle of deviation ( {tex}\delta{/tex} ) in terms of A , i and e , where symbols have their usual meanings. Draw a graph showing the variation of angle of deviation with the angle of incidence. In the figure, a ray of light is incident on a transparent liquid contained in a thin glass box at an angle of {tex}45^{\circ}{/tex} with its one face. The emergent ray passes along the face {tex}A B{/tex} . Find the refractive index of the liquid.
explanation: For quadrilateral {tex}A Q N R{/tex} , {tex} \angle {A}+\angle {QNR}=180^{\circ}{/tex} ...(i) For triangle QNR {tex} {r}_1+{r}_2+\angle {QNR}=180^{\circ}{/tex} ...(ii) comparing equation (i) and (ii) {tex} {r}_1+{r}_2={A}{/tex} ...(iii) The angle of deviation {tex} \delta=\left({i}-{r}_1\right)+\left({e}-{r}_2\right){/tex} ...(iv) from equation (iii) and (iv) {tex} \delta=i+e-A{/tex} Graph {tex} \frac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{\sin \theta}=\mu {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\mu \sin \theta {/tex} For second surface, {tex} \frac{\sin \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)}{\sin 90^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{\cos \theta}{\sin \theta} =1 {/tex} {tex} \tan \theta =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex} From the triangle GEF {tex} \sin \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} {/tex} {tex} \mu=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} {/tex}
solution: For quadrilateral {tex}A Q N R{/tex} , {tex} \angle {A}+\angle {QNR}=180^{\circ}{/tex} ...(i) For triangle QNR {tex} {r}_1+{r}_2+\angle {QNR}=180^{\circ}{/tex} ...(ii) comparing equation (i) and (ii) {tex} {r}_1+{r}_2={A}{/tex} ...(iii) The angle of deviation {tex} \delta=\left({i}-{r}_1\right)+\left({e}-{r}_2\right){/tex} ...(iv) from equation (iii) and (iv) {tex} \delta=i+e-A{/tex} Graph {tex} \frac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{\sin \theta}=\mu {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\mu \sin \theta {/tex} For second surface, {tex} \frac{\sin \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)}{\sin 90^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{\cos \theta}{\sin \theta} =1 {/tex} {tex} \tan \theta =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex} From the triangle GEF {tex} \sin \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} {/tex} {tex} \mu=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. For quadrilateral {tex}A Q N R{/tex},
    {tex} \angle {A}+\angle {QNR}=180^{\circ}{/tex} ...(i)
    For triangle QNR
    {tex} {r}_1+{r}_2+\angle {QNR}=180^{\circ}{/tex} ...(ii)
    comparing equation (i) and (ii)
    {tex} {r}_1+{r}_2={A}{/tex} ...(iii)
    The angle of deviation
    {tex} \delta=\left({i}-{r}_1\right)+\left({e}-{r}_2\right){/tex} ...(iv)
    from equation (iii) and (iv)
    {tex} \delta=i+e-A{/tex}
    Graph



  2. {tex} \frac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{\sin \theta}=\mu {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\mu \sin \theta {/tex}
    For second surface,
    {tex} \frac{\sin \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)}{\sin 90^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex}
    {tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{\cos \theta}{\sin \theta} =1 {/tex}
    {tex} \tan \theta =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex}
    From the triangle GEF
    {tex} \sin \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} {/tex}
    {tex} \mu=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} {/tex}

question_html:
A lens is a transparent medium bounded by two surfaces, with one or both surfaces being spherical. The focal length of a lens is determined by the radii of curvature of its two surfaces and the refractive index of its medium with respect to that of the surrounding medium. The power of a lens is reciprocal of its focal length. If a number of lenses are kept in contact, the power of the combination is the algebraic sum of the powers of the individual lenses.


A double-convex lens, with each face having same radius of curvature R, is made of glass of refractive index n. Its power is:
question_text: A lens is a transparent medium bounded by two surfaces, with one or both surfaces being spherical. The focal length of a lens is determined by the radii of curvature of its two surfaces and the refractive index of its medium with respect to that of the surrounding medium. The power of a lens is reciprocal of its focal length. If a number of lenses are kept in contact, the power of the combination is the algebraic sum of the powers of the individual lenses. A double-convex lens, with each face having same radius of curvature R, is made of glass of refractive index n. Its power is:

options.explanation: {tex}\frac{2(n-1)}{R}{/tex}

explanation: {tex}\frac{2(n-1)}{R}{/tex}

question_html:
Assertion (A): Although the surfaces of a goggle lens are curved, it does not have any power.
Reason (R): In case of goggles, both the curved surfaces are curved on the same side and have equal radii of curvature.
question_text: Assertion (A): Although the surfaces of a goggle lens are curved, it does not have any power. Reason (R): In case of goggles, both the curved surfaces are curved on the same side and have equal radii of curvature.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.explanation: The focal length of a lens is given by, {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex} For, goggle, R 1 = R 2 {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex} = 0 Therefore, Power P = {tex}\frac{1}{f}{/tex} = 0.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.

correct_option_text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
explanation: The focal length of a lens is given by, {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex} For, goggle, R 1 = R 2 {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex} = 0 Therefore, Power P = {tex}\frac{1}{f}{/tex} = 0.
