subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a ray diagram for the formation of the image of an object by a convex mirror. Hence, obtain the mirror equation.
  2. Why are multi-component lenses used for both the objective and the eyepiece in optical instruments?
  3. The magnification of a small object produced by a compound microscope is 200. The focal length of the eyepiece is 2 cm and the final image is formed at infinity. Find the magnification produced by the objective.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram for the formation of the image of an object by a convex mirror. Hence, obtain the mirror equation. Why are multi-component lenses used for both the objective and the eyepiece in optical instruments? The magnification of a small object produced by a compound microscope is 200. The focal length of the eyepiece is 2 cm and the final image is formed at infinity. Find the magnification produced by the objective.
explanation: For paraxial rays MP can be considered to be a straight line perpendicular to CP, Therefore right angled triangles {tex}A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F{/tex} and MPF are similar {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{P M}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} Or {tex}\frac{{B}^{\prime} {A}^{\prime}}{{BA}}=\frac{{B}^{\prime} {F}}{{FP}} (\because {PM}={AB}){/tex} ...(1) Since {tex}\angle {APB}=\angle {A}^{\prime} {PB} {B}^{\prime}{/tex} , the right angled triangles {tex}{A}^{\prime} {PB}{/tex} and ABP are also similar Therefore, {tex}\frac{{B}^{\prime} {A}^{\prime}}{{BA}}=\frac{{B}^{\prime} {P}}{{BP}}{/tex} ...(2) Comparing eq (1) and (2), we get {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{PF}-{PB}}{{FP}}=\frac{{B}^{\prime} {P}}{{BP}}{/tex} Using sign convention {tex}P F=f, P B^{\prime}=+v , P B=-u{/tex} on solving {tex}\frac{1}{{v}}+\frac{1}{{u}}=\frac{1}{{f}}{/tex} To improve image quality by minimizing various optical aberrations in lenses. Magnification produced by compound microscope {tex} {m} ={m}_{{o}} \times {m}_{{e}} {/tex} {tex} {~m}_{{o}} =\frac{{m}}{{~m}_{{e}}}=\frac{{m}}{\left|\frac{{D}}{{fe}}\right|} {/tex} {tex} {m}_{{o}} =\frac{200}{\frac{25}{2}}=16 {/tex}
solution: For paraxial rays MP can be considered to be a straight line perpendicular to CP, Therefore right angled triangles {tex}A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F{/tex} and MPF are similar {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{P M}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} Or {tex}\frac{{B}^{\prime} {A}^{\prime}}{{BA}}=\frac{{B}^{\prime} {F}}{{FP}} (\because {PM}={AB}){/tex} ...(1) Since {tex}\angle {APB}=\angle {A}^{\prime} {PB} {B}^{\prime}{/tex} , the right angled triangles {tex}{A}^{\prime} {PB}{/tex} and ABP are also similar Therefore, {tex}\frac{{B}^{\prime} {A}^{\prime}}{{BA}}=\frac{{B}^{\prime} {P}}{{BP}}{/tex} ...(2) Comparing eq (1) and (2), we get {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{PF}-{PB}}{{FP}}=\frac{{B}^{\prime} {P}}{{BP}}{/tex} Using sign convention {tex}P F=f, P B^{\prime}=+v , P B=-u{/tex} on solving {tex}\frac{1}{{v}}+\frac{1}{{u}}=\frac{1}{{f}}{/tex} To improve image quality by minimizing various optical aberrations in lenses. Magnification produced by compound microscope {tex} {m} ={m}_{{o}} \times {m}_{{e}} {/tex} {tex} {~m}_{{o}} =\frac{{m}}{{~m}_{{e}}}=\frac{{m}}{\left|\frac{{D}}{{fe}}\right|} {/tex} {tex} {m}_{{o}} =\frac{200}{\frac{25}{2}}=16 {/tex}
solution_html:
1. For paraxial rays MP can be considered to be a straight line perpendicular to CP, Therefore right angled triangles {tex}A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F{/tex} and MPF are similar
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{P M}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex}
    Or {tex}\frac{{B}^{\prime} {A}^{\prime}}{{BA}}=\frac{{B}^{\prime} {F}}{{FP}} (\because {PM}={AB}){/tex} ...(1)
    Since {tex}\angle {APB}=\angle {A}^{\prime} {PB} {B}^{\prime}{/tex}, the right angled triangles {tex}{A}^{\prime} {PB}{/tex} and ABP are also similar
    Therefore, {tex}\frac{{B}^{\prime} {A}^{\prime}}{{BA}}=\frac{{B}^{\prime} {P}}{{BP}}{/tex} ...(2)
    Comparing eq (1) and (2), we get
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{{PF}-{PB}}{{FP}}=\frac{{B}^{\prime} {P}}{{BP}}{/tex}
    Using sign convention
    {tex}P F=f, P B^{\prime}=+v , P B=-u{/tex}
    on solving {tex}\frac{1}{{v}}+\frac{1}{{u}}=\frac{1}{{f}}{/tex}
  2. To improve image quality by minimizing various optical aberrations in lenses.
  3. Magnification produced by compound microscope
    {tex} {m} ={m}_{{o}} \times {m}_{{e}} {/tex}
    {tex} {~m}_{{o}} =\frac{{m}}{{~m}_{{e}}}=\frac{{m}}{\left|\frac{{D}}{{fe}}\right|} {/tex}
    {tex} {m}_{{o}} =\frac{200}{\frac{25}{2}}=16 {/tex}

question_html:
1. What are the two main considerations for designing the objective and eyepiece lenses of an astronomical telescope? Obtain the expression for magnifying power of the telescope when the final image is formed at infinity.
  2. A ray of light is incident at an angle of {tex}45^{\circ}{/tex} at one face of an equilateral triangular prism and passes symmetrically through the prism. Calculate:
    1. the angle of deviation produced by the prism
    2. the refractive index of the material of the prism
question_text: What are the two main considerations for designing the objective and eyepiece lenses of an astronomical telescope? Obtain the expression for magnifying power of the telescope when the final image is formed at infinity. A ray of light is incident at an angle of {tex}45^{\circ}{/tex} at one face of an equilateral triangular prism and passes symmetrically through the prism. Calculate: the angle of deviation produced by the prism the refractive index of the material of the prism
explanation: Two main considerations Objective should have Larger diameter Larger focal length Eye piece should have Smaller diameter Smaller focal length Magnifying power of telescope Magnifying power is the ratio of the angle {tex}\beta{/tex} subtended at the eye by the final images to the angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} which the object subtends at the lens or eye {tex}{m} \approx \frac{\beta}{\alpha} \approx \frac{{~h}}{{f}_{{e}}} \cdot \frac{{f}_{0}}{{~h}}=\frac{{f}_{0}}{{f}_{{e}}}{/tex} {tex}{i}+{e}={D}+{A}{/tex} at {tex}{D}={D}_{{m}} , {i}={e}{/tex} {tex}2 {i}={D}_{{m}}+{A}{/tex} {tex}2 \times 45=D_{m}+60^{0}{/tex} {tex}D_{m}=30^{0}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+D_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{60^{0}+30^{0}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{60^{0}}{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
solution: Two main considerations Objective should have Larger diameter Larger focal length Eye piece should have Smaller diameter Smaller focal length Magnifying power of telescope Magnifying power is the ratio of the angle {tex}\beta{/tex} subtended at the eye by the final images to the angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} which the object subtends at the lens or eye {tex}{m} \approx \frac{\beta}{\alpha} \approx \frac{{~h}}{{f}_{{e}}} \cdot \frac{{f}_{0}}{{~h}}=\frac{{f}_{0}}{{f}_{{e}}}{/tex} {tex}{i}+{e}={D}+{A}{/tex} at {tex}{D}={D}_{{m}} , {i}={e}{/tex} {tex}2 {i}={D}_{{m}}+{A}{/tex} {tex}2 \times 45=D_{m}+60^{0}{/tex} {tex}D_{m}=30^{0}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+D_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{60^{0}+30^{0}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{60^{0}}{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Two main considerations
    Objective should have
    1. Larger diameter
    2. Larger focal length
    Eye piece should have
    1. Smaller diameter
    2. Smaller focal length

      Magnifying power of telescope
      Magnifying power is the ratio of the angle {tex}\beta{/tex} subtended at the eye by the final images to the angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} which the object subtends at the lens or eye
      {tex}{m} \approx \frac{\beta}{\alpha} \approx \frac{{~h}}{{f}_{{e}}} \cdot \frac{{f}_{0}}{{~h}}=\frac{{f}_{0}}{{f}_{{e}}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{i}+{e}={D}+{A}{/tex}
    at {tex}{D}={D}_{{m}} , {i}={e}{/tex}
    {tex}2 {i}={D}_{{m}}+{A}{/tex}
    {tex}2 \times 45=D_{m}+60^{0}{/tex}
    {tex}D_{m}=30^{0}{/tex}
    {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+D_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{60^{0}+30^{0}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{60^{0}}{2}\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}

question_html: In normal adjustment, for a refracting telescope, the distance between objective and eye piece lens is 1.00 m. If the magnifying power of the telescope is 19, find the focal length of the objective and the eyepiece lens.
question_text: In normal adjustment, for a refracting telescope, the distance between objective and eye piece lens is 1.00 m. If the magnifying power of the telescope is 19, find the focal length of the objective and the eyepiece lens.
explanation: Distance between objective and eyepiece {tex}{fo}+{fe}=1.00 {~m}=100 {~cm}{/tex} Magnifying power {tex}|{m}|=\frac{{fo}}{{fe}}=19{/tex} {tex}\text { On solving }{/tex} {tex}{fo}=95 {~cm}=0.95 {~m}{/tex} {tex}{fe}=5 {~cm}=0.05 {~m}{/tex}
solution: Distance between objective and eyepiece {tex}{fo}+{fe}=1.00 {~m}=100 {~cm}{/tex} Magnifying power {tex}|{m}|=\frac{{fo}}{{fe}}=19{/tex} {tex}\text { On solving }{/tex} {tex}{fo}=95 {~cm}=0.95 {~m}{/tex} {tex}{fe}=5 {~cm}=0.05 {~m}{/tex}
solution_html:
Distance between objective and eyepiece
{tex}{fo}+{fe}=1.00 {~m}=100 {~cm}{/tex}
Magnifying power
{tex}|{m}|=\frac{{fo}}{{fe}}=19{/tex}
{tex}\text { On solving }{/tex}
{tex}{fo}=95 {~cm}=0.95 {~m}{/tex}
{tex}{fe}=5 {~cm}=0.05 {~m}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: An air bubble is trapped at point {tex}{B}({CB}=20 {~cm}){/tex} in a glass sphere of radius 40 cm and refractive index 1.5 as shown in figure. Find the nature and position of the image of the bubble as seen by an observer at point {tex}P{/tex}.
question_text: An air bubble is trapped at point {tex}{B}({CB}=20 {~cm}){/tex} in a glass sphere of radius 40 cm and refractive index 1.5 as shown in figure. Find the nature and position of the image of the bubble as seen by an observer at point {tex}P{/tex} .
explanation: Using refraction formula at spherical surface from denser to rarer medium {tex}n_1={/tex} refractive index of rarer medium {tex}{n}_2={/tex} refractive index of denser medium {tex}\frac{{n}_1}{{v}}-\frac{{n}_2}{{u}}=\frac{{n}_1-{n}_2}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}{u}=-20 {~cm}, {R}=-40 {~cm}, {n}_1=1, {n}_2=1.5{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}}-\frac{1.5}{(-20)}=\frac{1-1.5}{(-40)}{/tex} {tex}{v}=-16 {~cm}{/tex} Nature of image is virtual.
solution: Using refraction formula at spherical surface from denser to rarer medium {tex}n_1={/tex} refractive index of rarer medium {tex}{n}_2={/tex} refractive index of denser medium {tex}\frac{{n}_1}{{v}}-\frac{{n}_2}{{u}}=\frac{{n}_1-{n}_2}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}{u}=-20 {~cm}, {R}=-40 {~cm}, {n}_1=1, {n}_2=1.5{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}}-\frac{1.5}{(-20)}=\frac{1-1.5}{(-40)}{/tex} {tex}{v}=-16 {~cm}{/tex} Nature of image is virtual.
solution_html:
Using refraction formula at spherical surface from denser to rarer medium
{tex}n_1={/tex} refractive index of rarer medium
{tex}{n}_2={/tex} refractive index of denser medium
{tex}\frac{{n}_1}{{v}}-\frac{{n}_2}{{u}}=\frac{{n}_1-{n}_2}{{R}}{/tex}
{tex}{u}=-20 {~cm}, {R}=-40 {~cm}, {n}_1=1, {n}_2=1.5{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{{v}}-\frac{1.5}{(-20)}=\frac{1-1.5}{(-40)}{/tex}
{tex}{v}=-16 {~cm}{/tex}
Nature of image is virtual.

subject: Physics
question_html: A ray of light is incident normally on one face of an equilateral glass prism of refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex}. When the prism is completely immersed in a transparent medium, it is observed that the emergent ray just grazes the adjacent face. Find the refractive index of the medium.
question_text: A ray of light is incident normally on one face of an equilateral glass prism of refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} . When the prism is completely immersed in a transparent medium, it is observed that the emergent ray just grazes the adjacent face. Find the refractive index of the medium.
explanation: From snell's law, {tex}\mu \cdot \sin i=\mu_m \cdot \sin r{/tex} {tex}\mu \cdot \sin 60^{\circ} =\mu_m \cdot \sin 90^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\mu_m =\mu \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
solution: From snell's law, {tex}\mu \cdot \sin i=\mu_m \cdot \sin r{/tex} {tex}\mu \cdot \sin 60^{\circ} =\mu_m \cdot \sin 90^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\mu_m =\mu \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
From snell's law, {tex}\mu \cdot \sin i=\mu_m \cdot \sin r{/tex}
{tex}\mu \cdot \sin 60^{\circ} =\mu_m \cdot \sin 90^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\mu_m =\mu \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A point light source rests on the bottom of a bucket filled with a liquid of refractive index {tex}\mu=1.25{/tex} up to height of 10 cm. Calculate:

  1. the critical angle for liquid-air interface
  2. radius of circular light patch formed on the surface by light emerging from the source.
question_text: A point light source rests on the bottom of a bucket filled with a liquid of refractive index {tex}\mu=1.25{/tex} up to height of 10 cm. Calculate: the critical angle for liquid-air interface radius of circular light patch formed on the surface by light emerging from the source.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\sin {i}_{{c}}=\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex}
    {tex}\sin {i}_{{c}}=\frac{4}{5} \left(\because \mu=1.25=\frac{5}{4}\right){/tex}
    {tex}{i}_{{c}}=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{5}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\text { or } {i}_{{c}}=53^{\circ}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\sin {i}_{{c}}=\frac{{r}}{\sqrt{{r}^{2}+{h}^{2}}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{{r}^{2}}{{r}^{2}+{h}^{2}}=\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}25 {r}^{2}=16 {r}^{2}+16 {~h}^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}9 {r}^{2}=1600{/tex}
    {tex}{r}=\frac{40}{3} {~cm}{/tex}

question_html:
When a ray of light propagates from a denser medium to a rarer medium, it bends away from the normal. When the incident angle is increased, the refracted ray deviates more from the normal. For a particular angle of incidence in the denser medium, the refracted ray just grazes the interface of the two surfaces. This angle of incidence is called the critical angle for the pair of media involved.

  1. For a ray incident at the critical angle, the angle of reflection is: (1)
    1. 0o
    2. < 90o
    3. > 90o
    4. 90o
  2. A ray of light of wavelength 600 nm is incident in water {tex}\left({n}=\frac{4}{3}\right){/tex} on the water-air interface at an angle less than the critical angle. The wavelength associated with the refracted ray is: (1)
    1. 400 nm
    2. 450 nm
    3. 600 nm
    4. 800 nm
    1. The interface {tex}A B{/tex} between the two media {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex} is shown in the figure. In the denser medium A , the incident ray PQ makes an angle of {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} with the horizontal. The refracted ray is parallel to the interface. The refractive index of medium {tex}B{/tex} w.r.t. medium A is: (1)
      1. {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
      2. {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}{/tex}
      3. {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}{/tex}
      4. {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}{/tex}

OR

    1. Two media A and B are separated by a plane boundary. The speed of light in medium A and B is {tex}2 \times 10^8 {~ms}^{-1}{/tex} and {tex}2.5 \times 10^8 {~ms}^{-1}{/tex} respectively. The critical angle for a ray of light going from medium {tex}A{/tex} to medium {tex}B{/tex} is: (1)
      1. {tex}\sin ^{-1} \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
      2. {tex}\sin ^{-1} \frac{4}{5}{/tex}
      3. {tex}\sin ^{-1} \frac{3}{5}{/tex}
      4. {tex}\sin ^{-1} \frac{2}{5}{/tex}
  2. The figure shows the path of a light ray through a triangular prism. In this phenomenon, the angle {tex}\theta{/tex} is given by: (1)
    1. {tex}\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\mathrm{n}^2-1}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\mathrm{n}^2-1}{/tex}
    3. {tex}\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\mathrm{n}^2-1}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\mathrm{n}^2-1}{/tex}

When the incident angle is increased, the refracted ray deviates more from the normal. For a particular angle of incidence in the denser medium, the refracted ray just grazes the interface of the two surfaces. This angle of incidence is called the critical angle for the pair of media involved. For a ray incident at the critical angle, the angle of reflection is: (1) 0o < 90o > 90o 90o A ray of light of wavelength 600 nm is incident in water {tex}\left({n}=\frac{4}{3}\right){/tex} on the water-air interface at an angle less than the critical angle. subject: Physics
question_html: A thin converging lens of focal length 10 cm is placed coaxially in contact with a thin diverging lens of focal length 15 cm . How will the combination behave? Justify your answer.
question_text: A thin converging lens of focal length 10 cm is placed coaxially in contact with a thin diverging lens of focal length 15 cm . How will the combination behave? Justify your answer.
explanation: It will behave like a conversing lens. Power of converging lens is more than the power of diverging lens. Hence the combination will behave like a conversing lens. Alternatively {tex}{P} ={P}_{1}+{P}_{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{100}{10}+\frac{100}{-15}{/tex} {tex}{P} =\frac{10}{3} {D}{/tex} Alternatively {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}+\frac{1}{{f}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{15}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex} {tex}{f}=30 {~cm}{/tex}
solution: It will behave like a conversing lens. Power of converging lens is more than the power of diverging lens. Hence the combination will behave like a conversing lens. Alternatively {tex}{P} ={P}_{1}+{P}_{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{100}{10}+\frac{100}{-15}{/tex} {tex}{P} =\frac{10}{3} {D}{/tex} Alternatively {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}+\frac{1}{{f}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{15}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex} {tex}{f}=30 {~cm}{/tex}
solution_html:
It will behave like a conversing lens.
Power of converging lens is more than the power of diverging lens. Hence the combination will behave like a conversing lens.
Alternatively
{tex}{P} ={P}_{1}+{P}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{100}{10}+\frac{100}{-15}{/tex}
{tex}{P} =\frac{10}{3} {D}{/tex}
Alternatively
{tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}+\frac{1}{{f}_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{15}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex}
{tex}{f}=30 {~cm}{/tex}

question_html: The radius of curvature of a convex mirror is 30 cm. It forms an image of an object which is half the size of the object. Find the separation between the object and the image.
question_text: The radius of curvature of a convex mirror is 30 cm. It forms an image of an object which is half the size of the object. Find the separation between the object and the image.
explanation: {tex}{m}=-\frac{{v}}{{u}}=\frac{{h}_{{I}}}{{h}_{{o}}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}{u}=-2 {v}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{{v}}+\frac{1}{{u}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{15}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{2 v}{/tex} On solving {tex}|{v}|=7.5 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}|{u}|=+15.0 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}\text { Separation } =15.0+7.5 1 / 2{/tex} {tex}=22.5 {~cm} {/tex}
solution: {tex}{m}=-\frac{{v}}{{u}}=\frac{{h}_{{I}}}{{h}_{{o}}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}{u}=-2 {v}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{{v}}+\frac{1}{{u}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{15}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{2 v}{/tex} On solving {tex}|{v}|=7.5 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}|{u}|=+15.0 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}\text { Separation } =15.0+7.5 1 / 2{/tex} {tex}=22.5 {~cm} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{m}=-\frac{{v}}{{u}}=\frac{{h}_{{I}}}{{h}_{{o}}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}{u}=-2 {v}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{{v}}+\frac{1}{{u}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{15}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{2 v}{/tex}
On solving
{tex}|{v}|=7.5 {~cm}{/tex}
{tex}|{u}|=+15.0 {~cm}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Separation } =15.0+7.5 1 / 2{/tex}
{tex}=22.5 {~cm} {/tex}

question_html:
A prism is an optical medium bounded by three refracting plane surfaces. A ray of light suffers successive refractions on passing through its two surfaces and deviates by a certain angle from its original path. The refractive index of the material of the prism is given by {tex}\mu=\sin \left(\frac{{A}+\delta {m}}{2}\right) / \sin \frac{{A}}{2}{/tex}. If the angle of incidence on the second surface is greater than an angle called critical angle, the ray will not be refracted from the second surface and is totally internally reflected.

  1. The critical angle for glass is {tex}\theta_{1}{/tex} and that for water is {tex}\theta_{2}{/tex}. The critical angle for glass-water surface would be (given {tex}{ }_{{a}} \mu_{{g}}=1.5{/tex}, {tex}{ }_{{a}} \mu_{{w}}=1.33{/tex})
    1. less than {tex}\theta_{2}{/tex}
    2. between {tex}\theta_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\theta_{2}{/tex}
    3. greater than {tex}\theta_{2}{/tex}
    4. less than {tex}\theta_{1}{/tex}
  2. When a ray of light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} and frequency {tex}v{/tex} is refracted into a denser medium
    1. {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}v{/tex} both increase.
    2. {tex}\lambda{/tex} increases but {tex}v{/tex} is unchanged.
    3. {tex}\lambda{/tex} decreases but {tex}v{/tex} is unchanged.
    4. {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}v{/tex} both decrease.
    1. The critical angle for a ray of light passing from glass to water is minimum for
      1. red colour
      2. blue colour
      3. yellow colour
      4. violet colour

        OR

    2. Three beams of red, yellow and violet colours are passed through a prism, one by one under the same condition. When the prism is in the position of minimum deviation, the angles of refraction from the second surface are {tex}{r}_{{R}}, {r}_{{Y}}{/tex} and {tex}{r}_{{V}}{/tex} respectively.
      Then
      1. {tex}{r}_{{V}} \lt {r}_{{Y}} \lt {r}_{{R}}{/tex}
      2. {tex}{r}_{{Y}} \lt {r}_{{R}} \lt {r}_{{V}}{/tex}
      3. {tex}{r}_{{R}} \lt {r}_{{Y}} \lt {r}_{{V}}{/tex}
      4. {tex}\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{R}}=\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{Y}}=\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{V}}{/tex}
  4. A ray of light is incident normally on a prism ABC of refractive index {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}, as shown in figure. After it strikes face AC, it will
    1. go straight undeviated
    2. just graze along the face AC
    3. refract and go out of the prism
    4. undergo total internal reflection

subject: Physics
question_html:
A lens is a transparent optical medium bounded by two surfaces; at least one of which should be spherical. Considering image formation by a single spherical surface successively at the two surfaces of a lens, lens maker's formula is obtained. It is useful to design lenses of desired focal length using surfaces of suitable radii of curvature. This formula helps us obtain a relation between u, v and f for a lens. Lenses form images of objects and they are used in a number of optical devices, for example microscopes and telescopes.


An object AB is kept in front of a composite convex lens, as shown in figure. Will the lens produce one image? If not, explain.

If the screen is removed, will the image still be formed? Explain.
explanation: Yes Rays are still intersecting/converging at the location of image.
solution: Yes Rays are still intersecting/converging at the location of image.
solution_html:
Yes
Rays are still intersecting/converging at the location of image.

question_html:
A lens is a transparent optical medium bounded by two surfaces; at least one of which should be spherical. Considering image formation by a single spherical surface successively at the two surfaces of a lens, lens maker's formula is obtained. It is useful to design lenses of desired focal length using surfaces of suitable radii of curvature. This formula helps us obtain a relation between u, v and f for a lens. Lenses form images of objects and they are used in a number of optical devices, for example microscopes and telescopes.


A double convex lens is made of glass of refractive index 1.55 with both faces of the same radius of curvature. Find the radius of curvature required if focal length is 20 cm.
question_text: A lens is a transparent optical medium bounded by two surfaces; at least one of which should be spherical. Considering image formation by a single spherical surface successively at the two surfaces of a lens, lens maker's formula is obtained. It is useful to design lenses of desired focal length using surfaces of suitable radii of curvature. This formula helps us obtain a relation between u, v and f for a lens. Lenses form images of objects and they are used in a number of optical devices, for example microscopes and telescopes. A double convex lens is made of glass of refractive index 1.55 with both faces of the same radius of curvature. Find the radius of curvature required if focal length is 20 cm.
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex} R 1 = +R, R 2 = -R {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left[\frac{2}{R}\right]{/tex} R = 2(n - 1)f R = 2(1.55 - 1) {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 22 cm
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex} R 1 = +R, R 2 = -R {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left[\frac{2}{R}\right]{/tex} R = 2(n - 1)f R = 2(1.55 - 1) {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 22 cm
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex}
R1 = +R, R2 = -R
{tex}\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left[\frac{2}{R}\right]{/tex}
R = 2(n - 1)f
R = 2(1.55 - 1) {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 22 cm

question_html:
A lens is a transparent optical medium bounded by two surfaces; at least one of which should be spherical. Two convex lenses A and B of focal lengths 15 cm and 10 cm respectively are placed coaxially d distance apart. A point object is kept at a distance of 30 cm in front of lens A. Find the value of d so that the rays emerging from lens B are parallel to its principal axis.
question_text: A lens is a transparent optical medium bounded by two surfaces; at least one of which should be spherical. Considering image formation by a single spherical surface successively at the two surfaces of a lens, lens maker's formula is obtained. It is useful to design lenses of desired focal length using surfaces of suitable radii of curvature. This formula helps us obtain a relation between u, v and f for a lens. Lenses form images of objects and they are used in a number of optical devices, for example microscopes and telescopes. Two convex lenses A and B of focal lengths 15 cm and 10 cm respectively are placed coaxially d distance apart. A point object is kept at a distance of 30 cm in front of lens A. Find the value of d so that the rays emerging from lens B are parallel to its principal axis.
explanation: For lens A (f = 15 cm) u = -30 cm {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_1}=\frac{1}{15}-\frac{1}{30}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex} v 1 = 30 cm For lens B For rays to go parallel to principal axis out of lens B the image formed by lens A must lie at the focus of B So d = 30 + 10 = 40 cm
solution: For lens A (f = 15 cm) u = -30 cm {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_1}=\frac{1}{15}-\frac{1}{30}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex} v 1 = 30 cm For lens B For rays to go parallel to principal axis out of lens B the image formed by lens A must lie at the focus of B So d = 30 + 10 = 40 cm
solution_html:
For lens A (f = 15 cm)
u = -30 cm
{tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{v_1}=\frac{1}{15}-\frac{1}{30}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex}
v1 = 30 cm
For lens B
For rays to go parallel to principal axis out of lens B the image formed by lens A must lie at the focus of B
So d = 30 + 10 = 40 cm

subject: Physics
question_html:
The lens maker's formula is useful to design lenses of desired focal lengths using surfaces of suitable radii of curvature. The focal length also depends on the refractive index of the material of the lens and the surrounding medium. The refractive index depends on the wavelength of the light used. The power of a lens is related to its focal length.


How will the power of a lens be affected with an increase of wavelength of light?
question_text: The lens maker's formula is useful to design lenses of desired focal lengths using surfaces of suitable radii of curvature. The focal length also depends on the refractive index of the material of the lens and the surrounding medium. The refractive index depends on the wavelength of the light used. The power of a lens is related to its focal length. How will the power of a lens be affected with an increase of wavelength of light?
explanation: {tex}P=\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} {tex}\mu \propto \frac{1}{\lambda} \Rightarrow P \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} So P decreases as {tex}\lambda{/tex} increases.
solution: {tex}P=\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} {tex}\mu \propto \frac{1}{\lambda} \Rightarrow P \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} So P decreases as {tex}\lambda{/tex} increases.
solution_html:
{tex}P=\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\mu \propto \frac{1}{\lambda} \Rightarrow P \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex}
So P decreases as {tex}\lambda{/tex} increases.

The radius of curvature of two surfaces of a convex lens is R each. For what value of {tex}\mu{/tex} of its material will its focal length become equal to R?
question_text: The lens maker's formula is useful to design lenses of desired focal lengths using surfaces of suitable radii of curvature. The focal length also depends on the refractive index of the material of the lens and the surrounding medium. The refractive index depends on the wavelength of the light used. The power of a lens is related to its focal length. The radius of curvature of two surfaces of a convex lens is R each. For what value of {tex}\mu{/tex} of its material will its focal length become equal to R?
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{R}=(\mu-1) \frac{2}{R}{/tex} (R 1 = R, R 2 = -R) {tex}\Rightarrow \mu = \frac{3}{2}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{R}=(\mu-1) \frac{2}{R}{/tex} (R 1 = R, R 2 = -R) {tex}\Rightarrow \mu = \frac{3}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{R}=(\mu-1) \frac{2}{R}{/tex} (R1 = R, R2 = -R)
{tex}\Rightarrow \mu = \frac{3}{2}{/tex}

The focal length of a concave lens of {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 is 20 cm in air. It is completely immersed in water of {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}. Calculate its focal length in water.
question_text: The lens maker's formula is useful to design lenses of desired focal lengths using surfaces of suitable radii of curvature. The focal length also depends on the refractive index of the material of the lens and the surrounding medium. The refractive index depends on the wavelength of the light used. The power of a lens is related to its focal length. The focal length of a concave lens of {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 is 20 cm in air. It is completely immersed in water of {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} . Calculate its focal length in water.
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{20}=\left(\frac{3}{2}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} When the lens is immersed in water {tex}\frac{1}{f_{\text {unknown }}}=\left(\frac{9}{8}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{20}}{\frac{1}{f}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{1}{8}}\Rightarrow{/tex} f = 80 cm
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{20}=\left(\frac{3}{2}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} When the lens is immersed in water {tex}\frac{1}{f_{\text {unknown }}}=\left(\frac{9}{8}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{20}}{\frac{1}{f}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{1}{8}}\Rightarrow{/tex} f = 80 cm
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{20}=\left(\frac{3}{2}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
When the lens is immersed in water
{tex}\frac{1}{f_{\text {unknown }}}=\left(\frac{9}{8}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{\frac{1}{20}}{\frac{1}{f}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{1}{8}}\Rightarrow{/tex} f = 80 cm

explanation: m = 3 = {tex}\frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = 3u f = {tex}\frac{100}{+5}{/tex} = +20 cm {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3 u}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1-3}{3 u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} u = -( {tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex} ) cm v = -40 cm
solution: m = 3 = {tex}\frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = 3u f = {tex}\frac{100}{+5}{/tex} = +20 cm {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3 u}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1-3}{3 u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} u = -( {tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex} ) cm v = -40 cm
solution_html:
m = 3 = {tex}\frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = 3u
f = {tex}\frac{100}{+5}{/tex} = +20 cm
{tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{3 u}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1-3}{3 u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex}
u = -({tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex}) cm
v = -40 cm

Strontium titanate is a rare oxide - a natural mineral found in Siberia. It is used as a substitute for diamond because its refractive index and critical angle are 2.41 and 24.5o, respectively, which are approximately equal to the refractive index and critical angle of diamond. Define total internal reflection of light. Give two conditions for it.
question_text: Strontium titanate is a rare oxide - a natural mineral found in Siberia. It is used as a substitute for diamond because its refractive index and critical angle are 2.41 and 24.5 o , respectively, which are approximately equal to the refractive index and critical angle of diamond. It has all the properties of diamond. Even an expert jeweller is unable to differentiate between diamond and strontium titanate. A ray of light is incident normally on one face of an equilateral triangular prism ABC made of strontium titanate. Define total internal reflection of light. Give two conditions for it.
explanation: When light travels from optically denser medium to rarer medium at an interface and gets reflected back into the same medium the phenomenon is called as total internal reflection. Conditions for TIR Light must travel from optically denser medium to rarer medium. Angle of incidence at the interface must be greater than the critical angle for the pair of media.
solution: When light travels from optically denser medium to rarer medium at an interface and gets reflected back into the same medium the phenomenon is called as total internal reflection. Conditions for TIR Light must travel from optically denser medium to rarer medium. Angle of incidence at the interface must be greater than the critical angle for the pair of media.
solution_html:
When light travels from optically denser medium to rarer medium at an interface and gets reflected back into the same medium the phenomenon is called as total internal reflection.
Conditions for TIR

  1. Light must travel from optically denser medium to rarer medium.
  2. Angle of incidence at the interface must be greater than the critical angle for the pair of media.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define critical angle for a given pair of media and total internal reflection. Obtain the relation between the critical angle and refractive index of the medium.
question_text: Define critical angle for a given pair of media and total internal reflection. Obtain the relation between the critical angle and refractive index of the medium.
explanation: Critical angle - When a ray of light passes from a denser to a rarer medium,It is the value of angle of incidence for which the angle of refraction becomes 90 ∘ is called critical angle for that pair of media. Total internal Reflection - When a ray of light passes from a denser to rarer medium and the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle for that pair of media, the ray under goes reflection i.e return to the same medium. This is called total internal reflection. From Snell’s law {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\mu_{r d}{/tex} When angle of incidence is equal to critical angle {tex}\left(\angle i=\angle i_c\right), \angle r=90^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\sin i_c}{\sin 90^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{\mu_{d r}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mu_{d r}=\frac{1}{\sin i_c}{/tex}
solution: Critical angle - When a ray of light passes from a denser to a rarer medium,It is the value of angle of incidence for which the angle of refraction becomes 90 ∘ is called critical angle for that pair of media. Total internal Reflection - When a ray of light passes from a denser to rarer medium and the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle for that pair of media, the ray under goes reflection i.e return to the same medium. This is called total internal reflection. From Snell’s law {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\mu_{r d}{/tex} When angle of incidence is equal to critical angle {tex}\left(\angle i=\angle i_c\right), \angle r=90^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\sin i_c}{\sin 90^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{\mu_{d r}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mu_{d r}=\frac{1}{\sin i_c}{/tex}
solution_html:
Critical angle - When a ray of light passes from a denser to a rarer medium,It is the value of angle of incidence for which the angle of refraction becomes 90∘ is called critical angle for that pair of media.
Total internal Reflection - When a ray of light passes from a denser to rarer medium and the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle for that pair of media, the ray under goes reflection i.e return to the same medium. This is called total internal reflection.
From Snell’s law {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\mu_{r d}{/tex}
When angle of incidence is equal to critical angle {tex}\left(\angle i=\angle i_c\right), \angle r=90^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\sin i_c}{\sin 90^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{\mu_{d r}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mu_{d r}=\frac{1}{\sin i_c}{/tex}

question_text: Explain the working principle of an optical fibre with the help of a diagram. Mention one use of a light pipe. A ray of light is incident at an angle of 60 o on one face of a prism with the prism angle A = 60 o . The ray passes symmetrically through the prism. Find the angle of minimum deviation {tex}\left(\delta_m\right){/tex} and refractive index of the material of the prism. If the prism is immersed in water, how will {tex}\delta_m{/tex} be affected? Justify your answer.
explanation: Working Principle - Optical fibre uses the principle of total internal reflection to capture the light transmitted in an optical fibre and confine the light to the core of the fibre. Uses: i)Transmission of audio and video signal ii) Examination of internal organs iii) Endoscopy the angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = i + e - A {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 2i - A {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 60 o Refractive Index is given by {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \frac{120^{\circ}}{2}}{\sin \frac{60^{\circ}}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\sqrt{3}{/tex} If the prism is immersed in water {tex}\mu{/tex} decreases and consequently angle of minimum deviation decreases. Since {tex}\delta_m{/tex} depends on {tex}\mu{/tex} through equation given above.
solution: Working Principle - Optical fibre uses the principle of total internal reflection to capture the light transmitted in an optical fibre and confine the light to the core of the fibre. Uses: i)Transmission of audio and video signal ii) Examination of internal organs iii) Endoscopy the angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = i + e - A {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 2i - A {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 60 o Refractive Index is given by {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \frac{120^{\circ}}{2}}{\sin \frac{60^{\circ}}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\sqrt{3}{/tex} If the prism is immersed in water {tex}\mu{/tex} decreases and consequently angle of minimum deviation decreases. Since {tex}\delta_m{/tex} depends on {tex}\mu{/tex} through equation given above.
solution_html:
1. Working Principle - Optical fibre uses the principle of total internal reflection to capture the light transmitted in an optical fibre and confine the light to the core of the fibre.

    Uses: i)Transmission of audio and video signal
    ii) Examination of internal organs
    iii) Endoscopy
  2. the angle of minimum deviation 
    {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = i + e - A
    {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 2i - A
    {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 60o
    Refractive Index is given by
    {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \frac{120^{\circ}}{2}}{\sin \frac{60^{\circ}}{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\mu=\sqrt{3}{/tex}
    If the prism is immersed in water {tex}\mu{/tex} decreases and consequently angle of minimum deviation decreases. Since {tex}\delta_m{/tex} depends on {tex}\mu{/tex} through equation given above.

subject: Physics
question_html: Why is a reflecting telescope preferred over a refracting telescope? Justify your answer giving two reasons.
question_text: Why is a reflecting telescope preferred over a refracting telescope? Justify your answer giving two reasons.
explanation: Reflecting telescopes have many advantages over refracting telescopes. Mirrors don't cause chromatic aberration and they are easier and cheaper to build large. The are also easier to mount because the back of the mirror can be used to attach to the mount. No Chromatic Aberration - No refraction in mirrors No Spherical Aberration - Due to use of parabolic reflector

subject: Physics
question_html: A double-convex lens of power 4 D is manufactured from a glass of refractive index 1.5. What is the radius of curvature of each face of this lens if both faces have the same radius of curvature?
question_text: A double-convex lens of power 4 D is manufactured from a glass of refractive index 1.5. What is the radius of curvature of each face of this lens if both faces have the same radius of curvature?
explanation: P = 4D, n = 1.5 {tex}\mathrm{P}=\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} R 1 = R, R 2 = -R {tex}\mathrm{P}=\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left(\frac{2}{R}\right){/tex} 4 = (1.5 - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{2}{R}\right){/tex} 4 = {tex}\left(\frac{0.5 \times 2}{R}\right){/tex} R = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} = 0.25 m = 25 cm
solution: P = 4D, n = 1.5 {tex}\mathrm{P}=\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} R 1 = R, R 2 = -R {tex}\mathrm{P}=\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left(\frac{2}{R}\right){/tex} 4 = (1.5 - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{2}{R}\right){/tex} 4 = {tex}\left(\frac{0.5 \times 2}{R}\right){/tex} R = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} = 0.25 m = 25 cm
solution_html:
P = 4D, n = 1.5
{tex}\mathrm{P}=\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
R1 = R, R2 = -R
{tex}\mathrm{P}=\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left(\frac{2}{R}\right){/tex}
4 = (1.5 - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{2}{R}\right){/tex}
4 = {tex}\left(\frac{0.5 \times 2}{R}\right){/tex}
R = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} = 0.25 m = 25 cm

question_html: The radii of curvature of the faces of a double-convex lens are 20 cm and 30 cm. Its power is {tex}\frac{25}{6}{/tex} D. What is the refractive index of the glass of the lens?
question_text: The radii of curvature of the faces of a double-convex lens are 20 cm and 30 cm. Its power is {tex}\frac{25}{6}{/tex} D. What is the refractive index of the glass of the lens?
explanation: R 1 = 20 cm, R 2 = -30 P = {tex}\frac{25}{6}{/tex} D = {tex}\frac{25}{600}{/tex} cm -1 P - (n - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{25}{600}{/tex} = (n - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{20}+\frac{1}{30}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{25}{600}{/tex} = (n - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{50}{20 \times 30}\right){/tex} 25 - (n - 1) 50 n - 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} n = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
solution: R 1 = 20 cm, R 2 = -30 P = {tex}\frac{25}{6}{/tex} D = {tex}\frac{25}{600}{/tex} cm -1 P - (n - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{25}{600}{/tex} = (n - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{20}+\frac{1}{30}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{25}{600}{/tex} = (n - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{50}{20 \times 30}\right){/tex} 25 - (n - 1) 50 n - 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} n = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
R1 = 20 cm, R2 = -30 P = {tex}\frac{25}{6}{/tex} D = {tex}\frac{25}{600}{/tex} cm-1
P - (n - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{25}{600}{/tex} = (n - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{20}+\frac{1}{30}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{25}{600}{/tex} = (n - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{50}{20 \times 30}\right){/tex}
25 - (n - 1) 50
n - 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
n = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}

question_html: In the given figure the radius of curvature of curved face in the plano-convex and the plano-concave lens is 15 cm each. The refractive index of the material of the lenses is 1.5. Find the final position of the image formed.
question_text: In the given figure the radius of curvature of curved face in the plano-convex and the plano-concave lens is 15 cm each. The refractive index of the material of the lenses is 1.5. Find the final position of the image formed.
explanation: Using the formula {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} Focal length of plano-convex lens = +30 cm Focal length of plano-concave lens = -30 cm For plano-convex lens As object is at {tex}\infty{/tex} ,, its real image will be formed at its focus i.e + 30 cm v 1 = +30 cm For plano-concave lens u = +(30-20) cm = +10 cm {tex}\frac{1}{f_2}=\left(\frac{1}{v_2}-\frac{1}{u_2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{-30}=\left(\frac{1}{v_2}-\frac{1}{10}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} v 2 = 15 cm
solution: Using the formula {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} Focal length of plano-convex lens = +30 cm Focal length of plano-concave lens = -30 cm For plano-convex lens As object is at {tex}\infty{/tex} ,, its real image will be formed at its focus i.e + 30 cm v 1 = +30 cm For plano-concave lens u = +(30-20) cm = +10 cm {tex}\frac{1}{f_2}=\left(\frac{1}{v_2}-\frac{1}{u_2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{-30}=\left(\frac{1}{v_2}-\frac{1}{10}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} v 2 = 15 cm
solution_html:
Using the formula {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
Focal length of plano-convex lens = +30 cm
Focal length of plano-concave lens = -30 cm
For plano-convex lens
As object is at {tex}\infty{/tex},, its real image will be formed at its focus i.e + 30 cm
v1 = +30 cm
For plano-concave lens u = +(30-20) cm = +10 cm
{tex}\frac{1}{f_2}=\left(\frac{1}{v_2}-\frac{1}{u_2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{-30}=\left(\frac{1}{v_2}-\frac{1}{10}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v2 = 15 cm

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A spherical surface of radius of curvature R separates two media of refractive indices n1 and n2. A point object is placed in front of the surface at distance u in medium of refractive index n1 and its image is formed by the surface at distance v, in the medium of refractive index n2. Derive a relation between u and v.
  2. A solid glass sphere of radius 6.0 cm has a small air bubble trapped at a distance 3.0 cm from its centre C as shown in the figure. The refractive index of the material of the sphere is 1.5. Find the apparent position of this bubble when seen through the surface of the sphere from an outside point E in air.
question_text: A spherical surface of radius of curvature R separates two media of refractive indices n 1 and n 2 . A point object is placed in front of the surface at distance u in medium of refractive index n 1 and its image is formed by the surface at distance v, in the medium of refractive index n 2 . Derive a relation between u and v. A solid glass sphere of radius 6.0 cm has a small air bubble trapped at a distance 3.0 cm from its centre C as shown in the figure. The refractive index of the material of the sphere is 1.5. Find the apparent position of this bubble when seen through the surface of the sphere from an outside point E in air.
explanation: Assuming the aperture of the surface is small as compared to other distance involved, so that small angle approximation can be taken under consideration. For small angles in {tex}\triangle{/tex} NOC, i is the exterior angle. By exterior angle theorem; {tex}\therefore{/tex} i = {tex}\angle{/tex} NOM + {tex}\angle{/tex} NCM {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{M N}{O M}+\frac{M N}{M C}{/tex} ...(i) Similarly r = {tex}\angle{/tex} NCM - {tex}\angle{/tex} NIM {tex}=\frac{M N}{M C}-\frac{M N}{M I}{/tex} ...(ii) By Snell’s law n 1 sin i = n 2 sin r for small angles n 1 i = n 2 r substituting i and r from (i) and (ii) we get {tex}\frac{n_1}{O M}+\frac{n_2}{M I}=\frac{n_2-n_1}{M C}{/tex} Applying Cartesian coordinates OM = -u, MI = +v, MC = +R {tex}\frac{n_2}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{n_2-n_1}{R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{n_2}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{n_2-n_1}{R}{/tex} R= -6 cm, u = -3cm , n 1 = 1.5 n 2 = 1 {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1.5}{3}=\frac{1-1.5}{-6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{0.5}{6}-\frac{1.5}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{0.5-3}{6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{-2.5}{6}{/tex} v = -2.4 cm from the left surface inside the sphere
solution: Assuming the aperture of the surface is small as compared to other distance involved, so that small angle approximation can be taken under consideration. For small angles in {tex}\triangle{/tex} NOC, i is the exterior angle. By exterior angle theorem; {tex}\therefore{/tex} i = {tex}\angle{/tex} NOM + {tex}\angle{/tex} NCM {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{M N}{O M}+\frac{M N}{M C}{/tex} ...(i) Similarly r = {tex}\angle{/tex} NCM - {tex}\angle{/tex} NIM {tex}=\frac{M N}{M C}-\frac{M N}{M I}{/tex} ...(ii) By Snell’s law n 1 sin i = n 2 sin r for small angles n 1 i = n 2 r substituting i and r from (i) and (ii) we get {tex}\frac{n_1}{O M}+\frac{n_2}{M I}=\frac{n_2-n_1}{M C}{/tex} Applying Cartesian coordinates OM = -u, MI = +v, MC = +R {tex}\frac{n_2}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{n_2-n_1}{R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{n_2}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{n_2-n_1}{R}{/tex} R= -6 cm, u = -3cm , n 1 = 1.5 n 2 = 1 {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1.5}{3}=\frac{1-1.5}{-6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{0.5}{6}-\frac{1.5}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{0.5-3}{6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{-2.5}{6}{/tex} v = -2.4 cm from the left surface inside the sphere
solution_html:
1. Assuming the aperture of the surface is small as compared to other distance involved, so that small angle approximation can be taken under consideration. For small angles in {tex}\triangle{/tex}NOC, i is the exterior angle.
    By exterior angle theorem;
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} i = {tex}\angle{/tex}NOM + {tex}\angle{/tex}NCM
    {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{M N}{O M}+\frac{M N}{M C}{/tex} ...(i)
    Similarly r = {tex}\angle{/tex}NCM - {tex}\angle{/tex}NIM
    {tex}=\frac{M N}{M C}-\frac{M N}{M I}{/tex} ...(ii)
    By Snell’s law
    n1sin i = n2 sin r
    for small angles
    n1 i = n2 r
    substituting i and r from (i) and (ii) we get
    {tex}\frac{n_1}{O M}+\frac{n_2}{M I}=\frac{n_2-n_1}{M C}{/tex}
    Applying Cartesian coordinates
    OM = -u, MI = +v, MC = +R
    {tex}\frac{n_2}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{n_2-n_1}{R}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{n_2}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{n_2-n_1}{R}{/tex}
    R= -6 cm, u = -3cm , n1 = 1.5 n2 = 1
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1.5}{3}=\frac{1-1.5}{-6}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{0.5}{6}-\frac{1.5}{3}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{0.5-3}{6}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{-2.5}{6}{/tex}
    v = -2.4 cm
    from the left surface inside the sphere

subject: Physics
question_html: A ray of light is refracted by a glass prism. Obtain an expression for the refractive index of the glass in terms of the angle of prism A and the angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta \mathrm{m}{/tex}.
question_text: A ray of light is refracted by a glass prism. Obtain an expression for the refractive index of the glass in terms of the angle of prism A and the angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta \mathrm{m}{/tex} .
explanation: At minimum deviation, a ray of light passes through a prism in symmetrical manner. i.e., {tex}\angle i=\angle e, \angle r_1=\angle r_2=\angle r{/tex} We know that i + e = A + {tex}\delta{/tex} and r 1 + r 2 = A {tex}\therefore{/tex} i + i = A + {tex}\delta \mathrm{m}{/tex} {tex}i=\frac{A+\delta m}{2}{/tex} also r + r = A {tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac{A}{2}{/tex} Using Snell’s law {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta m}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}{/tex}
solution: At minimum deviation, a ray of light passes through a prism in symmetrical manner. i.e., {tex}\angle i=\angle e, \angle r_1=\angle r_2=\angle r{/tex} We know that i + e = A + {tex}\delta{/tex} and r 1 + r 2 = A {tex}\therefore{/tex} i + i = A + {tex}\delta \mathrm{m}{/tex} {tex}i=\frac{A+\delta m}{2}{/tex} also r + r = A {tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac{A}{2}{/tex} Using Snell’s law {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta m}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}{/tex}
solution_html:
At minimum deviation, a ray of light passes through a prism in symmetrical manner.
i.e., {tex}\angle i=\angle e, \angle r_1=\angle r_2=\angle r{/tex}
We know that i + e = A + {tex}\delta{/tex} and r1 + r2 = A
{tex}\therefore{/tex} i + i = A + {tex}\delta \mathrm{m}{/tex}
{tex}i=\frac{A+\delta m}{2}{/tex}
also
r + r = A
{tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac{A}{2}{/tex}
Using Snell’s law
{tex}\mu=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex}
{tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta m}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Draw a labelled ray diagram showing the image formation by a refracting telescope. Define its magnifying power. Write two limitations of a refracting telescope over a reflecting telescope.
  2. The focal lengths of the objective and the eye-piece of a compound microscope are 1.0 cm and 2.5 cm respectively. Find the tube length of the microscope for obtaining a magnification of 300.
question_text: Draw a labelled ray diagram showing the image formation by a refracting telescope. Define its magnifying power. Write two limitations of a refracting telescope over a reflecting telescope. The focal lengths of the objective and the eye-piece of a compound microscope are 1.0 cm and 2.5 cm respectively. Find the tube length of the microscope for obtaining a magnification of 300.
explanation: Magnifying power of a telescope – It is defined as the ratio the angle subtended at the eye by the final image to the angle subtended by the object at the lens. The two limitations of a refracting telescope over a reflecting telescope are following Less resolving power and Difficult mechanical support. f o = 1.0 cm, f e = 2.5 cm, m = 300, D = 25 cm, L = ? {tex}|m|=\frac{L}{f_0} \cdot \frac{D}{f_e}{/tex} 300 = {tex}\frac{L}{1.0} \cdot \frac{25}{2.5}{/tex} L = 30 cm hence the required tube length of the microscope = 30 cm
solution: Magnifying power of a telescope – It is defined as the ratio the angle subtended at the eye by the final image to the angle subtended by the object at the lens. The two limitations of a refracting telescope over a reflecting telescope are following Less resolving power and Difficult mechanical support. f o = 1.0 cm, f e = 2.5 cm, m = 300, D = 25 cm, L = ? {tex}|m|=\frac{L}{f_0} \cdot \frac{D}{f_e}{/tex} 300 = {tex}\frac{L}{1.0} \cdot \frac{25}{2.5}{/tex} L = 30 cm hence the required tube length of the microscope = 30 cm
solution_html:
1. Magnifying power of a telescope – It is defined as the ratio the angle subtended at the eye by the final image to the angle subtended by the object at the lens.
    The two limitations of a refracting telescope over a reflecting telescope are following
     Less resolving power and
     Difficult mechanical support.
  2. fo = 1.0 cm, fe = 2.5 cm, m = 300, D = 25 cm, L = ?
    {tex}|m|=\frac{L}{f_0} \cdot \frac{D}{f_e}{/tex}
    300 = {tex}\frac{L}{1.0} \cdot \frac{25}{2.5}{/tex}
    L = 30 cm
    hence the required tube length of the microscope = 30 cm

explanation: From Fig. the two right-angled triangles A′B′F and MPF are similar. (For paraxial rays, MP can be considered to be a straight line perpendicular to CP.) Therefore, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{PM}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{FP}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{BA}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{FP}}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} PM = AB) ...(i) Since {tex}\angle{/tex} APB = {tex}\angle{/tex} A′PB′, the right angled triangles A′B′P and ABP are also similar. Therefore, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{BA}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{BP}}{/tex} ...(ii) Comparing equations (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{FP}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}-\mathrm{FP}}{\mathrm{FP}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{BP}}{/tex} ...(iii) B'P = -v, FP = -f, BP = -u; Using these in Eq. (iii) we get {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}=\frac{2}{R}{/tex} For lens: {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} u = -5m; f = +1m {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-5}=\frac{1}{(+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} m = 1.25m hence, the position of the image formed is at 1.25m behind the lens. {tex}m=\frac{I}{O}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{u}=\frac{(+\frac{5}{4})}{(-5)}{/tex} I = (-0.25) {tex}\times{/tex} (1.8) I = -0.45 m
solution_html:
1. From Fig. the two right-angled triangles A′B′F and MPF are similar. (For paraxial rays, MP can be considered to be a straight line perpendicular to CP.) Therefore,
    {tex}\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{PM}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{FP}}{/tex}
    or {tex}\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{BA}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{FP}}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} PM = AB) ...(i)
    Since {tex}\angle{/tex}APB = {tex}\angle{/tex}A′PB′, the right angled triangles A′B′P and ABP are also similar. Therefore,
    {tex}\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{BA}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{BP}}{/tex} ...(ii)
    Comparing equations (i) and (ii)
    {tex}\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{FP}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}-\mathrm{FP}}{\mathrm{FP}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{BP}}{/tex} ...(iii)
    B'P = -v, FP = -f, BP = -u;
    Using these in Eq. (iii) we get {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}=\frac{2}{R}{/tex}
  2. For lens: {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    u = -5m; f = +1m
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-5}=\frac{1}{(+1)}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex}m = 1.25m
    hence, the position of the image formed is at 1.25m behind the lens.
    {tex}m=\frac{I}{O}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{u}=\frac{(+\frac{5}{4})}{(-5)}{/tex}
    I = (-0.25) {tex}\times{/tex} (1.8)
    I = -0.45 m

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a ray diagram showing refraction of a ray of light through a triangular glass prism. Hence, obtain the relation for the refractive index ({tex}\mu{/tex}) in terms of angle of prism (A) and angle of minimum deviation {tex}\left(\delta_{\mathrm{m}}\right){/tex}.
  2. The radii of curvature of the two surfaces of a concave lens are 20 cm each. Find the refractive index of the material of the lens if its power is -5.0 D.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram showing refraction of a ray of light through a triangular glass prism. Hence, obtain the relation for the refractive index ( {tex}\mu{/tex} ) in terms of angle of prism (A) and angle of minimum deviation {tex}\left(\delta_{\mathrm{m}}\right){/tex} . The radii of curvature of the two surfaces of a concave lens are 20 cm each. Find the refractive index of the material of the lens if its power is -5.0 D.
explanation: In the quadrilateral AQNR, two of the angles (at the vertices Q and R) are right angles. Therefore, the sum of the other angles of the quadrilateral is 180 o . {tex}\angle{/tex} A + {tex}\angle{/tex} QNR = 180 o From the triangle QNR, r 1 + r 2 + {tex}\angle{/tex} QNR = 180 o Comparing these two equations, we get r 1 + r 2 = A ...(i) The total deviation {tex}\delta{/tex} is the sum of deviations at the two faces, {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i - r 1 ) + (e - r 2 ) that is, {tex}\delta{/tex} = i + e - A ...(ii) When {tex}\delta=\delta_m;{/tex} i = e & r 1 = r 2 From (i); 2r = A or r = {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex} From (ii); {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 2i - A or i = {tex}\frac{A+\delta_{\text {mu }}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_{\text {m }}}{2}\right)}{\sin A / 2}{/tex} hence, this is the relation of angle of prism (A) and angle of minimum deviation {tex}\left(\delta_{\mathrm{m}}\right){/tex} . Given; P = -5D f (in cm) = {tex}\frac{100}{(-5)}{/tex} = -20 cm Using Lens Maker’s formula; {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{(-20)}=(\mu-1)\left[\frac{1}{(-20)}-\frac{1}{(+20)}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{(-20)}=(\mu-1)\left[-\frac{1}{10}\right] ; \mu-1=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mu=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = 1.5
solution: In the quadrilateral AQNR, two of the angles (at the vertices Q and R) are right angles. Therefore, the sum of the other angles of the quadrilateral is 180 o . {tex}\angle{/tex} A + {tex}\angle{/tex} QNR = 180 o From the triangle QNR, r 1 + r 2 + {tex}\angle{/tex} QNR = 180 o Comparing these two equations, we get r 1 + r 2 = A ...(i) The total deviation {tex}\delta{/tex} is the sum of deviations at the two faces, {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i - r 1 ) + (e - r 2 ) that is, {tex}\delta{/tex} = i + e - A ...(ii) When {tex}\delta=\delta_m;{/tex} i = e & r 1 = r 2 From (i); 2r = A or r = {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex} From (ii); {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 2i - A or i = {tex}\frac{A+\delta_{\text {mu }}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_{\text {m }}}{2}\right)}{\sin A / 2}{/tex} hence, this is the relation of angle of prism (A) and angle of minimum deviation {tex}\left(\delta_{\mathrm{m}}\right){/tex} . Given; P = -5D f (in cm) = {tex}\frac{100}{(-5)}{/tex} = -20 cm Using Lens Maker’s formula; {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{(-20)}=(\mu-1)\left[\frac{1}{(-20)}-\frac{1}{(+20)}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{(-20)}=(\mu-1)\left[-\frac{1}{10}\right] ; \mu-1=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mu=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = 1.5
solution_html:
1. In the quadrilateral AQNR, two of the angles (at the vertices Q and R) are right angles. Therefore, the sum of the other angles of the quadrilateral is 180o.
    {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}QNR = 180o
    From the triangle QNR, r1 + r2 + {tex}\angle{/tex}QNR = 180o
    Comparing these two equations, we get
    r1 + r2 = A ...(i)
    The total deviation {tex}\delta{/tex} is the sum of deviations at the two faces,
    {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i - r1) + (e - r2) that is, {tex}\delta{/tex} = i + e - A ...(ii)
    When {tex}\delta=\delta_m;{/tex} i = e & r1 = r2
    From (i); 2r = A or r = {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex}
    From (ii); {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 2i - A or i = {tex}\frac{A+\delta_{\text {mu }}}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_{\text {m }}}{2}\right)}{\sin A / 2}{/tex}
    hence, this is the relation of angle of prism (A) and angle of minimum deviation {tex}\left(\delta_{\mathrm{m}}\right){/tex}.
  2. Given; P = -5D
    f (in cm) = {tex}\frac{100}{(-5)}{/tex} = -20 cm
    Using Lens Maker’s formula; {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{(-20)}=(\mu-1)\left[\frac{1}{(-20)}-\frac{1}{(+20)}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{(-20)}=(\mu-1)\left[-\frac{1}{10}\right] ; \mu-1=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \mu=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = 1.5

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a ray diagram to show the working of a compound microscope. Obtain the expression for the total magnification for the final image to be formed at the near point.
  2. In a compound microscope an object is placed at a distance of 1.5 cm from the objective of focal length 1.25 cm. If the eye-piece has a focal length of 5 cm and the final image is formed at the near point, find the magnifying power of the microscope.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram to show the working of a compound microscope. Obtain the expression for the total magnification for the final image to be formed at the near point. In a compound microscope an object is placed at a distance of 1.5 cm from the objective of focal length 1.25 cm. If the eye-piece has a focal length of 5 cm and the final image is formed at the near point, find the magnifying power of the microscope.
explanation: Linear magnification due to the objective is tan {tex}\beta=\left(\frac{h}{f_0}\right)=\left(\frac{h^{\prime}}{L}\right){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{m}_0=\frac{\mathrm{h}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{h}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{f}_0}{/tex} Here L is the distance between the second focal point of the objective and the first focal point of the eyepiece. Linear magnification due to eyepiece is {tex}\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}=\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right){/tex} Thus, the total magnification is, m = m 0 {tex}\times{/tex} m c {tex}\mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{f}_0}\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right){/tex} Hence the expression for the total magnification for the final image to be formed at the near point is {tex}\mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{f}_0}\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right){/tex} . Given: u 0 = -1.5 cm f 0 = 1.25 cm f c = 5 cm D = 25 cm {tex}\frac{1}{f_0}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_0}=\frac{1}{1.25}-\frac{1}{1.5}=\frac{2}{15}{/tex} v 0 = {tex}\frac{15}{2}{/tex} cm {tex}\left|\mathrm{m}_0\right|=\frac{\mathrm{v}_0}{\mathrm{u}_0}=\frac{15}{2} \times \frac{1}{1.5}=5{/tex} {tex}\left|\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}\right|=\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right){/tex} {tex}\left|\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}\right|=1+\frac{25}{5}=6{/tex} m = m 0 {tex}\times{/tex} m c = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 30 hence,the magnifying power of the microscope is 30
solution: Linear magnification due to the objective is tan {tex}\beta=\left(\frac{h}{f_0}\right)=\left(\frac{h^{\prime}}{L}\right){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{m}_0=\frac{\mathrm{h}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{h}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{f}_0}{/tex} Here L is the distance between the second focal point of the objective and the first focal point of the eyepiece. Linear magnification due to eyepiece is {tex}\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}=\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right){/tex} Thus, the total magnification is, m = m 0 {tex}\times{/tex} m c {tex}\mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{f}_0}\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right){/tex} Hence the expression for the total magnification for the final image to be formed at the near point is {tex}\mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{f}_0}\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right){/tex} . Given: u 0 = -1.5 cm f 0 = 1.25 cm f c = 5 cm D = 25 cm {tex}\frac{1}{f_0}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_0}=\frac{1}{1.25}-\frac{1}{1.5}=\frac{2}{15}{/tex} v 0 = {tex}\frac{15}{2}{/tex} cm {tex}\left|\mathrm{m}_0\right|=\frac{\mathrm{v}_0}{\mathrm{u}_0}=\frac{15}{2} \times \frac{1}{1.5}=5{/tex} {tex}\left|\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}\right|=\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right){/tex} {tex}\left|\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}\right|=1+\frac{25}{5}=6{/tex} m = m 0 {tex}\times{/tex} m c = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 30 hence,the magnifying power of the microscope is 30
solution_html:
1. Linear magnification due to the objective is
    tan {tex}\beta=\left(\frac{h}{f_0}\right)=\left(\frac{h^{\prime}}{L}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{m}_0=\frac{\mathrm{h}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{h}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{f}_0}{/tex}
    Here L is the distance between the second focal point of the objective and the first focal point of the eyepiece.
    Linear magnification due to eyepiece is
    {tex}\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}=\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right){/tex}
    Thus, the total magnification is, m = m0 {tex}\times{/tex} mc
    {tex}\mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{f}_0}\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right){/tex}
    Hence the expression for the total magnification for the final image to be formed at the near point is {tex}\mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{f}_0}\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right){/tex}.
  2. Given:
    u0 = -1.5 cm
    f0 = 1.25 cm
    fc = 5 cm
    D = 25 cm
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_0}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_0}=\frac{1}{1.25}-\frac{1}{1.5}=\frac{2}{15}{/tex}
    v0 = {tex}\frac{15}{2}{/tex} cm
    {tex}\left|\mathrm{m}_0\right|=\frac{\mathrm{v}_0}{\mathrm{u}_0}=\frac{15}{2} \times \frac{1}{1.5}=5{/tex}
    {tex}\left|\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}\right|=\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\left|\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}\right|=1+\frac{25}{5}=6{/tex}
    m = m0 {tex}\times{/tex} mc = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 30
    hence,the magnifying power of the microscope is 30

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a ray diagram for the formation of image of an object by an astronomical telescope, in normal adjustment. Obtain the expression for its magnifying power.
  2. The magnifying power of an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment is 2.9 and the objective and the eyepiece are separated by a distance of 150 cm. Find the focal lengths of the two lenses.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram for the formation of image of an object by an astronomical telescope, in normal adjustment. Obtain the expression for its magnifying power. The magnifying power of an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment is 2.9 and the objective and the eyepiece are separated by a distance of 150 cm. Find the focal lengths of the two lenses.
explanation: Magnifying power (m) {tex}\mathrm{m} \approx \frac{\beta}{\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\beta=\frac{h}{f_c}{/tex} {tex}\alpha=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{f}_0}{/tex} {tex}m=\frac{f_0}{f_e}{/tex} Hence,the expression for its magnifying power is given by {tex}m=\frac{f_0}{f_e}{/tex} m = 2.9, d = 150cm (Given) {tex}\mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{o}}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{c}}}=\frac{29}{10}{/tex} f 0 + f c = 150 f c = 38.5 cm f 0 = 150 - 38.5 = 111.5 cm
solution: Magnifying power (m) {tex}\mathrm{m} \approx \frac{\beta}{\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\beta=\frac{h}{f_c}{/tex} {tex}\alpha=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{f}_0}{/tex} {tex}m=\frac{f_0}{f_e}{/tex} Hence,the expression for its magnifying power is given by {tex}m=\frac{f_0}{f_e}{/tex} m = 2.9, d = 150cm (Given) {tex}\mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{o}}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{c}}}=\frac{29}{10}{/tex} f 0 + f c = 150 f c = 38.5 cm f 0 = 150 - 38.5 = 111.5 cm
solution_html:
1. Magnifying power (m)
    {tex}\mathrm{m} \approx \frac{\beta}{\alpha}{/tex}
    {tex}\beta=\frac{h}{f_c}{/tex}
    {tex}\alpha=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{f}_0}{/tex}
    {tex}m=\frac{f_0}{f_e}{/tex}
    Hence,the expression for its magnifying power is given by {tex}m=\frac{f_0}{f_e}{/tex}
  2. m = 2.9, d = 150cm (Given)
    {tex}\mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{o}}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{c}}}=\frac{29}{10}{/tex}
    f0 + fc = 150
    fc = 38.5 cm
    f0 = 150 - 38.5 = 111.5 cm

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a ray diagram to show how the final image is formed at infinity in an astronomical refracting telescope. Obtain an expression for its magnifying power.
  2. Two thin lenses L1 and L2, L1 being a convex lens of focal length 24 cm and L2 a concave lens of focal length 18 cm are placed coaxially at a separation of 45 cm. A 1 cm tall object is placed in front of the lens L1 at a distance of 36 cm. Find the location and height of the image formed by the combination.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram to show how the final image is formed at infinity in an astronomical refracting telescope. Obtain an expression for its magnifying power. Two thin lenses L 1 and L 2 , L 1 being a convex lens of focal length 24 cm and L 2 a concave lens of focal length 18 cm are placed coaxially at a separation of 45 cm. A 1 cm tall object is placed in front of the lens L 1 at a distance of 36 cm. Find the location and height of the image formed by the combination.
explanation: From the diagram {tex}\beta=\frac{h}{f_e}{/tex} and {tex}\alpha=\frac{h}{f_o}{/tex} Magnifying Power = {tex}\frac{f_O}{f_e}{/tex} Fr lens L 1 , {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-36}=\frac{1}{24}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{24}-\frac{1}{36}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{3-2}{72}=\frac{1}{72}{/tex} v = 72 cm For lens L 2 : {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{1}{u^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{f^{\prime}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{1}{(72-45)}=\frac{1}{-18}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{-18}+\frac{1}{27}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}=\frac{-3+2}{54}=\frac{-1}{54}{/tex} v = -54 cm Final distance {tex}v_1^{\prime}{/tex} = -54 - (-45) {tex}v_1^{\prime}{/tex} = -9 cm (to the left of convex lens) Magnification {tex}\frac{h_i}{h_o}=\frac{v_1^{\prime}}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{h_i}{1}=\frac{-9}{-36} \Rightarrow{/tex} h i = + {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} cm hence,height of the image formed {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} cm
solution: From the diagram {tex}\beta=\frac{h}{f_e}{/tex} and {tex}\alpha=\frac{h}{f_o}{/tex} Magnifying Power = {tex}\frac{f_O}{f_e}{/tex} Fr lens L 1 , {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-36}=\frac{1}{24}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{24}-\frac{1}{36}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{3-2}{72}=\frac{1}{72}{/tex} v = 72 cm For lens L 2 : {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{1}{u^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{f^{\prime}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{1}{(72-45)}=\frac{1}{-18}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{-18}+\frac{1}{27}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}=\frac{-3+2}{54}=\frac{-1}{54}{/tex} v = -54 cm Final distance {tex}v_1^{\prime}{/tex} = -54 - (-45) {tex}v_1^{\prime}{/tex} = -9 cm (to the left of convex lens) Magnification {tex}\frac{h_i}{h_o}=\frac{v_1^{\prime}}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{h_i}{1}=\frac{-9}{-36} \Rightarrow{/tex} h i = + {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} cm hence,height of the image formed {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} cm
solution_html:
1. From the diagram {tex}\beta=\frac{h}{f_e}{/tex} and {tex}\alpha=\frac{h}{f_o}{/tex}
    Magnifying Power = {tex}\frac{f_O}{f_e}{/tex}

  2. Fr lens L1,
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-36}=\frac{1}{24}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{24}-\frac{1}{36}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{3-2}{72}=\frac{1}{72}{/tex}
    v = 72 cm
    For lens L2:
    {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{1}{u^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{f^{\prime}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{1}{(72-45)}=\frac{1}{-18}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{-18}+\frac{1}{27}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}=\frac{-3+2}{54}=\frac{-1}{54}{/tex}
    v = -54 cm
    Final distance {tex}v_1^{\prime}{/tex} = -54 - (-45)
    {tex}v_1^{\prime}{/tex} = -9 cm (to the left of convex lens)
    Magnification {tex}\frac{h_i}{h_o}=\frac{v_1^{\prime}}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{h_i}{1}=\frac{-9}{-36} \Rightarrow{/tex} hi = +{tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} cm
    hence,height of the image formed {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} cm

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a ray diagram for formation of a real and diminished image of an object kept in front of a concave mirror. Hence derive the mirror equation.
  2. A concave mirror of focal length 10 cm produces a real image which is 3 times the size of the object. Find the distance of the object from the mirror.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram for formation of a real and diminished image of an object kept in front of a concave mirror. Hence derive the mirror equation. A concave mirror of focal length 10 cm produces a real image which is 3 times the size of the object. Find the distance of the object from the mirror.
explanation: {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F \sim \triangle M P F{/tex} {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} PM = AB) ...(i) Also, {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} P \sim \triangle A B P{/tex} {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(ii) Comparing eq. (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P-F P}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{F P}{/tex} B'P = -v FP = -f BP = -u On solving we get, {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{v}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{v}{u}{/tex} = -3 v = 3u {tex}\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{3 u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{u}+\frac{1}{3 u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{4}{3 u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}u=\frac{4 f}{3}=-\frac{40}{3}{/tex} cm Hence, the distance of the object from the mirror {tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex} cm.
solution: {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F \sim \triangle M P F{/tex} {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} PM = AB) ...(i) Also, {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} P \sim \triangle A B P{/tex} {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(ii) Comparing eq. (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P-F P}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{F P}{/tex} B'P = -v FP = -f BP = -u On solving we get, {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{v}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{v}{u}{/tex} = -3 v = 3u {tex}\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{3 u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{u}+\frac{1}{3 u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{4}{3 u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}u=\frac{4 f}{3}=-\frac{40}{3}{/tex} cm Hence, the distance of the object from the mirror {tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex} cm.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F \sim \triangle M P F{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} PM = AB) ...(i)
    Also, {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} P \sim \triangle A B P{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(ii)
    Comparing eq. (i) and (ii)
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P-F P}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{F P}{/tex}
    B'P = -v
    FP = -f
    BP = -u
    On solving we get, {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{v}{/tex}
    {tex}-\frac{v}{u}{/tex} = -3
    v = 3u
    {tex}\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{3 u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{u}+\frac{1}{3 u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{4}{3 u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    {tex}u=\frac{4 f}{3}=-\frac{40}{3}{/tex} cm
    Hence, the distance of the object from the mirror {tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex} cm.
