question_html:
1. Draw a labelled ray diagram of an astronomical telescope to show the image formation of a distant object by it in normal adjustment. What are the main considerations required in selecting the objective and eyepiece lenses so that the telescope has large magnifying power and high resolution?
  2. A biconvex lens of focal length 20 cm is immersed in water, whose refractive index is {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}. Find the change, if any, in the nature and the focal length of the lens. Refractive index of the material of convex lens is {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: Draw a labelled ray diagram of an astronomical telescope to show the image formation of a distant object by it in normal adjustment. What are the main considerations required in selecting the objective and eyepiece lenses so that the telescope has large magnifying power and high resolution? A biconvex lens of focal length 20 cm is immersed in water, whose refractive index is {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} . Find the change, if any, in the nature and the focal length of the lens. Refractive index of the material of convex lens is {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: The main considerations required in selecting the objective and eyepiece lenses are ; For large magnifying power f o > f e For high resolution Aperture of objective should be large {tex}\frac{1}{f_a}=\left(n_g-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_a}=\left(\frac{n_g}{n_w}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{f_w}{f_a}=\frac{\left(n_g-1\right)}{\left(\frac{n_g}{n_w}-1\right)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{f_w}{20}=\frac{\left(\frac{3}{2}-1\right)}{\left(\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{3}{4}-1\right)}=\frac{\frac{1}{ 2}}{\frac{1}{8}}=4{/tex} f w = 80 cm Changes in focal length {tex}\Delta{/tex} f = 80 - 60 = 20 cm Nature of the lens is converging because f w is positive.
solution: The main considerations required in selecting the objective and eyepiece lenses are ; For large magnifying power f o > f e For high resolution Aperture of objective should be large {tex}\frac{1}{f_a}=\left(n_g-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_a}=\left(\frac{n_g}{n_w}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{f_w}{f_a}=\frac{\left(n_g-1\right)}{\left(\frac{n_g}{n_w}-1\right)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{f_w}{20}=\frac{\left(\frac{3}{2}-1\right)}{\left(\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{3}{4}-1\right)}=\frac{\frac{1}{ 2}}{\frac{1}{8}}=4{/tex} f w = 80 cm Changes in focal length {tex}\Delta{/tex} f = 80 - 60 = 20 cm Nature of the lens is converging because f w is positive.
solution_html:
1.   
    The main considerations required in selecting the objective and eyepiece lenses are ;
    For large magnifying power
    fo > fe
    For high resolution
    Aperture of objective should be large
  2. {tex}\frac{1}{f_a}=\left(n_g-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_a}=\left(\frac{n_g}{n_w}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{f_w}{f_a}=\frac{\left(n_g-1\right)}{\left(\frac{n_g}{n_w}-1\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{f_w}{20}=\frac{\left(\frac{3}{2}-1\right)}{\left(\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{3}{4}-1\right)}=\frac{\frac{1}{ 2}}{\frac{1}{8}}=4{/tex}
    fw = 80 cm
    Changes in focal length {tex}\Delta{/tex}f = 80 - 60
    = 20 cm
    Nature of the lens is converging because fw is positive.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Two converging lenses of focal lengths f1 and f2 are placed coaxially in contact. Derive the expression for the focal length of the combination using the ray diagram for the formation of the image of an object by the combination.
  2. The figure shows the variation of image distance v with object distance u for a lens.
    1. What is the nature of the lens?
    2. Using the graph, find the focal length of the lens.
question_text: Two converging lenses of focal lengths f 1 and f 2 are placed coaxially in contact. Derive the expression for the focal length of the combination using the ray diagram for the formation of the image of an object by the combination. The figure shows the variation of image distance v with object distance u for a lens. What is the nature of the lens? Using the graph, find the focal length of the lens.
explanation: Image formed by the first lens A, {tex}\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}{/tex} ...(i) Image formed by second lens B, {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_1}=\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} ...(ii) Adding eq (i) and (ii), we get, {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} ...(iii) If the two lens system is regarded as equivalent to a single lens of focal length f, we have {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} ...(iv) From equation (iii) and (iv) ,we get {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} From graph we see that as the object moves away from the lens the image comes nearer. which means it is a convex lens. u = -20 cm v = {tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex} cm {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{40}+\frac{1}{20}{/tex} f = 8 cm Hence, the focal length of the lens 8 cm.
solution: Image formed by the first lens A, {tex}\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}{/tex} ...(i) Image formed by second lens B, {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_1}=\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} ...(ii) Adding eq (i) and (ii), we get, {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} ...(iii) If the two lens system is regarded as equivalent to a single lens of focal length f, we have {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} ...(iv) From equation (iii) and (iv) ,we get {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} From graph we see that as the object moves away from the lens the image comes nearer. which means it is a convex lens. u = -20 cm v = {tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex} cm {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{40}+\frac{1}{20}{/tex} f = 8 cm Hence, the focal length of the lens 8 cm.
solution_html:
1. Image formed by the first lens A,
    {tex}\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}{/tex} ...(i)
    Image formed by second lens B,
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_1}=\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} ...(ii)
    Adding eq (i) and (ii), we get,
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} ...(iii)
    If the two lens system is regarded as equivalent to a single lens of focal length f, we have
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} ...(iv)
    From equation (iii) and (iv) ,we get
    {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex}
    1. From graph we see that as the object moves away from the lens the image comes nearer. which means it is a convex lens.
    2. u = -20 cm
      v = {tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex} cm
      {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{3}{40}+\frac{1}{20}{/tex}
      f = 8 cm
      Hence, the focal length of the lens 8 cm.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a ray diagram showing refraction of light through a prism of angle A and obtain the relation between {tex}\mu{/tex}, A and the angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex}.
  2. An equiconvex lens of radius of curvature R and made of glass of refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} is cut into two identical plano-convex lenses. Find the focal length of the plano-convex lenses.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram showing refraction of light through a prism of angle A and obtain the relation between {tex}\mu{/tex} , A and the angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} . An equiconvex lens of radius of curvature R and made of glass of refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} is cut into two identical plano-convex lenses. Find the focal length of the plano-convex lenses.
explanation: From the figure {tex}\angle{/tex} A + {tex}\angle{/tex} QNR = 180 o From {tex}\Delta{/tex} QNR r 1 + r 2 + {tex}\angle{/tex} QNR = 180 o comparing the above equations r 1 + r 2 = A ...(i) {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i - r 1 ) + (e - r 2 ) {tex}\delta{/tex} = i + e - A ...(ii) At the minimum derivation D m {tex}\delta{/tex} = D m , i = e and r 1 = r 2 From equation (i) 2r = A r = {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex} From equation (ii) D m = 2i - A {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{A+D_m}{2}{/tex} The refractive index of the prism is {tex}\mathrm{n}_{21}=\frac{\sin \mathrm{i}}{\sin \mathrm{r}}=\frac{\sin \left[\frac{\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{D}_m\right)}{2}\right]}{\sin [A / 2]}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}=(n-1)\left[\frac{2}{R}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_2}=(n-1)\left[\frac{1}{R}\right]{/tex} f 2 = 2 f 1 Hence, the focal length of the plano-convex lenses is f 2 = 2 f 1 .
solution: From the figure {tex}\angle{/tex} A + {tex}\angle{/tex} QNR = 180 o From {tex}\Delta{/tex} QNR r 1 + r 2 + {tex}\angle{/tex} QNR = 180 o comparing the above equations r 1 + r 2 = A ...(i) {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i - r 1 ) + (e - r 2 ) {tex}\delta{/tex} = i + e - A ...(ii) At the minimum derivation D m {tex}\delta{/tex} = D m , i = e and r 1 = r 2 From equation (i) 2r = A r = {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex} From equation (ii) D m = 2i - A {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{A+D_m}{2}{/tex} The refractive index of the prism is {tex}\mathrm{n}_{21}=\frac{\sin \mathrm{i}}{\sin \mathrm{r}}=\frac{\sin \left[\frac{\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{D}_m\right)}{2}\right]}{\sin [A / 2]}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}=(n-1)\left[\frac{2}{R}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_2}=(n-1)\left[\frac{1}{R}\right]{/tex} f 2 = 2 f 1 Hence, the focal length of the plano-convex lenses is f 2 = 2 f 1 .
solution_html:
1. From the figure
    {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}QNR = 180o
    From {tex}\Delta{/tex}QNR
    r1 + r2 + {tex}\angle{/tex}QNR = 180o
    comparing the above equations
    r1 + r2 = A ...(i)
    {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i - r1) + (e - r2)
    {tex}\delta{/tex} = i + e - A ...(ii)
    At the minimum derivation Dm
    {tex}\delta{/tex} = Dm, i = e and r1 = r2
    From equation (i)
    2r = A
    r = {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex}
    From equation (ii)
    Dm = 2i - A
    {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{A+D_m}{2}{/tex}
    The refractive index of the prism is
    {tex}\mathrm{n}_{21}=\frac{\sin \mathrm{i}}{\sin \mathrm{r}}=\frac{\sin \left[\frac{\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{D}_m\right)}{2}\right]}{\sin [A / 2]}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}=(n-1)\left[\frac{2}{R}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_2}=(n-1)\left[\frac{1}{R}\right]{/tex}
    f2 = 2 f1
    Hence, the focal length of the plano-convex lenses is f2 = 2 f1.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a ray diagram to show the formation of image by a concave mirror when the object is placed between f and 2f. Using this diagram, derive the mirror equation.
  2. An object is kept 30 cm in front of a convex mirror of focal length 12 cm. Find the nature and position of the image formed.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram to show the formation of image by a concave mirror when the object is placed between f and 2f. Using this diagram, derive the mirror equation. An object is kept 30 cm in front of a convex mirror of focal length 12 cm. Find the nature and position of the image formed.
explanation: From {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} C^{\prime} \sim \triangle D P F{/tex} {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} PD = AB) ...(i) From {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} P \sim \triangle A B P{/tex} {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(ii) Comparing equation (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P-F P}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} \mathrm{P}}{B P}{/tex} Here B'P = -v FP = -f BP = -u {tex}\frac{-v+f}{-f}=\frac{-v}{-u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v-f}{f}=\frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v}{f}=1+\frac{v}{u}{/tex} On solving we get {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} u = -30 cm f = +12 cm {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{30}=\frac{7}{60}{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{60}{7}{/tex} cm Since v is positive image is virtual and erect and the position of is at {tex}\frac{60}{7}{/tex} cm in front of the mirror.
solution: From {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} C^{\prime} \sim \triangle D P F{/tex} {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} PD = AB) ...(i) From {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} P \sim \triangle A B P{/tex} {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(ii) Comparing equation (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P-F P}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} \mathrm{P}}{B P}{/tex} Here B'P = -v FP = -f BP = -u {tex}\frac{-v+f}{-f}=\frac{-v}{-u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v-f}{f}=\frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v}{f}=1+\frac{v}{u}{/tex} On solving we get {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} u = -30 cm f = +12 cm {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{30}=\frac{7}{60}{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{60}{7}{/tex} cm Since v is positive image is virtual and erect and the position of is at {tex}\frac{60}{7}{/tex} cm in front of the mirror.
solution_html:
1. From {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} C^{\prime} \sim \triangle D P F{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} PD = AB) ...(i)
    From {tex}\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} P \sim \triangle A B P{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(ii)
    Comparing equation (i) and (ii)
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P-F P}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} \mathrm{P}}{B P}{/tex}
    Here B'P = -v
    FP = -f
    BP = -u
    {tex}\frac{-v+f}{-f}=\frac{-v}{-u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{v-f}{f}=\frac{v}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{v}{f}=1+\frac{v}{u}{/tex}
    On solving we get
    {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
  2. u = -30 cm
    f = +12 cm
    {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{30}=\frac{7}{60}{/tex}
    v = {tex}\frac{60}{7}{/tex} cm
    Since v is positive image is virtual and erect and the position of is at {tex}\frac{60}{7}{/tex} cm in front of the mirror.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a ray diagram showing the image formation by a compound microscope. Hence obtain the expression for total magnification when the image is formed at least distance of distinct vision.
  2. A compound microscope consists of an objective lens of focal length 2.0 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 6.0 cm. If they are separated by a distance of 24 cm, find the total magnification when the image is formed at infinity.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram showing the image formation by a compound microscope. Hence obtain the expression for total magnification when the image is formed at least distance of distinct vision. A compound microscope consists of an objective lens of focal length 2.0 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 6.0 cm. If they are separated by a distance of 24 cm, find the total magnification when the image is formed at infinity.
explanation: Magnification due to objective {tex}m_0=\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}=\frac{L}{f_0}{/tex} {tex}\left(\because \tan \beta=\frac{h}{f_0}=\frac{h}{L}\right){/tex} Magnification due to eye piece when final image is formed at the near point m e = 1 + {tex}\frac{D}{f_e}{/tex} Total magnification m = m 0 m e {tex}m=\frac{L}{f_0}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex} {tex}m=\frac{L D}{f_o f_e}{/tex} {tex}m=\frac{24 \times 25}{2 \times 6}=\frac{600}{12}{/tex} = 50 Hence, the total magnification when the image is formed at infinity is 50.
solution: Magnification due to objective {tex}m_0=\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}=\frac{L}{f_0}{/tex} {tex}\left(\because \tan \beta=\frac{h}{f_0}=\frac{h}{L}\right){/tex} Magnification due to eye piece when final image is formed at the near point m e = 1 + {tex}\frac{D}{f_e}{/tex} Total magnification m = m 0 m e {tex}m=\frac{L}{f_0}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex} {tex}m=\frac{L D}{f_o f_e}{/tex} {tex}m=\frac{24 \times 25}{2 \times 6}=\frac{600}{12}{/tex} = 50 Hence, the total magnification when the image is formed at infinity is 50.
solution_html:
1. Magnification due to objective
    {tex}m_0=\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}=\frac{L}{f_0}{/tex} {tex}\left(\because \tan \beta=\frac{h}{f_0}=\frac{h}{L}\right){/tex}
    Magnification due to eye piece when final image is formed at the near point
    me = 1 + {tex}\frac{D}{f_e}{/tex}
    Total magnification
    m = m0me
    {tex}m=\frac{L}{f_0}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex}
  2. {tex}m=\frac{L D}{f_o f_e}{/tex}
    {tex}m=\frac{24 \times 25}{2 \times 6}=\frac{600}{12}{/tex} = 50
    Hence, the total magnification when the image is formed at infinity is 50.

subject: Physics
question_html: A bright point source is placed at the bottom of a tank containing a transparent liquid (refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex}) to a height H. Let r be the radius of the circular area of the liquid surface through which light can emerge out. Find the ratio (r/H).
question_text: A bright point source is placed at the bottom of a tank containing a transparent liquid (refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} ) to a height H. Let r be the radius of the circular area of the liquid surface through which light can emerge out. Find the ratio (r/H).
explanation: Sin i c = {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} cos i c {tex}=\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 i}=\frac{\sqrt{\mu^2-1}}{\mu}{/tex} tan {tex}\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu^2-1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{r}{H}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu^2-1}}{/tex}
solution: Sin i c = {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} cos i c {tex}=\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 i}=\frac{\sqrt{\mu^2-1}}{\mu}{/tex} tan {tex}\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu^2-1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{r}{H}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu^2-1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Sin ic = {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex}
cos ic {tex}=\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 i}=\frac{\sqrt{\mu^2-1}}{\mu}{/tex}
tan {tex}\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu^2-1}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{r}{H}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu^2-1}}{/tex}

question_html:
A prism is a solid transparent medium bounded by three rectangular faces with a triangular base and a top. A ray of light incident at angle i on one face of a prism suffers two refractions on passing through a prism. Hence it deviates through a certain angle {tex}\delta{/tex} from its original path. The angle of deviation becomes minimum {tex}\left(\delta=\delta_m\right){/tex} for a certain value of angle i. In such a condition, the refracted ray inside the prism becomes parallel to its base. An expression for refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} of the material of the prism can be obtained in terms of angle A and angle {tex}\delta_m{/tex}.


Show in a figure the variation of angle {tex}\delta{/tex} with angle of incidence i.

A prism is a solid transparent medium bounded by three rectangular faces with a triangular base and a top. A ray of light incident at angle i on one face of a prism suffers two refractions on passing through a prism. Hence it deviates through a certain angle {tex}\delta{/tex} from its original path. The angle of deviation becomes minimum {tex}\left(\delta=\delta_m\right){/tex} for a certain value of angle i. In such a condition, the refracted ray inside the prism becomes parallel to its base. An expression for refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} of the material of the prism can be obtained in terms of angle A and angle {tex}\delta_m{/tex}.


Show that for a prism of small angle A, the refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} of its material can be written as {tex}\mu=1+\frac{\delta_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{A}}{/tex}.
question_text: A prism is a solid transparent medium bounded by three rectangular faces with a triangular base and a top. A ray of light incident at angle i on one face of a prism suffers two refractions on passing through a prism. Hence it deviates through a certain angle {tex}\delta{/tex} from its original path. The angle of deviation becomes minimum {tex}\left(\delta=\delta_m\right){/tex} for a certain value of angle i. In such a condition, the refracted ray inside the prism becomes parallel to its base. An expression for refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} of the material of the prism can be obtained in terms of angle A and angle {tex}\delta_m{/tex} . Show that for a prism of small angle A, the refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} of its material can be written as {tex}\mu=1+\frac{\delta_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{A}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{ 2}}{/tex} For small angle {tex}\mu=\frac{\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}}{\frac{A}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{A+\delta_m}{A}{/tex} {tex}\mu=1+\frac{\delta_m}{A}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{ 2}}{/tex} For small angle {tex}\mu=\frac{\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}}{\frac{A}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{A+\delta_m}{A}{/tex} {tex}\mu=1+\frac{\delta_m}{A}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{ 2}}{/tex}
For small angle
{tex}\mu=\frac{\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}}{\frac{A}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\mu=\frac{A+\delta_m}{A}{/tex}
{tex}\mu=1+\frac{\delta_m}{A}{/tex}

A ray of light passes through an equilateral prism such that both the angle of incidence and the angle of emergence are equal to the angle of prism A. Find the refractive index of the material of the prism, in terms of A.
question_text: A prism is a solid transparent medium bounded by three rectangular faces with a triangular base and a top. A ray of light incident at angle i on one face of a prism suffers two refractions on passing through a prism. Hence it deviates through a certain angle {tex}\delta{/tex} from its original path. The angle of deviation becomes minimum {tex}\left(\delta=\delta_m\right){/tex} for a certain value of angle i. In such a condition, the refracted ray inside the prism becomes parallel to its base. An expression for refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} of the material of the prism can be obtained in terms of angle A and angle {tex}\delta_m{/tex} . A ray of light passes through an equilateral prism such that both the angle of incidence and the angle of emergence are equal to the angle of prism A. Find the refractive index of the material of the prism, in terms of A.
explanation: i + e = A r = {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin A}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{2 \sin (\frac{A}{2}) \cos (\frac{A}{2})}{\sin (\frac{A}{2})}{/tex} = 2 cos {tex}(\frac{A}{2}){/tex}
solution: i + e = A r = {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin A}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{2 \sin (\frac{A}{2}) \cos (\frac{A}{2})}{\sin (\frac{A}{2})}{/tex} = 2 cos {tex}(\frac{A}{2}){/tex}
solution_html:
i + e = A
r = {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\mu=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex}
{tex}\mu=\frac{\sin A}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\mu=\frac{2 \sin (\frac{A}{2}) \cos (\frac{A}{2})}{\sin (\frac{A}{2})}{/tex} = 2 cos {tex}(\frac{A}{2}){/tex}

A ray of light passes through a prism of angle 75o, as shown in the figure. The refractive index of the material of the prism, with respect to its surrounding is {tex}\sqrt 2 {/tex}. Find the angle of incidence i.
question_text: A prism is a solid transparent medium bounded by three rectangular faces with a triangular base and a top. A ray of light incident at angle i on one face of a prism suffers two refractions on passing through a prism. Hence it deviates through a certain angle {tex}\delta{/tex} from its original path. The angle of deviation becomes minimum {tex}\left(\delta=\delta_m\right){/tex} for a certain value of angle i. In such a condition, the refracted ray inside the prism becomes parallel to its base. An expression for refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} of the material of the prism can be obtained in terms of angle A and angle {tex}\delta_m{/tex} . A ray of light passes through a prism of angle 75 o , as shown in the figure. The refractive index of the material of the prism, with respect to its surrounding is {tex}\sqrt 2 {/tex} . Find the angle of incidence i.
explanation: {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin r_2}{\sin 90^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} sin r 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} r 2 = 45 o r 1 + r 2 = A r 1 + 45 o = 75 o r 1 = 30 o {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r_1}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} sin i = {tex}\sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} sin i = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} i = 45 o
solution: {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin r_2}{\sin 90^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} sin r 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} r 2 = 45 o r 1 + r 2 = A r 1 + 45 o = 75 o r 1 = 30 o {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r_1}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} sin i = {tex}\sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} sin i = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} i = 45 o
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\sin r_2}{\sin 90^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
sin r2 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
r2 = 45o
r1 + r2 = A
r1 + 45o = 75o
r1 = 30o
{tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r_1}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
sin i = {tex}\sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
sin i = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
i = 45o

question_html: A small object lies at the bottom of a vessel filled with water (refractive index {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}) up to a height H. When viewed from a point above the surface of water, the object appears raised by n percent of H. The value of n is:
question_text: A small object lies at the bottom of a vessel filled with water (refractive index {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} ) up to a height H. When viewed from a point above the surface of water, the object appears raised by n percent of H. The value of n is:

options.explanation: actual depth = H U = {tex}\frac 43{/tex} Apparent depth = actual depth/U = H/4/3 = 3H/4 shift = actual depth- apparent depth = H - {tex}\frac{3H}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac H4{/tex} = h * 100/4/4 = 25%

explanation: actual depth = H U = {tex}\frac 43{/tex} Apparent depth = actual depth/U = H/4/3 = 3H/4 shift = actual depth- apparent depth = H - {tex}\frac{3H}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac H4{/tex} = h * 100/4/4 = 25%

question_html: Draw a ray diagram to show the formation of an image at the least distance of distinct vision, by a compound microscope. Hence, obtain an expression for its angular magnification.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram to show the formation of an image at the least distance of distinct vision, by a compound microscope. Hence, obtain an expression for its angular magnification.
explanation: angular magnification m = {tex}\frac {\beta} {\alpha}{/tex} In the above diagram {tex}\triangle{/tex} NA''B'' tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N A^{\prime \prime}}{A^{\prime \prime} 0^{\prime}}=\frac{A B}{A^{\prime \prime} 0^{\prime}}{/tex} also In {tex}\triangle{/tex} A''B''o' tan {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A^{\prime \prime} B^{\prime \prime}}{A^{\prime \prime} O^{\prime}}{/tex} So m = {tex}\frac{\tan \beta}{\tan \alpha}=\frac{A B}{A^{\prime \prime} B^{\prime \prime}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A B}{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}} \times \frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A^{\prime \prime} B^{\prime}}=\frac{m_0}{m_e}{/tex} m o {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} magnification of objective lens m e {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} magnification of eye lens ​​​​​So, m o = {tex}\frac{v_0}{u_0}{/tex} m e = {tex}\frac{v_e}{u_e}{/tex} for eye lens using lens formula. {tex}\frac{-1}{u_e}+\frac{1}{v_e}=\frac{1}{f_e}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v_e}{u_e}=1-\frac{v_e}{f_e}{/tex} so, m e = {tex}1-\frac{v_e}{f_e}{/tex} v e = - D f e = + f e m e = {tex}1+\frac{D}{f_e}{/tex} m = {tex}m_0 \times\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex} m = {tex}\frac{v_0}{u_0}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex}
solution: angular magnification m = {tex}\frac {\beta} {\alpha}{/tex} In the above diagram {tex}\triangle{/tex} NA''B'' tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N A^{\prime \prime}}{A^{\prime \prime} 0^{\prime}}=\frac{A B}{A^{\prime \prime} 0^{\prime}}{/tex} also In {tex}\triangle{/tex} A''B''o' tan {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A^{\prime \prime} B^{\prime \prime}}{A^{\prime \prime} O^{\prime}}{/tex} So m = {tex}\frac{\tan \beta}{\tan \alpha}=\frac{A B}{A^{\prime \prime} B^{\prime \prime}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A B}{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}} \times \frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A^{\prime \prime} B^{\prime}}=\frac{m_0}{m_e}{/tex} m o {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} magnification of objective lens m e {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} magnification of eye lens ​​​​​So, m o = {tex}\frac{v_0}{u_0}{/tex} m e = {tex}\frac{v_e}{u_e}{/tex} for eye lens using lens formula. {tex}\frac{-1}{u_e}+\frac{1}{v_e}=\frac{1}{f_e}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v_e}{u_e}=1-\frac{v_e}{f_e}{/tex} so, m e = {tex}1-\frac{v_e}{f_e}{/tex} v e = - D f e = + f e m e = {tex}1+\frac{D}{f_e}{/tex} m = {tex}m_0 \times\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex} m = {tex}\frac{v_0}{u_0}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
angular magnification m = {tex}\frac {\beta} {\alpha}{/tex}
In the above diagram {tex}\triangle{/tex} NA''B'' tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N A^{\prime \prime}}{A^{\prime \prime} 0^{\prime}}=\frac{A B}{A^{\prime \prime} 0^{\prime}}{/tex}
also In {tex}\triangle{/tex} A''B''o' tan {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A^{\prime \prime} B^{\prime \prime}}{A^{\prime \prime} O^{\prime}}{/tex}
So m = {tex}\frac{\tan \beta}{\tan \alpha}=\frac{A B}{A^{\prime \prime} B^{\prime \prime}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A B}{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}} \times \frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A^{\prime \prime} B^{\prime}}=\frac{m_0}{m_e}{/tex}
mo {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} magnification of objective lens
me {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} magnification of eye lens
​​​​​So, mo = {tex}\frac{v_0}{u_0}{/tex} me = {tex}\frac{v_e}{u_e}{/tex}
for eye lens using lens formula.  {tex}\frac{-1}{u_e}+\frac{1}{v_e}=\frac{1}{f_e}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{v_e}{u_e}=1-\frac{v_e}{f_e}{/tex}
so, me = {tex}1-\frac{v_e}{f_e}{/tex}
ve = - D
fe = + fe
me = {tex}1+\frac{D}{f_e}{/tex}
m = {tex}m_0 \times\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex}
m = {tex}\frac{v_0}{u_0}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex}

question_html: A point source in air is kept 24 cm in front of a concave spherical glass surface ({tex}_{\mathrm{a}} \mu_g{/tex} = 1.5) and radius of curvature 60 cm. Find the nature of the image formed and its distance from the point source.
question_text: A point source in air is kept 24 cm in front of a concave spherical glass surface ( {tex}_{\mathrm{a}} \mu_g{/tex} = 1.5) and radius of curvature 60 cm. Find the nature of the image formed and its distance from the point source.
explanation: {tex}\mu_1{/tex} = 1 {tex}\mu_2{/tex} = 1.5 R = 60 cm u = -24 cm Light goes from air to glass ( using refraction formula) {tex}-\frac{\mu_1}{u}+\frac{\mu_2}{v}=\frac{\mu_2-\mu_1}{R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{-1}{-24}+\frac{1.5}{v}=\frac{1.5-1}{60}=\frac{1}{120}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1.5}{v}=\frac{1}{120}-\frac{1}{24}=-\frac{1}{30}{/tex} v = -45 cm So a virtual image will be formed is left to concave glass surface at 45 cm.
solution: {tex}\mu_1{/tex} = 1 {tex}\mu_2{/tex} = 1.5 R = 60 cm u = -24 cm Light goes from air to glass ( using refraction formula) {tex}-\frac{\mu_1}{u}+\frac{\mu_2}{v}=\frac{\mu_2-\mu_1}{R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{-1}{-24}+\frac{1.5}{v}=\frac{1.5-1}{60}=\frac{1}{120}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1.5}{v}=\frac{1}{120}-\frac{1}{24}=-\frac{1}{30}{/tex} v = -45 cm So a virtual image will be formed is left to concave glass surface at 45 cm.
solution_html:
{tex}\mu_1{/tex} = 1
{tex}\mu_2{/tex} = 1.5
R = 60 cm
u = -24 cm
Light goes from air to glass ( using refraction formula)
{tex}-\frac{\mu_1}{u}+\frac{\mu_2}{v}=\frac{\mu_2-\mu_1}{R}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{-1}{-24}+\frac{1.5}{v}=\frac{1.5-1}{60}=\frac{1}{120}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1.5}{v}=\frac{1}{120}-\frac{1}{24}=-\frac{1}{30}{/tex}
v = -45 cm
So a virtual image will be formed is left to concave glass surface at 45 cm.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A thin equiconvex lens of radius of curvature R made of material of refractive index {tex}\mu_1{/tex} is kept coaxially, in contact with an equiconcave lens of the same radius of curvature and refractive index {tex}\mu_2\left(>\mu_1\right){/tex}. Find:

  1. the ratio of their powers, and
  2. the power of the combination and its nature.
question_text: A thin equiconvex lens of radius of curvature R made of material of refractive index {tex}\mu_1{/tex} is kept coaxially, in contact with an equiconcave lens of the same radius of curvature and refractive index {tex}\mu_2\left(>\mu_1\right){/tex} . Find: the ratio of their powers, and the power of the combination and its nature.
explanation: By using lens maker formula, {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} P 1 = ( {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{R}\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(\mu_1-1\right) \times \frac{2}{R}{/tex} for equiconvex also P 2 = {tex}\left(\mu_2-1\right) \times-\frac{2}{R}{/tex} for equiconcave lens {tex}\frac{P_1}{P_2}=-\frac{\left(\mu_1-1\right)}{\left(\mu_2-1\right)}{/tex} P = P 1 + P 2 = {tex}\frac{2}{R}\left[\mu_1-1-\mu_2+1\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{R}\left(\mu_1-\mu_2\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu_2>\mu_1{/tex} P is -ve
solution: By using lens maker formula, {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} P 1 = ( {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{R}\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(\mu_1-1\right) \times \frac{2}{R}{/tex} for equiconvex also P 2 = {tex}\left(\mu_2-1\right) \times-\frac{2}{R}{/tex} for equiconcave lens {tex}\frac{P_1}{P_2}=-\frac{\left(\mu_1-1\right)}{\left(\mu_2-1\right)}{/tex} P = P 1 + P 2 = {tex}\frac{2}{R}\left[\mu_1-1-\mu_2+1\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{R}\left(\mu_1-\mu_2\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu_2>\mu_1{/tex} P is -ve
solution_html:
By using lens maker formula,  {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
P1 = ({tex}\mu{/tex} - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{R}\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(\mu_1-1\right) \times \frac{2}{R}{/tex} for equiconvex
also P2 = {tex}\left(\mu_2-1\right) \times-\frac{2}{R}{/tex} for equiconcave lens

  1. {tex}\frac{P_1}{P_2}=-\frac{\left(\mu_1-1\right)}{\left(\mu_2-1\right)}{/tex}
  2. P = P1 + P2 = {tex}\frac{2}{R}\left[\mu_1-1-\mu_2+1\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{R}\left(\mu_1-\mu_2\right){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu_2>\mu_1{/tex}
    P is -ve

subject: Physics
question_html: Find the two possible positions of an object kept in front of a lens of +5.0 D, so that the image formed in both cases is four times magnified.
question_text: Find the two possible positions of an object kept in front of a lens of +5.0 D, so that the image formed in both cases is four times magnified.
explanation: We Know P = {tex}\frac{100}{f}{/tex} f = {tex}\frac{100}{p}=\frac{100}{5}{/tex} = 20 cm Case 1 m = +4 (image is erect) m = {tex}\frac{v}{u}{/tex} = +4 v = 4u By using lens formula {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4 u}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} {tex}\frac{-3}{4 u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} u = {tex}\frac{-20 \times 3}{4}{/tex} = -15 cm Case 2 m = -4 (real and inverted) m = {tex}\frac{v}{u}{/tex} = -4 v = -4u By lens formula {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac {1}{ f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{-4 u}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{-4 u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} u = {tex}\frac{-20 \times 5}{4}{/tex} = -25 cm so distance of object is 25 cm.
solution: We Know P = {tex}\frac{100}{f}{/tex} f = {tex}\frac{100}{p}=\frac{100}{5}{/tex} = 20 cm Case 1 m = +4 (image is erect) m = {tex}\frac{v}{u}{/tex} = +4 v = 4u By using lens formula {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4 u}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} {tex}\frac{-3}{4 u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} u = {tex}\frac{-20 \times 3}{4}{/tex} = -15 cm Case 2 m = -4 (real and inverted) m = {tex}\frac{v}{u}{/tex} = -4 v = -4u By lens formula {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac {1}{ f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{-4 u}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{-4 u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} u = {tex}\frac{-20 \times 5}{4}{/tex} = -25 cm so distance of object is 25 cm.
solution_html:
We Know P = {tex}\frac{100}{f}{/tex}
f = {tex}\frac{100}{p}=\frac{100}{5}{/tex} = 20 cm
Case 1
m = +4 (image is erect)
m = {tex}\frac{v}{u}{/tex} = +4
v = 4u
By using lens formula
{tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{4 u}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{-3}{4 u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex}
u = {tex}\frac{-20 \times 3}{4}{/tex}
= -15 cm
Case 2
m = -4 (real and inverted)
m = {tex}\frac{v}{u}{/tex} = -4
v = -4u
By lens formula
{tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac {1}{ f}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{-4 u}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{5}{-4 u}=\frac{1}{20}{/tex}
u = {tex}\frac{-20 \times 5}{4}{/tex}
= -25 cm

so distance of object is 25 cm.

subject: Physics
question_html: For a glass prism of refractive index {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}, the value of angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta{/tex} is equal to the angle of prism A. Find the angle of prism A. How will the angle {tex}\delta{/tex} be affected if the prism were immersed in water?
question_text: For a glass prism of refractive index {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} , the value of angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta{/tex} is equal to the angle of prism A. Find the angle of prism A. How will the angle {tex}\delta{/tex} be affected if the prism were immersed in water?
explanation: Using prism formula {tex}\mu=\sqrt{3}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} A = {tex}\delta{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{2 A}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin A}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}\cdot \frac{2 \sin \frac{A}{2} \cdot \cos \frac{A}{2}}{\sin \frac {A}{2}}{/tex} {tex}2 \cos \frac{A}{2}=\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\cos \frac{A}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex} = 30 o A = 60 o {tex}\mu{/tex} for prism with respect to water decreases so, angle of deviation also decreases.
solution: Using prism formula {tex}\mu=\sqrt{3}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} A = {tex}\delta{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{2 A}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin A}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}\cdot \frac{2 \sin \frac{A}{2} \cdot \cos \frac{A}{2}}{\sin \frac {A}{2}}{/tex} {tex}2 \cos \frac{A}{2}=\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\cos \frac{A}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex} = 30 o A = 60 o {tex}\mu{/tex} for prism with respect to water decreases so, angle of deviation also decreases.
solution_html:
Using prism formula

{tex}\mu=\sqrt{3}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} A = {tex}\delta{/tex}
{tex}\sqrt{3}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{2 A}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin A}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}\cdot \frac{2 \sin \frac{A}{2} \cdot \cos \frac{A}{2}}{\sin \frac {A}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}2 \cos \frac{A}{2}=\sqrt{3}{/tex}
{tex}\cos \frac{A}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex} = 30o
A = 60o
{tex}\mu{/tex} for prism with respect to water decreases so, angle of deviation also decreases.

question_html: Draw a labelled ray diagram showing the formation of an image by an astronomical refracting telescope in normal adjustment. Hence, obtain the expression for its magnifying power.
question_text: Draw a labelled ray diagram showing the formation of an image by an astronomical refracting telescope in normal adjustment. Hence, obtain the expression for its magnifying power.
explanation: The astronomical telescope is as shown below m = magnifying power = {tex}\frac{\beta}{\alpha}{/tex} as angles are small, so m = {tex}\frac{\tan \beta}{\tan \alpha}{/tex} In {tex}\triangle{/tex} A'B'C 2 {tex}\tan \beta=\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{C_2 B^{\prime}}{/tex} In {tex}\triangle{/tex} A'B'C 1 {tex}\tan \alpha=\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{C_1 B^{\prime}}{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{C_2 B^{\prime}} \times \frac{C_1 B^{\prime}}{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{C_1 B^{\prime}}{C_2 B^{\prime}}=\frac{f_o}{-f_e}{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{f_o}{-f_e}{/tex} f o {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} focal length of objective lens f e {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} focal length of eye lens.
solution: The astronomical telescope is as shown below m = magnifying power = {tex}\frac{\beta}{\alpha}{/tex} as angles are small, so m = {tex}\frac{\tan \beta}{\tan \alpha}{/tex} In {tex}\triangle{/tex} A'B'C 2 {tex}\tan \beta=\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{C_2 B^{\prime}}{/tex} In {tex}\triangle{/tex} A'B'C 1 {tex}\tan \alpha=\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{C_1 B^{\prime}}{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{C_2 B^{\prime}} \times \frac{C_1 B^{\prime}}{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{C_1 B^{\prime}}{C_2 B^{\prime}}=\frac{f_o}{-f_e}{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{f_o}{-f_e}{/tex} f o {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} focal length of objective lens f e {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} focal length of eye lens.
solution_html:
The astronomical telescope is as shown below 


m = magnifying power = {tex}\frac{\beta}{\alpha}{/tex}
as angles are small, so m = {tex}\frac{\tan \beta}{\tan \alpha}{/tex}
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}A'B'C2
{tex}\tan \beta=\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{C_2 B^{\prime}}{/tex}
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}A'B'C1
{tex}\tan \alpha=\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{C_1 B^{\prime}}{/tex}
m = {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{C_2 B^{\prime}} \times \frac{C_1 B^{\prime}}{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{C_1 B^{\prime}}{C_2 B^{\prime}}=\frac{f_o}{-f_e}{/tex}
m = {tex}\frac{f_o}{-f_e}{/tex}
fo {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}focal length of objective lens
fe{tex}\rightarrow{/tex}focal length of eye lens.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A ray is incident on a prism of material of refractive index {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} at point M such that it grazes along NC after emerging from the prism, as shown in the figure.

Find:

  1. the critical angle for the prism.
  2. the angle of refraction at face AB.
question_text: A ray is incident on a prism of material of refractive index {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} at point M such that it grazes along NC after emerging from the prism, as shown in the figure. Find: the critical angle for the prism. the angle of refraction at face AB.
explanation: Critical angle c is given as, sin c = {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} sin c = {tex}\frac {1} {\sqrt2}{/tex} sin c = sin 45 o c = 45 o r 2 = c = 45 o as we know r 1 + r 2 = A r 1 + 45 o = 60 o r 1 = 15 o
solution: Critical angle c is given as, sin c = {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} sin c = {tex}\frac {1} {\sqrt2}{/tex} sin c = sin 45 o c = 45 o r 2 = c = 45 o as we know r 1 + r 2 = A r 1 + 45 o = 60 o r 1 = 15 o
solution_html:
1. Critical angle c is given as,
    sin c = {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex}
    sin c = {tex}\frac {1} {\sqrt2}{/tex}
    sin c = sin 45o
    c = 45o


  2. r2 = c = 45o
    as we know 
    r1 + r2 = A
    r1 + 45o = 60o
    r1 = 15o

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a labelled ray diagram showing the formation of the image at least distance of distinct vision by a compound microscope.
  2. A small object is placed at a distance of 3.0 cm from a magnifier of focal length 4.0 cm. Find:
    1. the position of the image formed, and
    2. the linear magnification produced.
question_text: Draw a labelled ray diagram showing the formation of the image at least distance of distinct vision by a compound microscope. A small object is placed at a distance of 3.0 cm from a magnifier of focal length 4.0 cm. Find: the position of the image formed, and the linear magnification produced.
explanation: u = -3 cm f = 4 cm by lens formula {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-3}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{-1}{12}{/tex} v = -12 cm Left of the magnifier by magnification formula m = {tex}1-\frac{v}{f}{/tex} = {tex}1-\frac{(-12)}{4}{/tex} = 1 + 3 = 4
solution: u = -3 cm f = 4 cm by lens formula {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-3}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{-1}{12}{/tex} v = -12 cm Left of the magnifier by magnification formula m = {tex}1-\frac{v}{f}{/tex} = {tex}1-\frac{(-12)}{4}{/tex} = 1 + 3 = 4
solution_html:
1. u = -3 cm
      f = 4 cm                                                                                                                                    by lens formula
      {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
      {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-3}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
      {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{-1}{12}{/tex}
      v = -12 cm Left of the magnifier
    2. by magnification formula m = {tex}1-\frac{v}{f}{/tex}
      = {tex}1-\frac{(-12)}{4}{/tex}
      = 1 + 3
      = 4

subject: Physics
question_html:
A ray of light is incident on a prism at an angle of 45o and passes symmetrically as shown in the figure. Calculate:

  1. the angle of minimum deviation,
  2. the refractive index of the material of the prism, and
  3. the angle of refraction at the point P.
question_text: A ray of light is incident on a prism at an angle of 45 o and passes symmetrically as shown in the figure. Calculate: the angle of minimum deviation, the refractive index of the material of the prism, and the angle of refraction at the point P.
explanation: When ray passes symmetrically, the angle of minimum deviation is given as {tex}\delta{/tex} = 2i - A = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 45 - 60 = 90 - 60 = 30 o angle of refraction = r = {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {60} {2}{/tex} = 30 o Using snell's law n 1 sin i = n 2 sin r 1 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 45 o = n 2 sin 30 o n 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{2}{1}{/tex} n 2 = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} Therefore angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta{/tex} = 30 o Refractive index of prism is {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} Angle of refraction at point P is r = 30 o
solution: When ray passes symmetrically, the angle of minimum deviation is given as {tex}\delta{/tex} = 2i - A = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 45 - 60 = 90 - 60 = 30 o angle of refraction = r = {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {60} {2}{/tex} = 30 o Using snell's law n 1 sin i = n 2 sin r 1 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 45 o = n 2 sin 30 o n 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{2}{1}{/tex} n 2 = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} Therefore angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta{/tex} = 30 o Refractive index of prism is {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} Angle of refraction at point P is r = 30 o
solution_html:
When ray passes symmetrically,

the angle of minimum deviation is given as 
{tex}\delta{/tex} = 2i - A
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 45 - 60
= 90 - 60
= 30o
angle of refraction = r = {tex}\frac{A}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {60} {2}{/tex}
= 30o

Using snell's law
n1sin i = n2sin r
1 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 45o = n2 sin 30o
n2 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{2}{1}{/tex}
n2 = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}

  1.  Therefore angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta{/tex} = 30o
  2. Refractive index of prism is {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
  3. Angle of refraction at point P is r = 30o

question_html:
The figure shows a water column BCFE surrounded by two media 1 and 3 of the same refractive index {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex}. A ray of light from a point source S is incident on surface BE at an angle of 30o.

  1. Trace the path of ray through media 2 and 3 as it emerges out of face CG.
  2. Find the angle of emergence at face CG.
question_text: The figure shows a water column BCFE surrounded by two media 1 and 3 of the same refractive index {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} . A ray of light from a point source S is incident on surface BE at an angle of 30 o . Trace the path of ray through media 2 and 3 as it emerges out of face CG. Find the angle of emergence at face CG.
explanation: {tex}\mu{/tex} refractive index of water = 1.33 {tex}\mu_1=\mu_3=\sqrt{2}{/tex} = 1.41 Using snell's law sin 30 o {tex}\times{/tex} 1.41 = sin r 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.33 sin r 1 = {tex}\sin 30 \times \frac{1.41}{1.33}{/tex} = 0.53 r 1 = 32 o sin 32 o {tex}\times{/tex} 1.33 = sin r 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.41 sin r 2 = {tex}\sin \frac{32^{\circ} \times 1.33}{1.41}{/tex} = 0.49 r 2 = 30 o In {tex}\triangle{/tex} PRT {tex}\angle{/tex} TRP = 60 o corresponding {tex}\angle{/tex} to CGF So {tex}\angle{/tex} PTR = 90 o = r 3 thus emergence angle is 0 o to normal.
solution: {tex}\mu{/tex} refractive index of water = 1.33 {tex}\mu_1=\mu_3=\sqrt{2}{/tex} = 1.41 Using snell's law sin 30 o {tex}\times{/tex} 1.41 = sin r 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.33 sin r 1 = {tex}\sin 30 \times \frac{1.41}{1.33}{/tex} = 0.53 r 1 = 32 o sin 32 o {tex}\times{/tex} 1.33 = sin r 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.41 sin r 2 = {tex}\sin \frac{32^{\circ} \times 1.33}{1.41}{/tex} = 0.49 r 2 = 30 o In {tex}\triangle{/tex} PRT {tex}\angle{/tex} TRP = 60 o corresponding {tex}\angle{/tex} to CGF So {tex}\angle{/tex} PTR = 90 o = r 3 thus emergence angle is 0 o to normal.
solution_html:
{tex}\mu{/tex}  refractive index of water = 1.33
{tex}\mu_1=\mu_3=\sqrt{2}{/tex} = 1.41


  2. Using snell's law   sin 30o {tex}\times{/tex} 1.41 = sin r1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.33
    sin r1 = {tex}\sin 30 \times \frac{1.41}{1.33}{/tex} = 0.53
    r1 = 32o
    sin 32o {tex}\times{/tex} 1.33 = sin r2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.41
    sin r2 = {tex}\sin \frac{32^{\circ} \times 1.33}{1.41}{/tex} = 0.49
    r2 = 30o
    In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PRT
    {tex}\angle{/tex}TRP = 60o corresponding {tex}\angle{/tex} to CGF
    So {tex}\angle{/tex}PTR = 90o = r3
    thus emergence angle is 0o to normal.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A ray of light passes through a prism of refractive index {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} as shown in the figure. Find:

  1. The angle of incidence ({tex}\angle r_2{/tex}) at face AC.
  2. The angle of minimum deviation for this prism.
question_text: A ray of light passes through a prism of refractive index {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} as shown in the figure. Find: The angle of incidence ( {tex}\angle r_2{/tex} ) at face AC. The angle of minimum deviation for this prism.
explanation: Using snell's law {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} sin r 2 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 90 o sin r 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} r 2 = 45 o By prism formula {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30 o = {tex}\sin \left(60+\frac{\delta m}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{60+\delta_{m}}{2}{/tex} = 45 o {tex}30+\frac{\delta m}{2}{/tex} = 45 {tex}\frac{\delta_m}{2}{/tex} = 15 {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 30 o
solution: Using snell's law {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} sin r 2 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 90 o sin r 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} r 2 = 45 o By prism formula {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30 o = {tex}\sin \left(60+\frac{\delta m}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{60+\delta_{m}}{2}{/tex} = 45 o {tex}30+\frac{\delta m}{2}{/tex} = 45 {tex}\frac{\delta_m}{2}{/tex} = 15 {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 30 o
solution_html:
1. Using snell's law
    {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} sin r2 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 90o
    sin r2 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
    r2 = 45o
  2. By prism formula {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30o = {tex}\sin \left(60+\frac{\delta m}{2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\frac{60+\delta_{m}}{2}{/tex} = 45o
    {tex}30+\frac{\delta m}{2}{/tex} = 45
    {tex}\frac{\delta_m}{2}{/tex} = 15
    {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 30o

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Trace the path of a ray of light PQ which is incident at an angle i on one face of a glass prism of angle A. It then emerges out from the other face at an angle e. Use the ray diagram to prove that the angle through which the ray is deviated is given by {tex}\angle \delta{/tex} = {tex}\angle{/tex}i + {tex}\angle{/tex}e -{tex}\angle{/tex}A.
  2. What will be the minimum value of {tex}\delta{/tex} if the ray passes symmetrically through the prism?
question_text: Trace the path of a ray of light PQ which is incident at an angle i on one face of a glass prism of angle A. It then emerges out from the other face at an angle e. Use the ray diagram to prove that the angle through which the ray is deviated is given by {tex}\angle \delta{/tex} = {tex}\angle{/tex} i + {tex}\angle{/tex} e - {tex}\angle{/tex} A. What will be the minimum value of {tex}\delta{/tex} if the ray passes symmetrically through the prism?
explanation: {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i - r 1 ) + (e - r 2 ) {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i + e) - (r 1 + r 2 ) In Quadrilateral AQOR {tex}\angle{/tex} Q = {tex}\angle{/tex} R = 90 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex} A + {tex}\angle{/tex} O = 180 o ...(1) In {tex}\triangle{/tex} QOR O + r 1 + r 2 = 180 o ...(2) Comparing (1) and (2) {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = r 1 + r 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i + e) - A If a ray passes symmetrically through a prism (parallel to base of prism), the value of angle of deviation is minimum. At this angle {tex}\angle{/tex} i = {tex}\angle{/tex} e and {tex}\angle{/tex} r 1 = {tex}\angle{/tex} r 2
solution: {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i - r 1 ) + (e - r 2 ) {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i + e) - (r 1 + r 2 ) In Quadrilateral AQOR {tex}\angle{/tex} Q = {tex}\angle{/tex} R = 90 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex} A + {tex}\angle{/tex} O = 180 o ...(1) In {tex}\triangle{/tex} QOR O + r 1 + r 2 = 180 o ...(2) Comparing (1) and (2) {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = r 1 + r 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i + e) - A If a ray passes symmetrically through a prism (parallel to base of prism), the value of angle of deviation is minimum. At this angle {tex}\angle{/tex} i = {tex}\angle{/tex} e and {tex}\angle{/tex} r 1 = {tex}\angle{/tex} r 2
solution_html:
1. {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i - r1) + (e - r2)
    {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i + e) - (r1 + r2)
    In Quadrilateral AQOR
    {tex}\angle{/tex} Q = {tex}\angle{/tex} R = 90o
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}O = 180o ...(1)
    In {tex}\triangle{/tex}QOR
    O + r1 + r2 = 180o ...(2)
    Comparing (1) and (2)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = r1 + r2
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i + e) - A
  2. If a ray passes symmetrically through a prism (parallel to base of prism), the value of angle of deviation is minimum.
    At this angle{tex}\angle{/tex} i = {tex}\angle{/tex} e and {tex}\angle{/tex} r1 = {tex}\angle{/tex} r2

question_html:
1. State the conditions for total internal reflection to take place.
  2. A tank is filled with a transparent liquid to height H. A coin suspended by a thread in the liquid is gradually lowered till it touches the bottom. The apparent depth is determined corresponding to different positions of the coin.
    1. Plot a graph showing variation of the apparent depth with the real depth of the coin.
    2. What is the physical significance of the slope of the graph?
question_text: State the conditions for total internal reflection to take place. A tank is filled with a transparent liquid to height H. A coin suspended by a thread in the liquid is gradually lowered till it touches the bottom. The apparent depth is determined corresponding to different positions of the coin. Plot a graph showing variation of the apparent depth with the real depth of the coin. What is the physical significance of the slope of the graph?
explanation: The conditions for total internal reflection to take place are: The light should travel from denser to rarer medium. The angle of incidence should be greater than the critical angle for a given pair of media. The slope will give {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} where {tex}\mu{/tex} is refractive index as per the graph above.
solution: The conditions for total internal reflection to take place are: The light should travel from denser to rarer medium. The angle of incidence should be greater than the critical angle for a given pair of media. The slope will give {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} where {tex}\mu{/tex} is refractive index as per the graph above.
solution_html:
1. The conditions for total internal reflection to take place are:
    1. The light should travel from denser to rarer medium.
    2. The angle of incidence should be greater than the critical angle for a given pair of media.
    2. The slope will give {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} where {tex}\mu{/tex} is refractive index as per the graph above.

question_html:
A converging lens made of glass ({tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5) has its spherical faces of radii of curvature 10 cm and 20 cm. Find its focal length

  1. in air, and
  2. when it is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.25.
question_text: A converging lens made of glass ( {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5) has its spherical faces of radii of curvature 10 cm and 20 cm. Find its focal length in air, and when it is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.25.
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{f_a}{/tex} = {tex}\left({ }_a \mu^g-1\right){/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} R 1 = 10 cm & R 2 = -20 cm, {tex}a \mu^g{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\frac{1}{f_a}{/tex} = (1.5 - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{-20}\right){/tex} {tex} f_a{/tex} = {tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex} = 13.3 cm {tex}\frac{1}{f_l}{/tex} = {tex}\left({ }_l \mu^g-1\right){/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{1.5}{1.25}-1\right){/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{-20}\right){/tex} {tex}f_1{/tex} = {tex}\frac{100}{3}{/tex} = 33.3 cm
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{f_a}{/tex} = {tex}\left({ }_a \mu^g-1\right){/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} R 1 = 10 cm & R 2 = -20 cm, {tex}a \mu^g{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\frac{1}{f_a}{/tex} = (1.5 - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{-20}\right){/tex} {tex} f_a{/tex} = {tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex} = 13.3 cm {tex}\frac{1}{f_l}{/tex} = {tex}\left({ }_l \mu^g-1\right){/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{1.5}{1.25}-1\right){/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{-20}\right){/tex} {tex}f_1{/tex} = {tex}\frac{100}{3}{/tex} = 33.3 cm
solution_html:
1. {tex}\frac{1}{f_a}{/tex} = {tex}\left({ }_a \mu^g-1\right){/tex}{tex}\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
    R1 = 10 cm & R2 = -20 cm, {tex}a \mu^g{/tex} = 1.5
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_a}{/tex} = (1.5 - 1){tex}\left(\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{-20}\right){/tex}
    {tex} f_a{/tex} = {tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex} = 13.3 cm
  2. {tex}\frac{1}{f_l}{/tex} = {tex}\left({ }_l \mu^g-1\right){/tex}{tex}\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
    = {tex}\left(\frac{1.5}{1.25}-1\right){/tex}{tex}\left(\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{-20}\right){/tex}
    {tex}f_1{/tex} = {tex}\frac{100}{3}{/tex} = 33.3 cm

question_html:
The refractive index of an equilateral triangular prism kept in air is {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex}. Calculate:

  1. the angle of minimum deviation.
  2. the critical angle for the prism.
question_text: The refractive index of an equilateral triangular prism kept in air is {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} . Calculate: the angle of minimum deviation. the critical angle for the prism.
explanation: {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\left.\delta_m+A\right)}{2}\right.}{\sin (A / 2)}{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\delta_m+60^{\circ}}{2}\right)}{\sin 30^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sin \left(\frac{\delta_m+60^{\circ}}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\sin \left(\frac{\delta_m+60^{\circ}}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{\delta_m+60^{\circ}}{2}\right){/tex} = 45 o {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 90 o - 60 o {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 30 o Sin {tex} \theta_c{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex} \theta_c{/tex} = 45 o
solution: {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\left.\delta_m+A\right)}{2}\right.}{\sin (A / 2)}{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\delta_m+60^{\circ}}{2}\right)}{\sin 30^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sin \left(\frac{\delta_m+60^{\circ}}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\sin \left(\frac{\delta_m+60^{\circ}}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{\delta_m+60^{\circ}}{2}\right){/tex} = 45 o {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 90 o - 60 o {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 30 o Sin {tex} \theta_c{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex} \theta_c{/tex} = 45 o
solution_html:
1. {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\left.\delta_m+A\right)}{2}\right.}{\sin (A / 2)}{/tex}
    {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\delta_m+60^{\circ}}{2}\right)}{\sin 30^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sin \left(\frac{\delta_m+60^{\circ}}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\sin \left(\frac{\delta_m+60^{\circ}}{2}\right){/tex}= {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{\delta_m+60^{\circ}}{2}\right){/tex}= 45o
    {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 90o - 60o {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 30o
  2. Sin{tex} \theta_c{/tex}= {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
    {tex} \theta_c{/tex} = 45o

question_html: An equiconvex lens forms a two times enlarged real image when an object is kept 16 cm from it. The lens is cut into two identical plano-convex lenses. If the object is again kept 16 cm in front of one of these lenses, then find the nature and position of the image formed.
question_text: An equiconvex lens forms a two times enlarged real image when an object is kept 16 cm from it. The lens is cut into two identical plano-convex lenses. If the object is again kept 16 cm in front of one of these lenses, then find the nature and position of the image formed.
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{f}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{32}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{16}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1+2}{32}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{32}{/tex} f = {tex}\frac{32}{3}{/tex} cm When lens is cut into two equal halves the new focal length = f' = 2f = {tex}\frac{64}{3}{/tex} cm {tex}\frac{1}{f^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{64}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{16}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{64}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{16}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3-4}{64}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{64}{/tex} y = -64 cm Image formed is virtual & erect.
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{f}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{32}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{16}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1+2}{32}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{32}{/tex} f = {tex}\frac{32}{3}{/tex} cm When lens is cut into two equal halves the new focal length = f' = 2f = {tex}\frac{64}{3}{/tex} cm {tex}\frac{1}{f^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{64}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{16}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{64}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{16}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3-4}{64}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{64}{/tex} y = -64 cm Image formed is virtual & erect.
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{f}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{f}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{32}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{16}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1+2}{32}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{32}{/tex}
f = {tex}\frac{32}{3}{/tex} cm
When lens is cut into two equal halves the new focal length = f' = 2f = {tex}\frac{64}{3}{/tex} cm
{tex}\frac{1}{f^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{64}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{16}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{64}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{16}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3-4}{64}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{64}{/tex}
y = -64 cm
Image formed is virtual & erect.

question_html:

  2. Two prisms ABC and DBC are arranged as shown in figure.

    The critical angles for the two prisms with respect to air are 41.1o and 45o respectively. Trace the path of the ray through the combination.
question_text: Write two necessary conditions for total internal reflection. Two prisms ABC and DBC are arranged as shown in figure. The critical angles for the two prisms with respect to air are 41.1 o and 45 o respectively. Trace the path of the ray through the combination.
explanation: Two conditions The light must travel from an optically denser medium to a rarer medium. Angle of incidence should be greater than the critical angle.
solution: Two conditions The light must travel from an optically denser medium to a rarer medium. Angle of incidence should be greater than the critical angle.
solution_html:
1. Two conditions
    1. The light must travel from an optically denser medium to a rarer medium.
    2. Angle of incidence should be greater than the critical angle.

question_html:
1. An object is placed in front of a converging lens. Obtain the conditions under which the magnification produced by the lens is (i) negative and (ii) positive.
  2. A point object is placed at O in front of a glass sphere as shown in figure.

    Show the formation of image by the sphere.
question_text: An object is placed in front of a converging lens. Obtain the conditions under which the magnification produced by the lens is (i) negative and (ii) positive. A point object is placed at O in front of a glass sphere as shown in figure. Show the formation of image by the sphere.
explanation: Two conditions m = {tex} \frac{h^{\prime}}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v}{u}{/tex} For real images / when object is placed beyond F, As u is negative and v is positive. for virtual image / when object is kept between F and the optical centre of the lens. As u and v both are negative.
solution: Two conditions m = {tex} \frac{h^{\prime}}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v}{u}{/tex} For real images / when object is placed beyond F, As u is negative and v is positive. for virtual image / when object is kept between F and the optical centre of the lens. As u and v both are negative.
solution_html:
1. Two conditions m = {tex} \frac{h^{\prime}}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v}{u}{/tex}
    1. For real images / when object is placed beyond F, As u is negative and v is positive.
    2. for virtual image / when object is kept between F and the optical centre of the lens. As u and v both are negative.

subject: Physics
question_html: A diver looking up through water {tex}\left(\mu=\frac{4}{3}\right){/tex} sees the outside world contained in a circular area on the surface of water. If the diver's eyes are {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex} m below the surface of water, then calculate the area of the circle.
question_text: A diver looking up through water {tex}\left(\mu=\frac{4}{3}\right){/tex} sees the outside world contained in a circular area on the surface of water. If the diver's eyes are {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex} m below the surface of water, then calculate the area of the circle.
explanation: Sin C = {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} Sin C = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} Sin C = {tex}\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+7}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+7}}{/tex} R = 3m Area = {tex}\pi R^2{/tex} = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} (3) 2 = 28.26 m 2
solution: Sin C = {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} Sin C = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} Sin C = {tex}\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+7}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+7}}{/tex} R = 3m Area = {tex}\pi R^2{/tex} = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} (3) 2 = 28.26 m 2
solution_html:
Sin C = {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}{/tex}
Sin C = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
Sin C = {tex}\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+7}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+7}}{/tex}
R = 3m
Area = {tex}\pi R^2{/tex}
= 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} (3)2
= 28.26 m2

question_html: With the help of a ray diagram explain the working of a reflecting telescope. Mention two advantages of a reflecting telescope over a refracting telescope.
question_text: With the help of a ray diagram explain the working of a reflecting telescope. Mention two advantages of a reflecting telescope over a refracting telescope.
explanation: Ray diagram of reflecting telescope Working Working: Parallel beam of light gathered by objective mirror is reflected to the secondary mirror, which further forms the image in front of the eyepiece.
solution: Ray diagram of reflecting telescope Working Working: Parallel beam of light gathered by objective mirror is reflected to the secondary mirror, which further forms the image in front of the eyepiece.
solution_html:
Ray diagram of reflecting telescope Working

Working: Parallel beam of light gathered by objective mirror is reflected to the secondary mirror, which further forms the image in front of the eyepiece.

subject: Physics
question_html: With the help of a ray diagram, show how a compound microscope forms a magnified image of a tiny object, at least distance of distinct vision. Hence derive an expression for the magnification produced by it.
question_text: With the help of a ray diagram, show how a compound microscope forms a magnified image of a tiny object, at least distance of distinct vision. Hence derive an expression for the magnification produced by it.
explanation: The linear magnification due to objective lens m 0 = {tex}\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{L}{f_0}{/tex} ...(i) h = size of object h' = size of first image As tan {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{f_0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h^{\prime}}{L}{/tex} magnification due to eye piece m e = 1 + {tex}\frac{D}{f_e}{/tex} ...(ii) Total Magnification m = m o {tex}\times{/tex} m e m. = {tex}\frac{L}{f_0}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex}
solution: The linear magnification due to objective lens m 0 = {tex}\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{L}{f_0}{/tex} ...(i) h = size of object h' = size of first image As tan {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{f_0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h^{\prime}}{L}{/tex} magnification due to eye piece m e = 1 + {tex}\frac{D}{f_e}{/tex} ...(ii) Total Magnification m = m o {tex}\times{/tex} m e m. = {tex}\frac{L}{f_0}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
The linear magnification due to objective lens
m0 = {tex}\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{L}{f_0}{/tex} ...(i)
h = size of object
h' = size of first image
As tan{tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{f_0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h^{\prime}}{L}{/tex}
magnification due to eye piece
me = 1 + {tex}\frac{D}{f_e}{/tex} ...(ii)
Total Magnification m = mo {tex}\times{/tex} me
m. = {tex}\frac{L}{f_0}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A ray of light travels from a denser to a rarer medium. After refraction, it bends away from the normal. When we keep increasing the angle of incidence, the angle of refraction also increases till the refracted ray grazes along the interface of two media. The angle of incidence for which it happens is called critical angle. If the angle of incidence is increased further the ray will not emerge and it will be reflected back in the denser medium. This phenomenon is called total internal reflection of light.


A ray of light travels from a medium into water at angle of incidence of {tex}18^{\circ}{/tex}. The refractive index of the medium is more than that of water and the critical angle for the interface between the two media is {tex}20^{\circ}{/tex}. Which one of the following figures best represents the correct path of the ray of light?
question_text: A ray of light travels from a denser to a rarer medium. After refraction, it bends away from the normal. The angle of incidence for which it happens is called critical angle. If the angle of incidence is increased further the ray will not emerge and it will be reflected back in the denser medium. This phenomenon is called total internal reflection of light. A ray of light travels from a medium into water at angle of incidence of {tex}18^{\circ}{/tex} . The refractive index of the medium is more than that of water and the critical angle for the interface between the two media is {tex}20^{\circ}{/tex} . Which one of the following figures best represents the correct path of the ray of light?

explanation: As the ray is travelling from denser to rarer medium and the angle of incidence is smaller than the critical angle, the ray will refract and will bend away from the normal.

question_html:
Two transparent media of refractive indices n1 and n2 are separated by a spherical transparent surface. The rays of light incident on the surface get refracted into the medium on the other side. The laws of refraction are valid at each point of the spherical surface. A lens is a transparent optical medium bounded by two surfaces, at least one of which should be spherical. The focal length of a lens is determined by the radii of curvature (R1 and R2) of its two surfaces and the refractive index (n) of the medium of the lens with respect to the surrounding medium. Depending on R1 and R2, a lens behaves as a diverging or a converging lens. The ability of a lens to diverge or converge a beam of light incident on it defines its power.

question_html:
A compound microscope consists of two converging lenses. One of them, of smaller aperture and smaller focal length is called objective and the other of slightly larger aperture and slightly larger focal length is called eye-piece. Both the lenses are fitted in a tube with an arrangement to vary the distance between them. A tiny object is placed in front of the objective at a distance slightly greater than its focal length. The objective produces the image of the object which acts as an object for the eye-piece. The eye-piece, in turn produces the final magnified image.
