question_html: An equilateral glass prism has a refractive index 1.6 in air. Calculate the angle of minimum deviation of the prism, when kept in a medium of refractive index {tex}\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{5}{/tex}.
question_text: An equilateral glass prism has a refractive index 1.6 in air. Calculate the angle of minimum deviation of the prism, when kept in a medium of refractive index {tex}\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{5}{/tex} .
explanation: a {tex}\mu{/tex} g = 1.6, a {tex}\mu{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{5}{/tex} , A = 60 o Thus, m {tex}\mu{/tex} g = {tex}\frac{1.6}{\frac{4}{5} \sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 1.414 = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} Now, {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \frac{\left(A+\delta_{m}\right)}{2}}{\sin A / 2}{/tex} where {tex}\delta_m{/tex} is the angle of minimum deviation. Thus, {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\frac{60+\delta_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin 60 / 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \frac{\left(60+\delta_{m}\right)}{2}}{\sin 30}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} sin ( {tex}\frac{60 + \delta_m}{2}{/tex} ) = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = sin 45 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{60 + \delta_m}{2}{/tex} = 45 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 30 o
solution: a {tex}\mu{/tex} g = 1.6, a {tex}\mu{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{5}{/tex} , A = 60 o Thus, m {tex}\mu{/tex} g = {tex}\frac{1.6}{\frac{4}{5} \sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 1.414 = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} Now, {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \frac{\left(A+\delta_{m}\right)}{2}}{\sin A / 2}{/tex} where {tex}\delta_m{/tex} is the angle of minimum deviation. Thus, {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\frac{60+\delta_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin 60 / 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \frac{\left(60+\delta_{m}\right)}{2}}{\sin 30}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} sin ( {tex}\frac{60 + \delta_m}{2}{/tex} ) = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = sin 45 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{60 + \delta_m}{2}{/tex} = 45 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 30 o
solution_html:
a{tex}\mu{/tex}g = 1.6, a{tex}\mu{/tex}m = {tex}\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{5}{/tex} , A = 60o
Thus, m{tex}\mu{/tex}g = {tex}\frac{1.6}{\frac{4}{5} \sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 1.414 = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \frac{\left(A+\delta_{m}\right)}{2}}{\sin A / 2}{/tex}
where {tex}\delta_m{/tex} is the angle of minimum deviation. Thus,
{tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\frac{60+\delta_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin 60 / 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \frac{\left(60+\delta_{m}\right)}{2}}{\sin 30}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} sin ({tex}\frac{60 + \delta_m}{2}{/tex}) = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = sin 45o 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{60 + \delta_m}{2}{/tex} = 45
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 30o

question_html: For paraxial rays, show that the focal length of a spherical mirror is one-half of its radius of curvature.
question_text: For paraxial rays, show that the focal length of a spherical mirror is one-half of its radius of curvature.
explanation: From ray diagram, tan {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{MD}{CD}{/tex} and tan 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{MD}{FD}{/tex} For small {tex}\theta{/tex} , tan {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} , tan 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{MD}{FD}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\frac{MD}{CD}{/tex} FD = {tex}\frac{CD}{2}{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\frac{CP}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} f = {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex}
solution: From ray diagram, tan {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{MD}{CD}{/tex} and tan 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{MD}{FD}{/tex} For small {tex}\theta{/tex} , tan {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} , tan 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{MD}{FD}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\frac{MD}{CD}{/tex} FD = {tex}\frac{CD}{2}{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\frac{CP}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} f = {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
From ray diagram,
tan {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{MD}{CD}{/tex} and tan 2{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{MD}{FD}{/tex}
For small {tex}\theta{/tex}, tan {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex}, tan 2{tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} 2{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{MD}{FD}{/tex} = 2{tex}\frac{MD}{CD}{/tex}
FD = {tex}\frac{CD}{2}{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\frac{CP}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f = {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex}

Draw a labelled ray diagram of an astronomical telescope in the near point adjustment position.
A giant refracting telescope at an observatory has an objective lens of focal length 15 m and an eyepiece of focal length 1.0 cm. If an eyepiece of focal length 1.0 cm is used, what is the angular magnification of the telescope? If this telescope is used to view the Moon, find the diameter of the image of the Moon formed by the objective lens. The diameter of the Moon is 3.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m, and the radius of lunar orbit is 3.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m.
question_text: Draw a labelled ray diagram of an astronomical telescope in the near point adjustment position. A giant refracting telescope at an observatory has an objective lens of focal length 15 m and an eyepiece of focal length 1.0 cm. If an eyepiece of focal length 1.0 cm is used, what is the angular magnification of the telescope? If this telescope is used to view the Moon, find the diameter of the image of the Moon formed by the objective lens. The diameter of the Moon is 3.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m, and the radius of lunar orbit is 3.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m.
explanation: A labelled ray diagram of an astronomical telescope in the near point adjustment position is shown below: Given:- Objective lens of focal length, f o = 15 m, eyepiece of focal length, f e = 1.0 cm = 0.01 m, diameter of the Moon, D = 3.48 × 10 6 m, radius of lunar orbit, r = 3.87 × 10 8 m. Angular magnification of a telescope, m = {tex}\frac{f_{o}}{f_{\mathrm{e}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{15}{0.01}{/tex} = 1500 Let d be the diameter of the image of the moon formed by the objective. Then, {tex}\frac Dr = \frac d{f_o}{/tex} {tex}\therefore\;{/tex} d = {tex}\frac Dr . f_o{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3.48 \times 10^{6}}{3.87 \times 10^{8}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 0.137 m = 13.7 cm
solution: A labelled ray diagram of an astronomical telescope in the near point adjustment position is shown below: Given:- Objective lens of focal length, f o = 15 m, eyepiece of focal length, f e = 1.0 cm = 0.01 m, diameter of the Moon, D = 3.48 × 10 6 m, radius of lunar orbit, r = 3.87 × 10 8 m. Angular magnification of a telescope, m = {tex}\frac{f_{o}}{f_{\mathrm{e}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{15}{0.01}{/tex} = 1500 Let d be the diameter of the image of the moon formed by the objective. Then, {tex}\frac Dr = \frac d{f_o}{/tex} {tex}\therefore\;{/tex} d = {tex}\frac Dr . f_o{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3.48 \times 10^{6}}{3.87 \times 10^{8}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 0.137 m = 13.7 cm
solution_html:
A labelled ray diagram of an astronomical telescope in the near point adjustment position is shown below:


Given:- Objective lens of focal length, fo = 15 m, eyepiece of focal length, fe = 1.0 cm = 0.01 m, diameter of the Moon, D = 3.48 × 106 m, radius of lunar orbit, r = 3.87 × 108 m.
Angular magnification of a telescope, m = {tex}\frac{f_{o}}{f_{\mathrm{e}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{15}{0.01}{/tex} = 1500
Let d be the diameter of the image of the moon formed by the objective.
Then,
{tex}\frac Dr = \frac d{f_o}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore\;{/tex}d = {tex}\frac Dr . f_o{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3.48 \times 10^{6}}{3.87 \times 10^{8}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 0.137 m = 13.7 cm

question_html: The human eye has an approximate angular resolution of {tex}\phi{/tex} = 5.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 rad and a typical photoprinter prints a minimum of 300 dpi (dots per inch, 1 inch = 2.54cm). At what a minimal distance z should a printed page be held so that one does not see the individual dots.
question_text: The human eye has an approximate angular resolution of {tex}\phi{/tex} = 5.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 rad and a typical photoprinter prints a minimum of 300 dpi (dots per inch, 1 inch = 2.54cm). At what a minimal distance z should a printed page be held so that one does not see the individual dots.
explanation: As there are 300 dots per inch the printed matter, the linear distance between the two dots, x = {tex}\frac{2.54}{300}{/tex} = 8.47 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 cm Limits of angular resolution, {tex}\phi{/tex} = 5.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 rad The minimal distance z, where a printed page should be held, so that one does not see the individual dots is given by {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x}{z}{/tex} {tex}\left( {\because {{\theta = }}\frac{l}{{\text{r}}}} \right){/tex} or z = {tex}\frac{x}{\phi}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8.47 \times 10^{-3}}{5.8 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 14.6 cm
solution: As there are 300 dots per inch the printed matter, the linear distance between the two dots, x = {tex}\frac{2.54}{300}{/tex} = 8.47 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 cm Limits of angular resolution, {tex}\phi{/tex} = 5.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 rad The minimal distance z, where a printed page should be held, so that one does not see the individual dots is given by {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x}{z}{/tex} {tex}\left( {\because {{\theta = }}\frac{l}{{\text{r}}}} \right){/tex} or z = {tex}\frac{x}{\phi}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8.47 \times 10^{-3}}{5.8 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 14.6 cm
solution_html:
As there are 300 dots per inch the printed matter, the linear distance between the two dots,
x = {tex}\frac{2.54}{300}{/tex} = 8.47 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 cm
Limits of angular resolution,
{tex}\phi{/tex} = 5.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 rad
The minimal distance z, where a printed page should be held, so that one does not see the individual dots is given by
{tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x}{z}{/tex} {tex}\left( {\because {{\theta = }}\frac{l}{{\text{r}}}} \right){/tex}
or z = {tex}\frac{x}{\phi}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8.47 \times 10^{-3}}{5.8 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 14.6 cm

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Under what conditions is the phenomenon of total internal reflection of light observed? Obtain the relation between the critical angle of incidence and the refractive index of the medium.
  2. Three lenses of focal lengths +10 cm, -10 cm and +30 cm are arranged coaxially as in the figure given below. Find the position of the final image formed by the combination.
question_text: Under what conditions is the phenomenon of total internal reflection of light observed? Obtain the relation between the critical angle of incidence and the refractive index of the medium. Three lenses of focal lengths +10 cm, -10 cm and +30 cm are arranged coaxially as in the figure given below. Find the position of the final image formed by the combination.
explanation: The conditions for total internal reflection are: Light must be trying to travel from optically denser medium to optically lighter medium. The angle of incidence must be greater than a certain angle known as critical angle. Derivation: Suppose light is travelling from medium 1 to medium 2. When light strikes the surface separating the media, some of it refracts and some of it reflects. If we keep increasing the angle of incidence, the refracted ray becomes parallel to the surface. This value of angle of incidence is known as the critical angle. If we increase the angle of incidence, even further, we will have a reflected ray and no refracted ray. Thus, we will have total internal reflection. Let us consider the condition when the angle of incidence is equal to critical angle, i c . By Snell’s law, {tex}\frac{\sin\;i_c}{sin\;90}=\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex} where n 1 and n 2 are the refractive indices of medium 1 and medium 2 respectively. Let n 12 be the refractive index of 1 with respect to 2. {tex}\sin\;i_c=\frac1{n_{12}}{/tex} i c = sin -1 ( {tex}\frac1{n_{12}}{/tex} ) Thus, we have obtained the required relation. For convex lens of focal length 10 cm, {tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}} - \frac{1}{u_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{-30}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v 1 = 15 cm Object distance for cancave lens u 2 = 15 - 5 = 10 cm {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{2}} - \frac{1}{u_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{-10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v 2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} For third lens, {tex}\frac{1}{f_{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{3}}-\frac{1}{u_{3}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{30}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{3}}-\frac{1}{\infty}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v 3 = 30 cm
solution: The conditions for total internal reflection are: Light must be trying to travel from optically denser medium to optically lighter medium. The angle of incidence must be greater than a certain angle known as critical angle. Derivation: Suppose light is travelling from medium 1 to medium 2. When light strikes the surface separating the media, some of it refracts and some of it reflects. If we keep increasing the angle of incidence, the refracted ray becomes parallel to the surface. This value of angle of incidence is known as the critical angle. If we increase the angle of incidence, even further, we will have a reflected ray and no refracted ray. Thus, we will have total internal reflection. Let us consider the condition when the angle of incidence is equal to critical angle, i c . By Snell’s law, {tex}\frac{\sin\;i_c}{sin\;90}=\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex} where n 1 and n 2 are the refractive indices of medium 1 and medium 2 respectively. Let n 12 be the refractive index of 1 with respect to 2. {tex}\sin\;i_c=\frac1{n_{12}}{/tex} i c = sin -1 ( {tex}\frac1{n_{12}}{/tex} ) Thus, we have obtained the required relation. For convex lens of focal length 10 cm, {tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}} - \frac{1}{u_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{-30}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v 1 = 15 cm Object distance for cancave lens u 2 = 15 - 5 = 10 cm {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{2}} - \frac{1}{u_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{-10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v 2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} For third lens, {tex}\frac{1}{f_{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{3}}-\frac{1}{u_{3}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{30}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{3}}-\frac{1}{\infty}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v 3 = 30 cm
solution_html:
1. The conditions for total internal reflection are:
    1. Light must be trying to travel from optically denser medium to optically lighter medium.
    2. The angle of incidence must be greater than a certain angle known as critical angle.
      Derivation:
      Suppose light is travelling from medium 1 to medium 2. When light strikes the surface separating the media, some of it refracts and some of it reflects. If we keep increasing the angle of incidence, the refracted ray becomes parallel to the surface. This value of angle of incidence is known as the critical angle. If we increase the angle of incidence, even further, we will have a reflected ray and no refracted ray. Thus, we will have total internal reflection.

      Let us consider the condition when the angle of incidence is equal to critical angle, ic .
      By Snell’s law,
      {tex}\frac{\sin\;i_c}{sin\;90}=\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex}
      where n1 and n2 are the refractive indices of medium 1 and medium 2 respectively. Let n12 be the refractive index of 1 with respect to 2.
      {tex}\sin\;i_c=\frac1{n_{12}}{/tex}
      ic = sin-1({tex}\frac1{n_{12}}{/tex})
      Thus, we have obtained the required relation.
  2. For convex lens of focal length 10 cm,
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}} - \frac{1}{u_{1}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{-30}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v1 = 15 cm
    Object distance for cancave lens u2 = 15 - 5 = 10 cm
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{2}} - \frac{1}{u_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{-10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} 
    For third lens,
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{3}}-\frac{1}{u_{3}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{30}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v_{3}}-\frac{1}{\infty}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v3 = 30 cm

question_html: A screen is placed 90 cm from an object. The image of the object on the screen is formed by a convex lens at two different locations separated by 20 cm. Determine the focal length of the lens.
question_text: A screen is placed 90 cm from an object. The image of the object on the screen is formed by a convex lens at two different locations separated by 20 cm. Determine the focal length of the lens.
explanation: Here, distance between object and screen, D = 90 cm Distance between two locations of convex lens, d = 20 cm Since, {tex}f = \frac{{{D^2} - {d^2}}}{{4D}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore f = \frac{{{{(90)}^2} - {{(20)}^2}}}{{4 \times 90}} = \frac{{(90 + 20)(90 - 20)}}{{360}} = \frac{{110 \times 7}}{{36}}{/tex} f = 21.4 cm
solution: Here, distance between object and screen, D = 90 cm Distance between two locations of convex lens, d = 20 cm Since, {tex}f = \frac{{{D^2} - {d^2}}}{{4D}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore f = \frac{{{{(90)}^2} - {{(20)}^2}}}{{4 \times 90}} = \frac{{(90 + 20)(90 - 20)}}{{360}} = \frac{{110 \times 7}}{{36}}{/tex} f = 21.4 cm
solution_html:
Here, distance between object and screen, D = 90 cm
Distance between two locations of convex lens, d = 20 cm
Since, {tex}f = \frac{{{D^2} - {d^2}}}{{4D}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore f = \frac{{{{(90)}^2} - {{(20)}^2}}}{{4 \times 90}} = \frac{{(90 + 20)(90 - 20)}}{{360}} = \frac{{110 \times 7}}{{36}}{/tex}
f = 21.4 cm

question_html: A convex lens of focal length 20 cm and a concave lens of focal length 15 cm are kept 30 cm apart with their principal axes coincident. When an object is placed 30 cm in front of the convex lens, calculate the position of the final image formed by the combination. Would this result change if the object were placed 30 cm in front of the concave lens? Give reason.
question_text: A convex lens of focal length 20 cm and a concave lens of focal length 15 cm are kept 30 cm apart with their principal axes coincident. When an object is placed 30 cm in front of the convex lens, calculate the position of the final image formed by the combination. Would this result change if the object were placed 30 cm in front of the concave lens? Give reason.
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{20}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{30}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{20 \times 30}{30-20}=\frac{600}{10}=60 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} u for concave lens = +30 cm {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{-15}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{30}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{15 \times 30}{15-30}=\frac{450}{15}=30 cm{/tex} No, the result will not change from principle of reversibility.
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{20}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{30}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{20 \times 30}{30-20}=\frac{600}{10}=60 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} u for concave lens = +30 cm {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{-15}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{30}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{15 \times 30}{15-30}=\frac{450}{15}=30 cm{/tex} No, the result will not change from principle of reversibility.
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{20}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{30}{/tex}
{tex}v=\frac{20 \times 30}{30-20}=\frac{600}{10}=60 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
u for concave lens = +30 cm
{tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{-15}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{30}{/tex}
{tex}v=\frac{15 \times 30}{15-30}=\frac{450}{15}=30 cm{/tex}
No, the result will not change from principle of reversibility.

question_html: In the case of a concave mirror of focal length f, when an object is kept between f and 2f, show that its image is formed beyond 2f.
question_text: In the case of a concave mirror of focal length f, when an object is kept between f and 2f, show that its image is formed beyond 2f.
explanation: Let u = -(2f - x), x < f, x > 0, f < 0 By mirror equation {tex}=\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{-f}+\frac{1}{(2 f-x)}=\frac{1}{(2 f-x)}-\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{f-2 f+x}{f(2 f-x)}=\frac{-(f-x)}{f(2 f-x)}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{-(f-x)}{f(2 f-x)}>2 f{/tex} as {tex}x<\mathrm{f}{/tex} The image is real and enlarged. Alternatively, a student can explain with the help of the ray diagram.
solution: Let u = -(2f - x), x < f, x > 0, f < 0 By mirror equation {tex}=\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{-f}+\frac{1}{(2 f-x)}=\frac{1}{(2 f-x)}-\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{f-2 f+x}{f(2 f-x)}=\frac{-(f-x)}{f(2 f-x)}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{-(f-x)}{f(2 f-x)}>2 f{/tex} as {tex}x<\mathrm{f}{/tex} The image is real and enlarged. Alternatively, a student can explain with the help of the ray diagram.
solution_html:
Let u = -(2f - x), x < f, x > 0, f < 0
By mirror equation {tex}=\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{-f}+\frac{1}{(2 f-x)}=\frac{1}{(2 f-x)}-\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{f-2 f+x}{f(2 f-x)}=\frac{-(f-x)}{f(2 f-x)}{/tex}
{tex}v=\frac{-(f-x)}{f(2 f-x)}>2 f{/tex} as {tex}x<\mathrm{f}{/tex}
The image is real and enlarged.
Alternatively, a student can explain with the help of the ray diagram.

question_html: Draw a ray diagram to show the image formation of a distant object by a refracting telescope. Write the expression for its angular magnification in terms of the focal lengths of the lenses used. State the important considerations required to achieve large resolution and their consequent limitations.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram to show the image formation of a distant object by a refracting telescope. Write the expression for its angular magnification in terms of the focal lengths of the lenses used. State the important considerations required to achieve large resolution and their consequent limitations.
explanation: Angular magnification {tex}m=\frac{-f_0}{f_e}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{f_0}{f_e}{/tex} Important considerations: For achieving large resolution, the objective of large aperture is required. Consequent Limitation: Heavy, hence difficult to make and support by their edge / suffers with chromatic aberrations.
solution: Angular magnification {tex}m=\frac{-f_0}{f_e}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{f_0}{f_e}{/tex} Important considerations: For achieving large resolution, the objective of large aperture is required. Consequent Limitation: Heavy, hence difficult to make and support by their edge / suffers with chromatic aberrations.
solution_html:
Angular magnification {tex}m=\frac{-f_0}{f_e}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{f_0}{f_e}{/tex}
Important considerations:
For achieving large resolution, the objective of large aperture is required.

Consequent Limitation: Heavy, hence difficult to make and support by their edge / suffers with chromatic aberrations.

  2. Derive the relation for the refractive index of the prism in terms of the angle of minimum deviation and angle of prism.
question_text: Plot a graph for angle of deviation as a function of angle of incidence for a triangular prism. Derive the relation for the refractive index of the prism in terms of the angle of minimum deviation and angle of prism.
explanation: Since {tex} \mathrm{n}_{21}=\frac{\sin \mathrm{i}}{\sin \mathrm{r}} {/tex} From the figure and calculations r 1 + r 2 = A At minimum deviation i.e. {tex}\delta = \delta{/tex} m, i = e and r 1 = r 2 = r {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = {tex}\frac {A} {2}{/tex} ...(eq n 1) From the figure {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i - r 1 ) + (e - r 2 ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\delta_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} = (i + e) + (r 1 + r 2 ) {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}{/tex} ... (eq n 2) {tex}\therefore n_{21}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\operatorname{Sin} \frac{A+\delta_m}{2}}{\sin \frac{A}2}{/tex}
solution: Since {tex} \mathrm{n}_{21}=\frac{\sin \mathrm{i}}{\sin \mathrm{r}} {/tex} From the figure and calculations r 1 + r 2 = A At minimum deviation i.e. {tex}\delta = \delta{/tex} m, i = e and r 1 = r 2 = r {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = {tex}\frac {A} {2}{/tex} ...(eq n 1) From the figure {tex}\delta{/tex} = (i - r 1 ) + (e - r 2 ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\delta_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} = (i + e) + (r 1 + r 2 ) {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}{/tex} ... (eq n 2) {tex}\therefore n_{21}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\operatorname{Sin} \frac{A+\delta_m}{2}}{\sin \frac{A}2}{/tex}
solution_html:
2. Since {tex} \mathrm{n}_{21}=\frac{\sin \mathrm{i}}{\sin \mathrm{r}} {/tex}
    From the figure and calculations
    r1 + r2 = A
    At minimum deviation i.e. {tex}\delta = \delta{/tex}m, i = e and r1 = r2 = r
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = {tex}\frac {A} {2}{/tex} ...(eqn 1)

question_html:
1. Using the ray diagram for a system of two lenses of focal lengths f1 and f2 in contact with each other, show that the two lens system can be regarded as equivalent to a single lens of focal length f, where
    {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_2}{/tex}.
    Also write the relation for the equivalent power of the lens combination.
  2. Determine the position of the image formed by the lens combination given in the figure.
question_text: Using the ray diagram for a system of two lenses of focal lengths f 1 and f 2 in contact with each other, show that the two lens system can be regarded as equivalent to a single lens of focal length f, where {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_2}{/tex} . Also write the relation for the equivalent power of the lens combination. Determine the position of the image formed by the lens combination given in the figure.
explanation: For less A {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}=\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} eg. (I) For less B {tex}\frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_1}{/tex} eg.(II) Adding eqn. (i) & eqn. (ii) {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~F}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} equivalent power P = P 2 + P 2 Image formed by lense of f = +10 cm {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}_1}=\frac{1}{f_1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{30}=\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} v 1 = 15 cm This image formed by the lens act as object from concave lens {tex}\therefore{/tex} u 2 = 15 - 5 = 10 cm {tex}\frac{1}{f_2}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}_2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{-10}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{v}=\infty{/tex} Therefore virtual image forms at right of concave lens at {tex}\mathrm{v}=\infty{/tex} and act as convex lens. (f = +30 cm) {tex}\therefore{/tex} u 2 = 15 - 5 = 10 cm {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_3}=\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{f_3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_3}=\frac{1}{\infty}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex} v 3 = 30 cm
solution: For less A {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}=\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} eg. (I) For less B {tex}\frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_1}{/tex} eg.(II) Adding eqn. (i) & eqn. (ii) {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~F}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} equivalent power P = P 2 + P 2 Image formed by lense of f = +10 cm {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}_1}=\frac{1}{f_1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{30}=\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} v 1 = 15 cm This image formed by the lens act as object from concave lens {tex}\therefore{/tex} u 2 = 15 - 5 = 10 cm {tex}\frac{1}{f_2}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}_2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{-10}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{v}=\infty{/tex} Therefore virtual image forms at right of concave lens at {tex}\mathrm{v}=\infty{/tex} and act as convex lens. (f = +30 cm) {tex}\therefore{/tex} u 2 = 15 - 5 = 10 cm {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_3}=\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{f_3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_3}=\frac{1}{\infty}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex} v 3 = 30 cm
solution_html:
1. For less A
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}=\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} eg. (I)
    For less B
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_1}{/tex} eg.(II)
    Adding eqn. (i) & eqn. (ii)
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_1}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~F}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} equivalent power
    P = P2 + P2
  2. Image formed by lense of f = +10 cm

    {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}_1}=\frac{1}{f_1}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{30}=\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} v1 = 15 cm
    This image formed by the lens act as object from concave lens
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} u2 = 15 - 5 = 10 cm
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_2}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}_2}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{-10}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{v}=\infty{/tex}
    Therefore virtual image forms at right of concave lens at {tex}\mathrm{v}=\infty{/tex} and act as convex lens. (f = +30 cm)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} u2 = 15 - 5 = 10 cm
    {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_3}=\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{f_3}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v_3}=\frac{1}{\infty}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex}
    v3 = 30 cm

question_html:
1. Draw a labelled ray diagram of compound microscope, when final image forms at the least distance of distinct vision.
  2. Why is its objective of short focal length and of short aperture, compared to its eyepiece? Explain.
  3. The focal length of the objective is 4 cm while that of eyepiece is 10 cm. The object is placed at a distance of 6 cm from the objective lens.
    1. Calculate the magnifying power of the compound microscope, if its final image is formed at the near point.
    2. Also calculate length of the compound microscope.
question_text: Draw a labelled ray diagram of compound microscope, when final image forms at the least distance of distinct vision. Why is its objective of short focal length and of short aperture, compared to its eyepiece? Explain. The focal length of the objective is 4 cm while that of eyepiece is 10 cm. The object is placed at a distance of 6 cm from the objective lens. Calculate the magnifying power of the compound microscope, if its final image is formed at the near point. Also calculate length of the compound microscope.
explanation: ray diagram The magnifying power of compound microscope {tex}m=m_0 \times m_e=\frac{L}{f_0} \times\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex} To have high magnifying power and high resolution, the focal length of the objective and its aperture should be short. Focal length of eyepiece is comparatively greater than the objective so that image formed by objective lens may form within the focal length of eyepiece and the final magnified image may be formed. Aperture in short for higher resolution u 0 = -6 cm for objective lens {tex}\frac{1}{f_0}=\frac{1}{v_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_0}=\frac{1}{f_0}+\frac{1}{u_0}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{-6}{/tex} v 0 = 12 cm Since for eyepiece v e = -D = -25 cm {tex}\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{v_e}-\frac{1}{f_e}=\frac{1}{-25}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex} u e = -7.14 cm For magnifying power of compound microscope {tex}m=\frac{v_o}{u_o}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex} {tex}m=\frac{12}{6}\left(1+\frac{25}{10}\right)=7{/tex} Length of Microscope L = v 0 + |u e | = 12 + 7.14 L = 19.14 cm
solution: ray diagram The magnifying power of compound microscope {tex}m=m_0 \times m_e=\frac{L}{f_0} \times\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex} To have high magnifying power and high resolution, the focal length of the objective and its aperture should be short. Focal length of eyepiece is comparatively greater than the objective so that image formed by objective lens may form within the focal length of eyepiece and the final magnified image may be formed. Aperture in short for higher resolution u 0 = -6 cm for objective lens {tex}\frac{1}{f_0}=\frac{1}{v_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_0}=\frac{1}{f_0}+\frac{1}{u_0}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{-6}{/tex} v 0 = 12 cm Since for eyepiece v e = -D = -25 cm {tex}\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{v_e}-\frac{1}{f_e}=\frac{1}{-25}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex} u e = -7.14 cm For magnifying power of compound microscope {tex}m=\frac{v_o}{u_o}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex} {tex}m=\frac{12}{6}\left(1+\frac{25}{10}\right)=7{/tex} Length of Microscope L = v 0 + |u e | = 12 + 7.14 L = 19.14 cm
solution_html:
1. ray diagram

    The magnifying power of compound microscope
  2. {tex}m=m_0 \times m_e=\frac{L}{f_0} \times\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex}
    To have high magnifying power and high resolution, the focal length of the objective and its aperture should be short.
    Focal length of eyepiece is comparatively greater than the objective so that image formed by objective lens may form within the focal length of eyepiece and the final magnified image may be formed.
    Aperture in short for higher resolution
  3. u0 = -6 cm
    for objective lens
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_0}=\frac{1}{v_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v_0}=\frac{1}{f_0}+\frac{1}{u_0}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{-6}{/tex}
    v0 = 12 cm
    Since for eyepiece ve = -D = -25 cm
    {tex}\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{v_e}-\frac{1}{f_e}=\frac{1}{-25}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
    ue = -7.14 cm
    1. For magnifying power of compound microscope
      {tex}m=\frac{v_o}{u_o}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex}
      {tex}m=\frac{12}{6}\left(1+\frac{25}{10}\right)=7{/tex}
    2. Length of Microscope
      L = v0 + |ue| = 12 + 7.14
      L = 19.14 cm

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. With the help of a labelled ray diagram, explain the construction and working of a Cassegrain reflecting telescope.
  2. An amateur astronomer wishes to estimate roughly the size of the Sun using his crude telescope consisting of an objective lens of focal length 200 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 10 cm. By adjusting the distance of the eyepiece from the objective, he obtains an image of the Sun on a screen 40 cm behind the eyepiece. The diameter of the Sun’s image is measured to be 6.0 cm. Estimate the Sun’s size, given that the average Earth-Sun distance is 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 m.
question_text: With the help of a labelled ray diagram, explain the construction and working of a Cassegrain reflecting telescope. An amateur astronomer wishes to estimate roughly the size of the Sun using his crude telescope consisting of an objective lens of focal length 200 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 10 cm. By adjusting the distance of the eyepiece from the objective, he obtains an image of the Sun on a screen 40 cm behind the eyepiece. The diameter of the Sun’s image is measured to be 6.0 cm. Estimate the Sun’s size, given that the average Earth-Sun distance is 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 11 m.
explanation: It consists for large concave (primary) paraboidal mirror having in its central part a hole. There is a small convex (secondary) mirror near the focus of concave mirror. Eye pieces if placed near the hole of the concave mirror. The parallel rays from distance object are reflected by the large concave mirror. These rays fall on the convex mirror which reflects these rays outside the hole. The final magnified image in formed. For eyepiece. {tex}\frac{1}{v_e}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{v_e}-\frac{1}{f_e}=\frac{1}{40}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}u_e=\frac{40}{3} \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} Magnification produced by eye pieces is {tex}m_e=\frac{v_e}{\left|u_e\right|}=\frac{40}{\frac{40}3}=3{/tex} Diameter of the image formed by the objective is d = {tex}\frac {6}3{/tex} = 2 cm If D be the diameter of the SUN then the angle subtended by it on the objective will be {tex}\alpha=\frac{D}{1.5 \times 10^{11}} \mathrm{rad}{/tex} Angle subtended by the image at the objective = angle subtended by the SUN {tex}\therefore \alpha=\frac{\text { size of image }}{f_o}=\frac{2}{200}=\frac{1}{100} \mathrm{rad}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \frac{D}{1.5 \times 10^{11}}=\frac{1}{100}{/tex} or D = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 m
solution: It consists for large concave (primary) paraboidal mirror having in its central part a hole. There is a small convex (secondary) mirror near the focus of concave mirror. Eye pieces if placed near the hole of the concave mirror. The parallel rays from distance object are reflected by the large concave mirror. These rays fall on the convex mirror which reflects these rays outside the hole. The final magnified image in formed. For eyepiece. {tex}\frac{1}{v_e}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{v_e}-\frac{1}{f_e}=\frac{1}{40}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}u_e=\frac{40}{3} \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} Magnification produced by eye pieces is {tex}m_e=\frac{v_e}{\left|u_e\right|}=\frac{40}{\frac{40}3}=3{/tex} Diameter of the image formed by the objective is d = {tex}\frac {6}3{/tex} = 2 cm If D be the diameter of the SUN then the angle subtended by it on the objective will be {tex}\alpha=\frac{D}{1.5 \times 10^{11}} \mathrm{rad}{/tex} Angle subtended by the image at the objective = angle subtended by the SUN {tex}\therefore \alpha=\frac{\text { size of image }}{f_o}=\frac{2}{200}=\frac{1}{100} \mathrm{rad}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \frac{D}{1.5 \times 10^{11}}=\frac{1}{100}{/tex} or D = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 m
solution_html:
1. It consists for large concave (primary) paraboidal mirror having in its central part a hole. There is a small convex (secondary) mirror near the focus of concave mirror. Eye pieces if placed near the hole of the concave mirror.
    The parallel rays from distance object are reflected by the large concave mirror. These rays fall on the convex mirror which reflects these rays outside the hole. The final magnified image in formed.
  2. For eyepiece.
    {tex}\frac{1}{v_e}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e}{/tex}
    or {tex}\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{v_e}-\frac{1}{f_e}=\frac{1}{40}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
    {tex}u_e=\frac{40}{3} \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
    Magnification produced by eye pieces is
    {tex}m_e=\frac{v_e}{\left|u_e\right|}=\frac{40}{\frac{40}3}=3{/tex}
    Diameter of the image formed by the objective is
    d = {tex}\frac {6}3{/tex} = 2 cm
    If D be the diameter of the SUN then the angle subtended by it on the objective will be
    {tex}\alpha=\frac{D}{1.5 \times 10^{11}} \mathrm{rad}{/tex}
    Angle subtended by the image at the objective
    = angle subtended by the SUN
    {tex}\therefore \alpha=\frac{\text { size of image }}{f_o}=\frac{2}{200}=\frac{1}{100} \mathrm{rad}{/tex}.
    {tex}\therefore \frac{D}{1.5 \times 10^{11}}=\frac{1}{100}{/tex}
    or
    D = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 m

question_html: A ray of light incident on the face AB of an isosceles triangular prism makes an angle of incidence (i) and deviates by angle {tex}\beta{/tex} as shown in the figure. Show that in the position of minimum deviation {tex}\angle \beta=\angle \alpha{/tex}. Also find out the condition when the refracted ray QR suffers total internal reflection.
question_text: A ray of light incident on the face AB of an isosceles triangular prism makes an angle of incidence (i) and deviates by angle {tex}\beta{/tex} as shown in the figure. Show that in the position of minimum deviation {tex}\angle \beta=\angle \alpha{/tex} . Also find out the condition when the refracted ray QR suffers total internal reflection.
explanation: For minimum deviation r 1 + r 2 = A ; r 1 = r 2 (90 - {tex}\beta{/tex} ) + (90 - {tex}\beta{/tex} ) = A 180 - 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} = A 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} = 180 - A 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} = 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\alpha{/tex} r 1 + r 2 = A r 1 + i c = A i c = A - r 1 i c = A - (90 - {tex}\beta{/tex} )
solution: For minimum deviation r 1 + r 2 = A ; r 1 = r 2 (90 - {tex}\beta{/tex} ) + (90 - {tex}\beta{/tex} ) = A 180 - 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} = A 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} = 180 - A 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} = 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\alpha{/tex} r 1 + r 2 = A r 1 + i c = A i c = A - r 1 i c = A - (90 - {tex}\beta{/tex} )
solution_html:
For minimum deviation
r1 + r2 = A ; r1 = r2
(90 - {tex}\beta{/tex}) + (90 - {tex}\beta{/tex}) = A
180 - 2{tex}\beta{/tex} = A
2{tex}\beta{/tex} = 180 - A
2{tex}\beta{/tex} = 2{tex}\alpha{/tex}
{tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\alpha{/tex}
r1 + r2 = A
r1 + ic = A
ic = A - r1
ic = A - (90 - {tex}\beta{/tex})

question_html: A point O marked on the surface of a glass sphere of diameter 20 cm is viewed through glass from the position directly opposite to the point O. If the refractive index of the glass is 1.5, find the position of the image formed. Also, draw the ray diagram for the formation of the image.
question_text: A point O marked on the surface of a glass sphere of diameter 20 cm is viewed through glass from the position directly opposite to the point O. If the refractive index of the glass is 1.5, find the position of the image formed. Also, draw the ray diagram for the formation of the image.
explanation: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}_1}{-\mathrm{u}}+\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2-\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1.5}{20}+\frac{1}{V}=\frac{1-1.5}{-10}{/tex} v = -40 cm
solution: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}_1}{-\mathrm{u}}+\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2-\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1.5}{20}+\frac{1}{V}=\frac{1-1.5}{-10}{/tex} v = -40 cm
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}_1}{-\mathrm{u}}+\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2-\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{R}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1.5}{20}+\frac{1}{V}=\frac{1-1.5}{-10}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Show that the image formed by a convex mirror of an object is always virtual.
question_text: Show that the image formed by a convex mirror of an object is always virtual.
explanation: Since rays diverge it is a virtual image Alternatively {tex}\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v}{f}=1+\frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{m}=\frac{-v}{u}=1-\frac{v}{u}{/tex} since {tex}V<\mathrm{f}{/tex} always m is positive and less than 1 So, the image is always virtual
solution: Since rays diverge it is a virtual image Alternatively {tex}\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v}{f}=1+\frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{m}=\frac{-v}{u}=1-\frac{v}{u}{/tex} since {tex}V<\mathrm{f}{/tex} always m is positive and less than 1 So, the image is always virtual
solution_html:
Since rays diverge it is a virtual image
Alternatively
{tex}\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{v}{f}=1+\frac{v}{u}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{m}=\frac{-v}{u}=1-\frac{v}{u}{/tex} since {tex}V<\mathrm{f}{/tex} always m is positive and less than 1
So, the image is always virtual

question_html: A converging beam of light travelling in air converges at a point P as shown in the figure. When a glass sphere of refractive index 1.5 is introduced in between the path of the beam, calculate the new position of the image. Also draw the ray diagram for the image formed.
question_text: A converging beam of light travelling in air converges at a point P as shown in the figure. When a glass sphere of refractive index 1.5 is introduced in between the path of the beam, calculate the new position of the image. Also draw the ray diagram for the image formed.
explanation: u = 20 cm, n 2 = 1.5, n 1 = 1, R = 5 cm Using {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{v}-\frac{\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2-\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1.5}{v}-\frac{1}{20}=\frac{1.5-1}{5}{/tex} v = 10 cm For the 2nd refraction u = 0 then, {tex}\frac{1}{y}-\frac{1.5}{0}=\frac{1-1.5}{-5}{/tex} v = 0 So, the final image will be a pole of the surface 2.
solution: u = 20 cm, n 2 = 1.5, n 1 = 1, R = 5 cm Using {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{v}-\frac{\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2-\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1.5}{v}-\frac{1}{20}=\frac{1.5-1}{5}{/tex} v = 10 cm For the 2nd refraction u = 0 then, {tex}\frac{1}{y}-\frac{1.5}{0}=\frac{1-1.5}{-5}{/tex} v = 0 So, the final image will be a pole of the surface 2.
solution_html:
u = 20 cm, n2 = 1.5, n1 = 1, R = 5 cm
Using {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{v}-\frac{\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2-\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{R}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1.5}{v}-\frac{1}{20}=\frac{1.5-1}{5}{/tex}
v = 10 cm
For the 2nd refraction
u = 0
then,
{tex}\frac{1}{y}-\frac{1.5}{0}=\frac{1-1.5}{-5}{/tex}
v = 0
So, the final image will be a pole of the surface 2.

subject: Physics
question_html: Draw a ray diagram to show the formation of a virtual image of an object by a convex lens. Using this diagram, obtain the expressions for the lens formula.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram to show the formation of a virtual image of an object by a convex lens. Using this diagram, obtain the expressions for the lens formula.
explanation: {tex}\Delta A^{\prime} B^{\prime} P \sim \triangle A B P{/tex} {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Delta A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F \sim \triangle M P F{/tex} {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{M P}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{P F}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{P F}{/tex} ...(ii) From (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{P F}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{-v}{-u}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P F}{P F}=1+\frac{B^{\prime} P}{P F}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{v}{u}=1-\frac{v}{f}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\Delta A^{\prime} B^{\prime} P \sim \triangle A B P{/tex} {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Delta A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F \sim \triangle M P F{/tex} {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{M P}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{P F}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{P F}{/tex} ...(ii) From (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{P F}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{-v}{-u}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P F}{P F}=1+\frac{B^{\prime} P}{P F}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{v}{u}=1-\frac{v}{f}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\Delta A^{\prime} B^{\prime} P \sim \triangle A B P{/tex}
{tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\Delta A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F \sim \triangle M P F{/tex}
{tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{M P}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{P F}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{P F}{/tex} ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii)
{tex}\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{P F}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{-v}{-u}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P F}{P F}=1+\frac{B^{\prime} P}{P F}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{v}{u}=1-\frac{v}{f}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Draw a ray diagram to show the formation of an erect image of an object kept in front of a concave mirror. Hence deduce the mirror formula.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram to show the formation of an erect image of an object kept in front of a concave mirror. Hence deduce the mirror formula.
explanation: {tex}A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F \sim \triangle M P F{/tex} {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{M P}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{P F}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P F}{P F}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P F}{P F}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Delta A^{\prime} B^{\prime} C \sim \triangle A B C{/tex} {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} C}{B C}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P C}{P C-P B}{/tex} ...(ii) or {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} P+P F}{P F}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P C}{P C-P B}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{v-f}{-f}=\frac{v-2 f}{-2 f+u}{/tex} Cross multiply and divide by uvf: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
solution: {tex}A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F \sim \triangle M P F{/tex} {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{M P}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{P F}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P F}{P F}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P F}{P F}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Delta A^{\prime} B^{\prime} C \sim \triangle A B C{/tex} {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} C}{B C}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P C}{P C-P B}{/tex} ...(ii) or {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} P+P F}{P F}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P C}{P C-P B}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{v-f}{-f}=\frac{v-2 f}{-2 f+u}{/tex} Cross multiply and divide by uvf: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}A^{\prime} B^{\prime} F \sim \triangle M P F{/tex}
{tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{M P}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{P F}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P F}{P F}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P F}{P F}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\Delta A^{\prime} B^{\prime} C \sim \triangle A B C{/tex}
{tex}\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B}=\frac{B^{\prime} C}{B C}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P C}{P C-P B}{/tex} ...(ii)
or {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} P+P F}{P F}=\frac{B^{\prime} P+P C}{P C-P B}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{v-f}{-f}=\frac{v-2 f}{-2 f+u}{/tex}
Cross multiply and divide by uvf:
{tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Explain the working of a refracting telescope with the help of a labelled diagram. What are the main limitations of this type of telescope and how are these overcome in a reflecting telescope?
question_text: Explain the working of a refracting telescope with the help of a labelled diagram. What are the main limitations of this type of telescope and how are these overcome in a reflecting telescope?
explanation: Working The objective forms a real image of a distant object at its second focal point. The eyepiece magnifies this image producing a final inverted image. Limitations It needs large sized lenses which are expensive and very heavy, difficult to make and tend to have chromatic and spherical aberrations and distortions. Reflecting telescopes Reflecting telescopes can overcome these limitations because the mirrors used in them. are free from chromatic aberration and can have very little spherical aberration. are less heavy and easier to support.
solution: Working The objective forms a real image of a distant object at its second focal point. The eyepiece magnifies this image producing a final inverted image. Limitations It needs large sized lenses which are expensive and very heavy, difficult to make and tend to have chromatic and spherical aberrations and distortions. Reflecting telescopes Reflecting telescopes can overcome these limitations because the mirrors used in them. are free from chromatic aberration and can have very little spherical aberration. are less heavy and easier to support.
solution_html:
Working
The objective forms a real image of a distant object at its second focal point. The eyepiece magnifies this image producing a final inverted image.
Limitations
It needs large sized lenses which are expensive and very heavy, difficult to make and tend to have chromatic and spherical aberrations and distortions.
Reflecting telescopes
Reflecting telescopes can overcome these limitations because the mirrors used in them.

  1. are free from chromatic aberration and can have very little spherical aberration.
  2. are less heavy and easier to support.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Define the term power of a lens. Write its SI unit.
  2. Derive the expression for the power of two thin lenses placed coaxially in contact with each other.
question_text: Define the term power of a lens. Write its SI unit. Derive the expression for the power of two thin lenses placed coaxially in contact with each other.
explanation: The power of a lens is a measure of its ability to converge or diverge a given beam of light incident on it. The SI unit of power is the diopter (D) Derivation For the first lens: {tex}\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}{/tex} For the second lens: {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_1}=\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} Adding, we get {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The equivalent focal length f, of the combination is given by {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} In terms of power , the relation becomes P = P 1 + P 2 Thus the power of two this lens, placed co-axially in contact with each other is the algebraic sum of their individual powers.
solution: The power of a lens is a measure of its ability to converge or diverge a given beam of light incident on it. The SI unit of power is the diopter (D) Derivation For the first lens: {tex}\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}{/tex} For the second lens: {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_1}=\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} Adding, we get {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The equivalent focal length f, of the combination is given by {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} In terms of power , the relation becomes P = P 1 + P 2 Thus the power of two this lens, placed co-axially in contact with each other is the algebraic sum of their individual powers.
solution_html:
1. The power of a lens is a measure of its ability to converge or diverge a given beam of light incident on it.
    The SI unit of power is the diopter (D)
  2. Derivation

    For the first lens:
    {tex}\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}{/tex}
    For the second lens:
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_1}=\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex}
    Adding, we get
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The equivalent focal length f, of the combination is given by
    {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex}
    In terms of power , the relation becomes
    P = P1 + P2
    Thus the power of two this lens, placed co-axially in contact with each other is the algebraic sum of their individual powers.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Explain the working of a compound microscope with the help of a labelled diagram.
  2. Write the considerations that you keep in mind, while choosing lenses to be used as eyepiece and objective in a compound microscope.
question_text: Explain the working of a compound microscope with the help of a labelled diagram. Write the considerations that you keep in mind, while choosing lenses to be used as eyepiece and objective in a compound microscope.
explanation: Working: When object is placed just beyond the focus of objective lens, the objective forms a real and highly magnified image of the object. This image is formed at the focus of the eye piece or at a point whose distance from the eye piece is less than the focal length of the eye piece. The eye piece then forms a (virtual) magnified image of the image formed by the objective. Both the objective and the eye piece must have short focal lengths for high magnification.
solution: Working: When object is placed just beyond the focus of objective lens, the objective forms a real and highly magnified image of the object. This image is formed at the focus of the eye piece or at a point whose distance from the eye piece is less than the focal length of the eye piece. The eye piece then forms a (virtual) magnified image of the image formed by the objective. Both the objective and the eye piece must have short focal lengths for high magnification.
solution_html:
1. Working: When object is placed just beyond the focus of objective lens, the objective forms a real and highly magnified image of the object. This image is formed at the focus of the eye piece or at a point whose distance from the eye piece is less than the focal length of the eye piece. The eye piece then forms a (virtual) magnified image of the image formed by the objective.
  2. Both the objective and the eye piece must have short focal lengths for high magnification.

question_html: Out of the two optical instruments, a microscope and a telescope, which one plays the role in magnifying the objects and in resolving the two objects kept close to each other? Explain, giving example.
question_text: Out of the two optical instruments, a microscope and a telescope, which one plays the role in magnifying the objects and in resolving the two objects kept close to each other? Explain, giving example.
explanation: Microscope is used for magnifying the object Telescope is used for resolving the two distant objects kept close to each other Example Micro-organisms are magnified by the microscopes for their visibility. Celestial bodies/Distant objects are distinguished by telescope.
solution: Microscope is used for magnifying the object Telescope is used for resolving the two distant objects kept close to each other Example Micro-organisms are magnified by the microscopes for their visibility. Celestial bodies/Distant objects are distinguished by telescope.
solution_html:
Microscope is used for magnifying the object
Telescope is used for resolving the two distant objects kept close to each other
Example
Micro-organisms are magnified by the microscopes for their visibility. Celestial bodies/Distant objects are distinguished by telescope.

question_html: A triangular prism of refracting angle 60o is made of a transparent material of refractive index {tex}\frac {2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}. A ray of light is incident normally on the face KL as shown in the figure. Trace the path of the ray as it passes through the prism and calculate the angle of emergence and angle of deviation.
question_text: A triangular prism of refracting angle 60 o is made of a transparent material of refractive index {tex}\frac {2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} . A ray of light is incident normally on the face KL as shown in the figure. Trace the path of the ray as it passes through the prism and calculate the angle of emergence and angle of deviation.
explanation: When light ray incident on face KL, it is pass undeviated, because it is normal to the surface and incident on face KM. The angle of incidence for face KM is equal to 60°. {tex}\begin{aligned} & \frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin r}=\frac{n_2}{n_1} \\ & \frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin r}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{aligned} \quad\left[\begin{array}{l} n_2=\text { Second medium }=\text { air } \\ n_1=\text { Glass medium }=\frac 2 {\sqrt{3}} \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin r=\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}=1{/tex} sin r = 1 r = 90 o Angle of emergence = 90 o Angle of deviation = 30 o
solution: When light ray incident on face KL, it is pass undeviated, because it is normal to the surface and incident on face KM. The angle of incidence for face KM is equal to 60°. {tex}\begin{aligned} & \frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin r}=\frac{n_2}{n_1} \\ & \frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin r}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{aligned} \quad\left[\begin{array}{l} n_2=\text { Second medium }=\text { air } \\ n_1=\text { Glass medium }=\frac 2 {\sqrt{3}} \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin r=\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}=1{/tex} sin r = 1 r = 90 o Angle of emergence = 90 o Angle of deviation = 30 o
solution_html:
When light ray incident on face KL, it is pass undeviated, because it is normal to the surface and incident on face KM. The angle of incidence for face KM is equal to 60°.

{tex}\begin{aligned} & \frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin r}=\frac{n_2}{n_1} \\ & \frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin r}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{aligned} \quad\left[\begin{array}{l} n_2=\text { Second medium }=\text { air } \\ n_1=\text { Glass medium }=\frac 2 {\sqrt{3}} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin r=\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}=1{/tex}
sin r = 1
r = 90o
Angle of emergence = 90o
Angle of deviation = 30o

question_html: Find the position of the image formed by the lens combination given in figure.
question_text: Find the position of the image formed by the lens combination given in figure.
explanation: For 1st lens, u 1 = -30 cm, f 1 = +10 cm, v 1 = ? As {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{u_{1}}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{-30}=\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{30}=\frac{1}{15}{/tex} , v 1 = 15 cm An image formed by the first lens serves as an object for the second lens at a distance = (15 - 5) = 10 cm to the right of the second lens, and the object is virtual. Therefore, for the second lens, u 2 = 10 cm, v 2 = ?, f 2 = - 10 cm As {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{u_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{10}=\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_{2}}{/tex} = 0, v 2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} The virtual image is formed at infinity to the left of the 2nd lens. This acts as an object for a 3rd lens. Therefore, for 3rd lens, u 3 = {tex}\infty{/tex} , v 3 = ? f 3 = +30 cm As {tex}\frac{1}{v_{3}}-\frac{1}{u_{3}}=\frac{1}{f_{3}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_{3}}-\frac{1}{\infty}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex} or v 3 = 30 cm The final image is formed at 30 cm to the right of the third lens.
solution: For 1st lens, u 1 = -30 cm, f 1 = +10 cm, v 1 = ? Therefore, for the second lens, u 2 = 10 cm, v 2 = ?, f 2 = - 10 cm As {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{u_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{10}=\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_{2}}{/tex} = 0, v 2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} The virtual image is formed at infinity to the left of the 2nd lens. This acts as an object for a 3rd lens. Therefore, for 3rd lens, u 3 = {tex}\infty{/tex} , v 3 = ? f 3 = +30 cm As {tex}\frac{1}{v_{3}}-\frac{1}{u_{3}}=\frac{1}{f_{3}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_{3}}-\frac{1}{\infty}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex} or v 3 = 30 cm The final image is formed at 30 cm to the right of the third lens.
solution_html:
For 1st lens,
u1 = -30 cm, f1 = +10 cm, v1 = ?
As {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{u_{1}}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{-30}=\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{30}=\frac{1}{15}{/tex}, v1 = 15 cm
An image formed by the first lens serves as an object for the second lens at a distance = (15 - 5) = 10 cm to the right of the second lens, and the object is virtual. Therefore, for the second lens,
u2 = 10 cm, v2 = ?, f2 = - 10 cm
As {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{u_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{10}=\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_{2}}{/tex} = 0, v2 = {tex}\infty{/tex}
The virtual image is formed at infinity to the left of the 2nd lens. This acts as an object for a 3rd lens. Therefore, for 3rd lens,
u3 = {tex}\infty{/tex}, v3 = ?
f3 = +30 cm
As {tex}\frac{1}{v_{3}}-\frac{1}{u_{3}}=\frac{1}{f_{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_{3}}-\frac{1}{\infty}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex} or v3 = 30 cm
The final image is formed at 30 cm to the right of the third lens.

question_html: A symmetric biconvex lens of radius of curvature R and made of glass of refractive index 1.5, is placed on a layer of liquid placed on top of a plane mirror as shown in the figure. An optical needle with its tip on the principal axis of the lens is moved along the axis until its real, inverted image coincides with the needle itself. The distance of the needle from the lens is measured to be x. On removing the liquid layer and repeating the experiment, the distance is found to be y. Obtain the expression for the refractive index of the liquid in terms of x and y.
question_text: A symmetric biconvex lens of radius of curvature R and made of glass of refractive index 1.5, is placed on a layer of liquid placed on top of a plane mirror as shown in the figure. An optical needle with its tip on the principal axis of the lens is moved along the axis until its real, inverted image coincides with the needle itself. The distance of the needle from the lens is measured to be x. On removing the liquid layer and repeating the experiment, the distance is found to be y. Obtain the expression for the refractive index of the liquid in terms of x and y.
explanation: Given, Radius of curvature of the convex is R and {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 When lens is placed on a layer of water then focal length of the combination is f. Thus the first measurement gives the focal length of combination i.e. f = x. In the second measurement, we get the focal length f 1 of convex lens i.e. f 1 = y, therefore the focal length of Plano convex lens of water f 2 is given as {tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{f_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{y}=\frac{y-x}{x y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f 2 = {tex}\frac{x y}{y-x}{/tex} ...(i) For plano convex lens of liquid of refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} , we have {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} = ( {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex} = {tex}(\mu-1)\left(-\frac{1}{R}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} = - {tex}\frac{(\mu-1)}{R}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{R}{f_{2}}{/tex} ...(ii) Now from equation (i) and (ii), {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{R}{x y / y-x}{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{R(y-x)}{x y}{/tex} ....(iii) Now again for the plano convex lens of air, if image coincide at 2f 1 then for case II we have, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2f 1 = R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f 1 = {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex} Again y = 2f 1 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = R ....(iv) {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{y(y-x)}{x y}{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{y-x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x-y+x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x-y}{x}{/tex} This is the required expression for the refractive index of the liquid.
solution: Given, Radius of curvature of the convex is R and {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 When lens is placed on a layer of water then focal length of the combination is f. Thus the first measurement gives the focal length of combination i.e. f = x. In the second measurement, we get the focal length f 1 of convex lens i.e. f 1 = y, therefore the focal length of Plano convex lens of water f 2 is given as {tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{f_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{y}=\frac{y-x}{x y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f 2 = {tex}\frac{x y}{y-x}{/tex} ...(i) For plano convex lens of liquid of refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} , we have {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} = ( {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex} = {tex}(\mu-1)\left(-\frac{1}{R}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} = - {tex}\frac{(\mu-1)}{R}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{R}{f_{2}}{/tex} ...(ii) Now from equation (i) and (ii), {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{R}{x y / y-x}{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{R(y-x)}{x y}{/tex} ....(iii) Now again for the plano convex lens of air, if image coincide at 2f 1 then for case II we have, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2f 1 = R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f 1 = {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex} Again y = 2f 1 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = R ....(iv) {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{y(y-x)}{x y}{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{y-x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x-y+x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x-y}{x}{/tex} This is the required expression for the refractive index of the liquid.
solution_html:
Given, Radius of curvature of the convex is R and {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5
When lens is placed on a layer of water then focal length of the combination is f. Thus the first measurement gives the focal length of combination i.e. f = x.

In the second measurement, we get the focal length f1 of convex lens i.e. f1 = y, therefore the focal length of Plano convex lens of water f2 is given as
{tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{f_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{y}=\frac{y-x}{x y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f2 = {tex}\frac{x y}{y-x}{/tex} ...(i)
For plano convex lens of liquid of refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex}, we have
{tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} = ({tex}\mu{/tex} - 1){tex}\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex}= {tex}(\mu-1)\left(-\frac{1}{R}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} = -{tex}\frac{(\mu-1)}{R}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{R}{f_{2}}{/tex} ...(ii)
Now from equation (i) and (ii),
{tex}\mu{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{R}{x y / y-x}{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{R(y-x)}{x y}{/tex} ....(iii)
Now again for the plano convex lens of air, if image coincide at 2f1 then for case II we have,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2f1 = R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f1 =  {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex}
Again y = 2f1 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = R ....(iv)
{tex}\mu{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{y(y-x)}{x y}{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{y-x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x-y+x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x-y}{x}{/tex}
This is the required expression for the refractive index of the liquid.

(i) Similarly, {tex}\Delta \mathrm{MNF}{/tex} and {tex}\Delta \mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{F}{/tex} are similarly {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{M N}{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}=\frac{N F}{F A^{\prime}}{/tex} ..... (ii) As MN = BA FA' = PA' - PF {tex}\therefore{/tex} equation (ii) takes the following from {tex}\frac{B A}{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}=\frac{P {F}}{P A^{\prime}-P F}{/tex} Using equation (i) and (iii) {tex}\frac{P A}{P A^{\prime}}=\frac{P F}{P A^{\prime}-P F}{/tex} For the given figure, asper the sign convention, PA = -u PA' = -v PF = -f {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-u}{-v}=\frac{-f}{-v-(-f)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} Linear magnification = {tex}\frac{{ - v}}{u}{/tex} (alternatively {tex}m=\frac{h_{i}}{h_{0}}{/tex} ) Advantages of reflecting telescope over refracting telescope Mechanical support is easier Magnifying power is large Resolving power is large Spherical aberration is reduced Free from chromatic aberration (any two)

question_html:
1.  With the help of a ray diagram, show how a concave mirror is used to obtain an erect and magnified image of an object.
  2. Using the above ray diagram, obtain the mirror formula and the expression for linear magnification.
question_text: With the help of a ray diagram, show how a concave mirror is used to obtain an erect and magnified image of an object. Using the above ray diagram, obtain the mirror formula and the expression for linear magnification.
explanation: Ray Diagram: From similar triangles A'B'F and MPF, we have {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{P M}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} (Since PM = BA) From similar triangles A'B'P and ABP, we have {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} Now, B'F = B'P + PF = (+ v) + (- f ) = v - f and BP = - u {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{v-f}{-f}=\frac{+v}{-u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{-v}{f}+1=\frac{-v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} This is the mirror formula. Linear magnification is given by = {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}{/tex} From similar triangle A'B'P' and ABP, we get {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Linear magnification is given by:- {tex}m=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}=\frac{+v}{-u}=-\frac{v}{u}{/tex}

Linear magnification is given by = {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}{/tex} From similar triangle A'B'P' and ABP, we get {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Linear magnification is given by:- {tex}m=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}=\frac{+v}{-u}=-\frac{v}{u}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Ray Diagram:
  2. From similar triangles A'B'F and MPF, we have

    From similar triangles A'B'P and ABP, we have
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex}
    Hence, {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex}
    Now, B'F = B'P + PF
    = (+ v) + (- f )
    = v - f
    and  BP = - u
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{v-f}{-f}=\frac{+v}{-u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{-v}{f}+1=\frac{-v}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    This is the mirror formula.

    From similar triangle A'B'P' and ABP, we get

question_html:
1. Show using a proper diagram of how unpolarised light can be linearly polarized by reflection from a transparent glass surface.
  2. The figure shows a ray of light falling normally on the face AB of an equilateral glass prism having a refractive index {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} placed in water of refractive index {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}. Will this ray suffer total internal 3 reflection on striking the face AC? Justify your answer.
question_text: Show using a proper diagram of how unpolarised light can be linearly polarized by reflection from a transparent glass surface. The figure shows a ray of light falling normally on the face AB of an equilateral glass prism having a refractive index {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} placed in water of refractive index {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} . Will this ray suffer total internal 3 reflection on striking the face AC? Justify your answer.
explanation: The diagram, showing polarization by reflection is as shown. [Here the reflected and refracted rays are at right angle to each other.]It is possible to transform unpolarised light in the polarized light. Polarized light waves ore light waves in which the vibration occur in a single plane, the process of transforming unpolarised into polarized is know as polarization . Brewster angle of incident at which light with a particular polarization is perfectly transmitted through a transparent dielectric is perfectly transmitted through a transparent dielectric surface. With no reflection when unpolarised light is incident at this angle, the light that is reflected from the surface is polarized. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}r=\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-i_{B}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu=\left(\frac{\sin i_{B}}{\sin r}=\tan i_{B}\right){/tex} Thus light gets totally polarized by reflection when it is incident at an angle i B (Brewster's angle), where {tex}i_{\mathrm{B}}=\tan ^{-1} \mu{/tex} The angle of incidence, of the ray, on striking the face AC is i = 60° (as from figure) Also, relative refractive index of glass, with respect to the surrounding water, is {tex}\mu_{r}=\frac{\frac{3}{2}}{\frac{4}{3}}=\frac{9}{8}{/tex} Also sin i = sin 60 o = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{1.732}{2}{/tex} = 0.866 For total internal reflection, the required critical angle, in this case, is given by {tex}\sin i_{c}=\frac{1}{\mu}=\frac{8}{9}=0.89{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} i < i c Hence the ray would not suffer total internal reflection on striking the face AC
solution: The diagram, showing polarization by reflection is as shown. [Here the reflected and refracted rays are at right angle to each other.]It is possible to transform unpolarised light in the polarized light. Polarized light waves ore light waves in which the vibration occur in a single plane, the process of transforming unpolarised into polarized is know as polarization . Brewster angle of incident at which light with a particular polarization is perfectly transmitted through a transparent dielectric is perfectly transmitted through a transparent dielectric surface. With no reflection when unpolarised light is incident at this angle, the light that is reflected from the surface is polarized. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}r=\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-i_{B}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu=\left(\frac{\sin i_{B}}{\sin r}=\tan i_{B}\right){/tex} Thus light gets totally polarized by reflection when it is incident at an angle i B (Brewster's angle), where {tex}i_{\mathrm{B}}=\tan ^{-1} \mu{/tex} The angle of incidence, of the ray, on striking the face AC is i = 60° (as from figure) Also, relative refractive index of glass, with respect to the surrounding water, is {tex}\mu_{r}=\frac{\frac{3}{2}}{\frac{4}{3}}=\frac{9}{8}{/tex} Also sin i = sin 60 o = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{1.732}{2}{/tex} = 0.866 For total internal reflection, the required critical angle, in this case, is given by {tex}\sin i_{c}=\frac{1}{\mu}=\frac{8}{9}=0.89{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} i < i c Hence the ray would not suffer total internal reflection on striking the face AC
solution_html:
1. The diagram, showing polarization by reflection is as shown. [Here the reflected and refracted rays are at right angle to each other.]It is possible to transform unpolarised light in the polarized light. Polarized light waves ore light waves in which the vibration occur in a single plane, the process of transforming unpolarised into polarized is know as polarization .
    Brewster angle of incident at which light with a particular polarization is perfectly transmitted through a transparent dielectric is perfectly transmitted through a transparent dielectric surface. With no reflection when unpolarised light is incident at this angle, the light that is reflected from the surface is polarized.

    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}r=\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-i_{B}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu=\left(\frac{\sin i_{B}}{\sin r}=\tan i_{B}\right){/tex}
    Thus light gets totally polarized by reflection when it is incident at an angle iB (Brewster's angle), where{tex}i_{\mathrm{B}}=\tan ^{-1} \mu{/tex}
  2. The angle of incidence, of the ray, on striking the face AC is i = 60° (as from figure)
    Also, relative refractive index of glass, with respect to the surrounding water, is 

    {tex}\mu_{r}=\frac{\frac{3}{2}}{\frac{4}{3}}=\frac{9}{8}{/tex}  
    Also sin i = sin 60o = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{1.732}{2}{/tex}
    = 0.866
    For total internal reflection, the required critical angle, in this case, is given by
    {tex}\sin i_{c}=\frac{1}{\mu}=\frac{8}{9}=0.89{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex}  i < ic
    Hence the ray would not suffer total internal reflection on striking the face AC
