1. Prove that for a concave mirror, the radius of curvature is twice the focal length for small aperture as compare to its radius of curvature.
  2. With the help of a suitable diagram, derive the mirror formula for a concave mirror.
question_text: Prove that for a concave mirror, the radius of curvature is twice the focal length for small aperture as compare to its radius of curvature. With the help of a suitable diagram, derive the mirror formula for a concave mirror.
explanation: Let us consider MM' as small aperture concave mirror. The line through centre of curvature MC is perpendicular to the mirror. Applying law of reflection, {tex}\angle O M C=\angle C M F{/tex} ( angle of incidence = angle of reflection) {tex}\angle M C F=\angle O M C{/tex} (alternate angles) Hence {tex}\angle M C F=\angle C M F{/tex} So, CF = MF Since aperture of mirror is very small, D is very near to P and we can put CF = FP PC = 2PF R = 2f Or, {tex}f=\frac{R}{2}{/tex} Triangle A 'B' F and MPF are similar Therefore, {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{P M}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} PM = AB) ...(1) Since < APB = < A'PB, the right-angled triangle A'B'P and ABP are also similar, Therefore, {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(2) From equation (1) and (2) {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P-F P}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(3) Apply the sign convention That is B'P = -V FP = -f BP = -u using the relation equation (3) become {tex}\frac{-v+f}{-f}=\frac{-v}{-u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v-f}{f}=\frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} This relation is known as mirror equation where, v = image distance u = object distances f = focal length
solution: Let us consider MM' as small aperture concave mirror. The line through centre of curvature MC is perpendicular to the mirror. Applying law of reflection, {tex}\angle O M C=\angle C M F{/tex} ( angle of incidence = angle of reflection) {tex}\angle M C F=\angle O M C{/tex} (alternate angles) Hence {tex}\angle M C F=\angle C M F{/tex} So, CF = MF Since aperture of mirror is very small, D is very near to P and we can put CF = FP PC = 2PF R = 2f Or, {tex}f=\frac{R}{2}{/tex} Triangle A 'B' F and MPF are similar Therefore, {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{P M}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} PM = AB) ...(1) Since < APB = < A'PB, the right-angled triangle A'B'P and ABP are also similar, Therefore, {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(2) From equation (1) and (2) {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P-F P}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(3) Apply the sign convention That is B'P = -V FP = -f BP = -u using the relation equation (3) become {tex}\frac{-v+f}{-f}=\frac{-v}{-u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v-f}{f}=\frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} This relation is known as mirror equation where, v = image distance u = object distances f = focal length
solution_html:
1. Let us consider MM' as small aperture concave mirror. The line through centre of curvature MC is perpendicular to the mirror. Applying law of reflection,
    {tex}\angle O M C=\angle C M F{/tex}
    ( angle of incidence = angle of reflection)
    {tex}\angle M C F=\angle O M C{/tex} (alternate angles)
    Hence {tex}\angle M C F=\angle C M F{/tex}
    So, CF = MF
    Since aperture of mirror is very small, D is very near to P and we can put
    CF = FP
    PC = 2PF
    R = 2f
    Or, {tex}f=\frac{R}{2}{/tex}


  2. Triangle A 'B' F and MPF are similar
    Therefore,
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{P M}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex}
    or {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} PM = AB) ...(1)
    Since < APB = < A'PB, the right-angled triangle A'B'P and ABP are also similar, Therefore,
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(2)
    From equation (1) and (2)
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P-F P}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(3)
    Apply the sign convention
    That is B'P = -V
    FP = -f
    BP = -u
    using the relation equation (3) become
    {tex}\frac{-v+f}{-f}=\frac{-v}{-u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{v-f}{f}=\frac{v}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    This relation is known as mirror equation
    where, v = image distance
    u = object distances
    f = focal length

subject: Physics
question_html: Why should the objective of a telescope have a large focal length and large aperture? Justify your answer.
question_text: Why should the objective of a telescope have a large focal length and large aperture? Justify your answer.
explanation: Large focal length : to increase magnifying power {tex}\left(\because m=\frac{f_{c}}{f_{e}}\right){/tex} Large aperture : to increase resolving power So, it can distinguish two very close points on the surface of a planet distinctly. {tex}\left(\because R P=\frac{2 a}{1.22 \lambda}\right){/tex}
solution: Large focal length : to increase magnifying power {tex}\left(\because m=\frac{f_{c}}{f_{e}}\right){/tex} Large aperture : to increase resolving power So, it can distinguish two very close points on the surface of a planet distinctly. {tex}\left(\because R P=\frac{2 a}{1.22 \lambda}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Large focal length : to increase magnifying power
{tex}\left(\because m=\frac{f_{c}}{f_{e}}\right){/tex}
Large aperture : to increase resolving power So, it can distinguish two very close points on the surface of a planet distinctly.
{tex}\left(\because R P=\frac{2 a}{1.22 \lambda}\right){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Does the magnifying power of a microscope depend on the colour of the light used? Justify your answer.
question_text: Does the magnifying power of a microscope depend on the colour of the light used? Justify your answer.
explanation: Yes, the magnifying power of a microscope depends on the colour of the light used. Justification, {tex}m \propto \frac{1}{f_{o} f_{e}}{/tex} And focal length depends on colour/ {tex} \mu{/tex} The magnifying power of the microscope {tex}\propto \frac{1}{f_{o} f_{e}}{/tex} Where f o and f e are the focal lengths of the objective lens and eyepiece lens respectively. The focal length of a lens depends upon the refractive index and different colour of light has a different refractive index with respect to the medium of lens material. Hence, the magnifying power of a microscope depends on the colour of the light used.
solution: Yes, the magnifying power of a microscope depends on the colour of the light used. Justification, {tex}m \propto \frac{1}{f_{o} f_{e}}{/tex} And focal length depends on colour/ {tex} \mu{/tex} The magnifying power of the microscope {tex}\propto \frac{1}{f_{o} f_{e}}{/tex} Where f o and f e are the focal lengths of the objective lens and eyepiece lens respectively. The focal length of a lens depends upon the refractive index and different colour of light has a different refractive index with respect to the medium of lens material. Hence, the magnifying power of a microscope depends on the colour of the light used.
solution_html:
Yes, the magnifying power of a microscope depends on the colour of the light used.
Justification, {tex}m \propto \frac{1}{f_{o} f_{e}}{/tex}
And focal length depends on colour/{tex} \mu{/tex}
The magnifying power of the microscope  {tex}\propto \frac{1}{f_{o} f_{e}}{/tex}
Where fo and fe are the focal lengths of the objective lens and eyepiece lens respectively.
The focal length of a lens depends upon the refractive index and different colour of light has a different refractive index with respect to the medium of lens material. Hence, the magnifying power of a microscope depends on the colour of the light used.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define the magnifying power of a compound microscope when the final image is formed at infinity. Why must both the objective and the eyepiece of a compound microscope have short focal lengths? Explain.
question_text: Define the magnifying power of a compound microscope when the final image is formed at infinity. Why must both the objective and the eyepiece of a compound microscope have short focal lengths? Explain.
explanation: Magnifying power is defined as the angle subtended by the image to the angle subtended (at the unaided eye) by the object. Or The magnifying power of a compound microscope is defined as the ratio of the angle subtended by the final image at the eye to the angle subtended by the object when both are placed at the least distance of distinct vision. {tex}m=m_{0} \times m_{e}=\frac{L}{f_{0}} \times \frac{D}{f_{e}}{/tex} To increase the magnifying power both the objective and eyepiece must have short focal lengths {tex}\left(\text { as } m=\frac{L}{f_{0}} \times \frac{D}{f_{e}}\right){/tex}
solution: Magnifying power is defined as the angle subtended by the image to the angle subtended (at the unaided eye) by the object. Or The magnifying power of a compound microscope is defined as the ratio of the angle subtended by the final image at the eye to the angle subtended by the object when both are placed at the least distance of distinct vision. {tex}m=m_{0} \times m_{e}=\frac{L}{f_{0}} \times \frac{D}{f_{e}}{/tex} To increase the magnifying power both the objective and eyepiece must have short focal lengths {tex}\left(\text { as } m=\frac{L}{f_{0}} \times \frac{D}{f_{e}}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Magnifying power is defined as the angle subtended by the image to the angle subtended (at the unaided eye) by the object.
Or The magnifying power of a compound microscope is defined as the ratio of the angle subtended by the final image at the eye to the angle subtended by the object when both are placed at the least distance of distinct vision.
{tex}m=m_{0} \times m_{e}=\frac{L}{f_{0}} \times \frac{D}{f_{e}}{/tex}
To increase the magnifying power both the objective and eyepiece must have short focal
lengths {tex}\left(\text { as } m=\frac{L}{f_{0}} \times \frac{D}{f_{e}}\right){/tex}

question_html:
1. State the condition under which a large magnification can be achieved in an astronomical telescope.
  2. Give two reasons to explain why a reflecting telescope is preferred over a refracting telescope.
question_text: State the condition under which a large magnification can be achieved in an astronomical telescope. Give two reasons to explain why a reflecting telescope is preferred over a refracting telescope.
explanation: {tex}m=\frac{f_{0}}{f_{e}}{/tex} (1+f e /D) The distance of the eyepiece is so adjusted that the objective's image lies within its focal length. The eyepiece magnifies this image so that the final image is magnified and inverted with respect to the object condition: By increasing f o or decreasing f e (a) No chromatic aberration. (b) No spherical aberration. (c) Mechanical advantage - low weight, easier to support. (d) Mirrors are easy to prepare. (e) More economical.
solution: {tex}m=\frac{f_{0}}{f_{e}}{/tex} (1+f e /D) The distance of the eyepiece is so adjusted that the objective's image lies within its focal length. The eyepiece magnifies this image so that the final image is magnified and inverted with respect to the object condition: By increasing f o or decreasing f e (a) No chromatic aberration. (b) No spherical aberration. (c) Mechanical advantage - low weight, easier to support. (d) Mirrors are easy to prepare. (e) More economical.
solution_html:
1. {tex}m=\frac{f_{0}}{f_{e}}{/tex}(1+fe/D)    
    The distance of the eyepiece is so adjusted that the objective's image lies within its focal length. The eyepiece magnifies this image so that the final image is magnified and inverted with respect to the object
     condition: By increasing fo  or decreasing fe
  2. (a) No chromatic aberration.
    (b) No spherical aberration.
    (c) Mechanical advantage - low weight, easier to support.
    (d) Mirrors are easy to prepare.
    (e) More economical.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a ray diagram showing the formation of image by a reflecting telescope.
  2. Write two advantages of a reflecting telescope over a refracting telescope.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram showing the formation of image by a reflecting telescope. Write two advantages of a reflecting telescope over a refracting telescope.
explanation: Two advantage The image formed in a reflecting telescope is brighter than the image formed in a refracting telescope. Lenses used in large sized refracting telescopes are very heavy and bulky and difficult to manufacture whereas mirrors used in large sized reflecting telescopes can be made thin and light using different techniques.
solution: Two advantage The image formed in a reflecting telescope is brighter than the image formed in a refracting telescope. Lenses used in large sized refracting telescopes are very heavy and bulky and difficult to manufacture whereas mirrors used in large sized reflecting telescopes can be made thin and light using different techniques.
solution_html:
2. Two advantage
    The image formed in a reflecting telescope is brighter than the image formed in a refracting telescope.
    Lenses used in large sized refracting telescopes are very heavy and bulky and difficult to manufacture whereas mirrors used in large sized reflecting telescopes can be made thin and light using different techniques.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1.  Draw a ray diagram to show the image formation by a combination of two thin convex lenses in contact. Obtain the expression for the power of this combination in terms of the focal lengths of the lenses.
  2. A ray of light passing from air through an equilateral glass prism undergoes minimum deviation when the angle of incidence is {tex}\frac 34{/tex}th of the angle of prism. Calculate the speed of light in the prism.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram to show the image formation by a combination of two thin convex lenses in contact. Obtain the expression for the power of this combination in terms of the focal lengths of the lenses. A ray of light passing from air through an equilateral glass prism undergoes minimum deviation when the angle of incidence is {tex}\frac 34{/tex} th of the angle of prism. Calculate the speed of light in the prism.
explanation: Two thin lenses, of focal length f 1 and f 2 are kept in contact. Let O be the position of the object and let u be the object distance. The distance of the image (which is at I 1 ), for the first lens is v 1 This image serves as object for the second lens. Let the final image be at I. We then have {tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}=\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_{1}}{/tex} Adding, we get {tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} P = P 1 + P 2 A ray of light passing from the air through an equilateral glass prism undergoes minimum deviation. Thus, At a minimum deviation {tex}r=\frac{A}{2}=30^{\circ}{/tex} We are given that, {tex}i=\frac{3}{4} A=45^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Speed of light in the prism v = {tex}c\over\mu{/tex} {tex}=\frac{c}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = (2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 )
solution: Two thin lenses, of focal length f 1 and f 2 are kept in contact. Let O be the position of the object and let u be the object distance. The distance of the image (which is at I 1 ), for the first lens is v 1 This image serves as object for the second lens. Let the final image be at I. We then have {tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}=\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_{1}}{/tex} Adding, we get {tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} P = P 1 + P 2 A ray of light passing from the air through an equilateral glass prism undergoes minimum deviation. Thus, At a minimum deviation {tex}r=\frac{A}{2}=30^{\circ}{/tex} We are given that, {tex}i=\frac{3}{4} A=45^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Speed of light in the prism v = {tex}c\over\mu{/tex} {tex}=\frac{c}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = (2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 )
solution_html:
1. Two thin lenses, of focal length f1 and f2 are kept in contact. Let O be the position of the object and let u be the object distance. The distance of the image (which is at I1), for the first lens is v1
    This image serves as object for the second lens. Let the final image be at I. We then have
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}=\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_{1}}{/tex}  
    Adding, we get
    {tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} P =  P1 + P2
  2. A ray of light passing from the air through an equilateral glass prism undergoes minimum deviation. Thus, At a minimum deviation
    {tex}r=\frac{A}{2}=30^{\circ}{/tex}
    We are given that,  {tex}i=\frac{3}{4} A=45^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Speed of light in the prism v = {tex}c\over\mu{/tex} {tex}=\frac{c}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
    = (2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1)

subject: Physics
question_html: An object is placed in front of a convex lens made of glass. How does the image distance vary if the refractive index of the medium is increased in such a way that still it remains less than the glass?
question_text: An object is placed in front of a convex lens made of glass. How does the image distance vary if the refractive index of the medium is increased in such a way that still it remains less than the glass?
explanation: From the lens maker’s formula, it is clear that n 21 decreases then focal length increase. {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\left(n_{21}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\left(\because n_{21}=\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}\right){/tex} Here refractive index of the glass with respect to surrounding material decreases. Hence, focal length increases which will also increase the image distance.
solution: From the lens maker’s formula, it is clear that n 21 decreases then focal length increase. {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\left(n_{21}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\left(\because n_{21}=\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}\right){/tex} Here refractive index of the glass with respect to surrounding material decreases. Hence, focal length increases which will also increase the image distance.
solution_html:
From the lens maker’s formula, it is clear that n21 decreases then focal length increase.
{tex}\frac{1}{f}=\left(n_{21}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\left(\because n_{21}=\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}\right){/tex}
Here refractive index of the glass with respect to surrounding material decreases. Hence, focal length increases which will also increase the image distance.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1.  Define the refractive index of a medium.
  2. In the following ray diagram, calculate the speed of light in the liquid of an unknown refractive index.
question_text: Define the refractive index of a medium. In the following ray diagram, calculate the speed of light in the liquid of an unknown refractive index.
explanation: Refractive index of a medium is the ratio of the speed of light (c) in free space to the speed of light (v) in that medium. It is a dimensionless quantity. {tex}\mu=\frac{c}{v}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{c}{v}=\frac{1}{\sin i_{c}}, {1\over sin i_c} ={OA\over {CA}}{/tex} , {tex}{OA\over CA}={\sqrt{ 30^2+40^2}\over 30}={50\over 30}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{v}={50\over 30}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{30}{50} \times 3 \times 10^{8}{/tex} = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s
solution: Refractive index of a medium is the ratio of the speed of light (c) in free space to the speed of light (v) in that medium. It is a dimensionless quantity. {tex}\mu=\frac{c}{v}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{c}{v}=\frac{1}{\sin i_{c}}, {1\over sin i_c} ={OA\over {CA}}{/tex} , {tex}{OA\over CA}={\sqrt{ 30^2+40^2}\over 30}={50\over 30}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{v}={50\over 30}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{30}{50} \times 3 \times 10^{8}{/tex} = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s
solution_html:
1. Refractive index of a medium is the ratio of the speed of light (c) in free space to the speed of light (v) in that medium. It is a dimensionless quantity.
    {tex}\mu=\frac{c}{v}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\mu=\frac{c}{v}=\frac{1}{\sin i_{c}}, {1\over sin i_c} ={OA\over {CA}}{/tex} ,{tex}{OA\over CA}={\sqrt{ 30^2+40^2}\over 30}={50\over 30}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{v}={50\over 30}{/tex}
    {tex}v=\frac{30}{50} \times 3 \times 10^{8}{/tex}
    = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Derive the mathematical relation between refractive indices {tex}n_1{/tex} and {tex}n_2{/tex} of two media and radius of curvature R for refraction at a convex spherical surface. Consider the object to be a point source lying on the principal axis in rarer medium of refractive index {tex}n_1{/tex}and a real image formed in the denser medium of refractive index {tex}n_2{/tex}. Hence, derive lens maker's formula.
  2. Light from a point source in air falls on a convex spherical glass surface of refractive index 1.5 and radius of curvature 20 cm. The distance of light source from the glass surface is 100 cm. At what position is the image formed?
question_text: Derive the mathematical relation between refractive indices {tex}n_1{/tex} and {tex}n_2{/tex} of two media and radius of curvature R for refraction at a convex spherical surface. Consider the object to be a point source lying on the principal axis in rarer medium of refractive index {tex}n_1{/tex} and a real image formed in the denser medium of refractive index {tex}n_2{/tex} . Hence, derive lens maker's formula. Light from a point source in air falls on a convex spherical glass surface of refractive index 1.5 and radius of curvature 20 cm. The distance of light source from the glass surface is 100 cm. At what position is the image formed?
explanation: Derivation of {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{\left(n_2-n_1\right)}{R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{n_2-n_1}{n_1}\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} Finding position of image formed by convex spherical glass surface For paraxial rays, tan x = x = sin x Snell's law reduces to {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{i}{r}=\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore i \times n_1=r \times n_2{/tex} {tex}(\alpha+\beta) n_1=(\beta-\gamma) n_2{/tex} {tex}n_1\left(\frac{N M}{O M}+\frac{N M}{M C}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{N M}{M C}-\frac{N M}{M I}\right) n_2{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{-u}+\frac{1}{R}\right) n_1=\left(\frac{1}{+R}-\frac{1}{v}\right) n_2{/tex} {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{\left(n_2-n_1\right)}{R}{/tex} Applying above relations to refraction through a lens: For surface 1, {tex}\frac{n_1-n_2}{R_2}=\frac{n_2}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{n_1}{v}{/tex} ...(i) For surface 2, {tex}\frac{n_2-n_1}{R_1}=\frac{n_1}{v}-\frac{n_2}{V^{\prime}}{/tex} ...(ii) Adding eqn. (i) and (ii), {tex}\left(n_2-n_1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)=n_1\left(\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}\right) {/tex} For u = {tex}\infty{/tex} , v = f, {tex}\therefore \frac{n_1}{f}=\left(n_2-n_1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} R = 20 cm , n 2 = 1.5, n 1 = 1, u = -100 cm {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}=\frac{\left(n_2-n_1\right)}{R}+\frac{n_1}{u}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{1.5}{v}=\frac{0.5}{20 cm }-\frac{1}{100 cm }{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{1.5}{v}=\frac{1.5}{100}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = 100 cm, a real image on the other side, 100 cm away from the surface.
solution: Derivation of {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{\left(n_2-n_1\right)}{R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{n_2-n_1}{n_1}\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} Finding position of image formed by convex spherical glass surface For paraxial rays, tan x = x = sin x Snell's law reduces to {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{i}{r}=\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore i \times n_1=r \times n_2{/tex} {tex}(\alpha+\beta) n_1=(\beta-\gamma) n_2{/tex} {tex}n_1\left(\frac{N M}{O M}+\frac{N M}{M C}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{N M}{M C}-\frac{N M}{M I}\right) n_2{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{-u}+\frac{1}{R}\right) n_1=\left(\frac{1}{+R}-\frac{1}{v}\right) n_2{/tex} {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{\left(n_2-n_1\right)}{R}{/tex} Applying above relations to refraction through a lens: For surface 1, {tex}\frac{n_1-n_2}{R_2}=\frac{n_2}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{n_1}{v}{/tex} ...(i) For surface 2, {tex}\frac{n_2-n_1}{R_1}=\frac{n_1}{v}-\frac{n_2}{V^{\prime}}{/tex} ...(ii) Adding eqn. (i) and (ii), {tex}\left(n_2-n_1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)=n_1\left(\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}\right) {/tex} For u = {tex}\infty{/tex} , v = f, {tex}\therefore \frac{n_1}{f}=\left(n_2-n_1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} R = 20 cm , n 2 = 1.5, n 1 = 1, u = -100 cm {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}=\frac{\left(n_2-n_1\right)}{R}+\frac{n_1}{u}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{1.5}{v}=\frac{0.5}{20 cm }-\frac{1}{100 cm }{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{1.5}{v}=\frac{1.5}{100}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = 100 cm, a real image on the other side, 100 cm away from the surface.
solution_html:
1. Derivation of {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{\left(n_2-n_1\right)}{R}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{n_2-n_1}{n_1}\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
  2. Finding position of image formed by convex spherical glass surface

    1. For paraxial rays, tan x = x = sin x
      Snell's law reduces to
      {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{i}{r}=\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex}
      {tex}\therefore i \times n_1=r \times n_2{/tex}
      {tex}(\alpha+\beta) n_1=(\beta-\gamma) n_2{/tex}
      {tex}n_1\left(\frac{N M}{O M}+\frac{N M}{M C}\right){/tex}{tex}=\left(\frac{N M}{M C}-\frac{N M}{M I}\right) n_2{/tex}
      {tex}\left(\frac{1}{-u}+\frac{1}{R}\right) n_1=\left(\frac{1}{+R}-\frac{1}{v}\right) n_2{/tex}
      {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{\left(n_2-n_1\right)}{R}{/tex}
      Applying above relations to refraction through a lens:

      For surface 1,
      {tex}\frac{n_1-n_2}{R_2}=\frac{n_2}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{n_1}{v}{/tex} ...(i)
      For surface 2,
      {tex}\frac{n_2-n_1}{R_1}=\frac{n_1}{v}-\frac{n_2}{V^{\prime}}{/tex} ...(ii)
      Adding eqn. (i) and (ii),
      {tex}\left(n_2-n_1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)=n_1\left(\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}\right) {/tex}
      For u = {tex}\infty{/tex}, v = f,
      {tex}\therefore \frac{n_1}{f}=\left(n_2-n_1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
      {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
    2. R = 20 cm , n2 = 1.5, n1 = 1, u = -100 cm
      {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}=\frac{\left(n_2-n_1\right)}{R}+\frac{n_1}{u}{/tex}
      or, {tex}\frac{1.5}{v}=\frac{0.5}{20 cm }-\frac{1}{100 cm }{/tex}
      or, {tex}\frac{1.5}{v}=\frac{1.5}{100}{/tex}
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = 100 cm, a real image on the other side, 100 cm away from the surface.

question_html:
1. Draw a labelled ray diagram to obtain the real image formed by an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment position. Define its magnifying power.
  2. You are given three lenses of power 0.5 D, 4D and 10 D to design a telescope.
    1. Which lenses should be used as objective and eyepiece? Justify your answer.
    2. Why is the aperture of the objective preferred to be large?
question_text: Draw a labelled ray diagram to obtain the real image formed by an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment position. Define its magnifying power. You are given three lenses of power 0.5 D, 4D and 10 D to design a telescope. Which lenses should be used as objective and eyepiece? Justify your answer. Why is the aperture of the objective preferred to be large?
explanation: In astronomical telescope for normal adjustment, final image is formed at infinity and it is virtual. The labelled ray diagram to obtain one of the real image formed by the astronomical telescope is shown below: Magnifying power is defined as the ratio of the angle subtended at the eye by the focal image as seen through the telescope to the angle subtended at the eye by the object seen directly, when both the image and the object lies at infinity. We know the objective lens of a telescope should have larger focal length and eyepiece lens should have smaller focal length. And focal length is inverse of power, so lens of power (P = 1/f). Thus, 10D can be used as eyepiece and lens of power 0.5 D can be used as objective lens. The objective lens of a telescope should have larger aperture, in order to form bright image of distant objects, so that it can gather sufficient light rays from the distant objects.
solution: In astronomical telescope for normal adjustment, final image is formed at infinity and it is virtual. The labelled ray diagram to obtain one of the real image formed by the astronomical telescope is shown below: Magnifying power is defined as the ratio of the angle subtended at the eye by the focal image as seen through the telescope to the angle subtended at the eye by the object seen directly, when both the image and the object lies at infinity. We know the objective lens of a telescope should have larger focal length and eyepiece lens should have smaller focal length. And focal length is inverse of power, so lens of power (P = 1/f). Thus, 10D can be used as eyepiece and lens of power 0.5 D can be used as objective lens. The objective lens of a telescope should have larger aperture, in order to form bright image of distant objects, so that it can gather sufficient light rays from the distant objects.
solution_html:
1. In astronomical telescope for normal adjustment, final image is formed at infinity and it is virtual.
    The labelled ray diagram to obtain one of the real image formed by the astronomical telescope is shown below:

    Magnifying power is defined as the ratio of the angle subtended at the eye by the focal image as seen through the telescope to the angle subtended at the eye by the object seen directly, when both the image and the object lies at infinity.
  2.  
    1. We know the objective lens of a telescope should have larger focal length and eyepiece lens should have smaller focal length. And focal length is inverse of power, so lens of power (P = 1/f).
      Thus, 10D can be used as eyepiece and lens of power 0.5 D can be used as objective lens.
    2. The objective lens of a telescope should have larger aperture, in order to form bright image of distant objects, so that it can gather sufficient light rays from the distant objects.

subject: Physics
question_html: A biconvex lens made of a transparent material of refractive index 1.25 is immersed in water of refractive index 1.33. Will the lens behaves as a converging or a diverging lens? Give reason.
question_text: A biconvex lens made of a transparent material of refractive index 1.25 is immersed in water of refractive index 1.33. Will the lens behaves as a converging or a diverging lens? Give reason.
explanation: When a lens is placed in a liquid, where refractive index is more than that of the material of lens, then the nature of the lens changes. Since the refractive index of the material in which the lens is immersed is more, the focal length would decrease and become negative. Hence, it would then behave as a diverging lens.
solution: When a lens is placed in a liquid, where refractive index is more than that of the material of lens, then the nature of the lens changes. Since the refractive index of the material in which the lens is immersed is more, the focal length would decrease and become negative. Hence, it would then behave as a diverging lens.
solution_html: When a lens is placed in a liquid, where refractive index is more than that of the material of lens, then the nature of the lens changes. Since the refractive index of the material in which the lens is immersed is more, the focal length would decrease and become negative. Hence, it would then behave as a diverging lens.

subject: Physics
question_html: A biconvex lens made of a transparent material of refractive index 1.25 is immersed in a water of refractive index 1.33. Will the lens behave as a converging or a diverging lens? Give reason.
question_text: A biconvex lens made of a transparent material of refractive index 1.25 is immersed in a water of refractive index 1.33. Will the lens behave as a converging or a diverging lens? Give reason.
explanation: A biconvex lens behaves as a converging lens in water as the refractive index of water (1.33) is more than the refractive index of the material of the lens (1.25). On the other hand, a biconvex lens acts as a diverging lens in air because the refractive index of air is less than that of the material of the lens.
solution: A biconvex lens behaves as a converging lens in water as the refractive index of water (1.33) is more than the refractive index of the material of the lens (1.25). On the other hand, a biconvex lens acts as a diverging lens in air because the refractive index of air is less than that of the material of the lens.
solution_html:
A biconvex lens behaves as a converging lens in water as the refractive index of water (1.33) is more than the refractive index of the material of the lens (1.25).
On the other hand, a biconvex lens acts as a diverging lens in air because the refractive index of air is less than that of the material of the lens.

question_html:
1. A mobile phone lies along the principal axis of a concave mirror. Show with the help of a suitable diagram, the formation of its image. Explain why magnification is not a uniform.
  2. Suppose the lower half of the concave mirror's reflecting surface is covered with an opaque material. What effect this will have on the image of the object? Explain.
question_text: A mobile phone lies along the principal axis of a concave mirror. Show with the help of a suitable diagram, the formation of its image. Explain why magnification is not a uniform. Suppose the lower half of the concave mirror's reflecting surface is covered with an opaque material. What effect this will have on the image of the object? Explain.
explanation: The ray diagram for the formation of the image of the phone is shown as below. The image of the part which is on the plane perpendicular to principle axis will be on the same plane. It will be of the same size i.e. B'C = BC. The mobile phone has many rectangles that are at different positions with different distances from the mirror. As there are different object distances, so their images and magnification are different. As the laws of reflection are to be true for all points of the remaining part of the mirror, the image will be that of the whole object. However, as the area of the reflecting surface has been reduced, the intensity of the image will be low, i.e. half.
solution: The ray diagram for the formation of the image of the phone is shown as below. The image of the part which is on the plane perpendicular to principle axis will be on the same plane. It will be of the same size i.e. B'C = BC. The mobile phone has many rectangles that are at different positions with different distances from the mirror. As there are different object distances, so their images and magnification are different. As the laws of reflection are to be true for all points of the remaining part of the mirror, the image will be that of the whole object. However, as the area of the reflecting surface has been reduced, the intensity of the image will be low, i.e. half.
solution_html:
1. The ray diagram for the formation of the image of the phone is shown as below. The image of the part which is on the plane perpendicular to principle axis will be on the same plane. It will be of the same size i.e. B'C = BC.
    The mobile phone has many rectangles that are at different positions with different distances from the mirror. As there are different object distances, so their images and magnification are different.
  2. As the laws of reflection are to be true for all points of the remaining part of the mirror, the image will be that of the whole object. However, as the area of the reflecting surface has been reduced, the intensity of the image will be low, i.e. half.

question_html: Two monochromatic rays of light are incident normally on the face AB of an isosceles right-angled prism ABC. The refractive indices of the glass prism for the two rays 1 and 2 are respectively 1.35 and 1.45. Trace the path of these rays after entering through the prism.
question_text: Two monochromatic rays of light are incident normally on the face AB of an isosceles right-angled prism ABC. The refractive indices of the glass prism for the two rays 1 and 2 are respectively 1.35 and 1.45. Trace the path of these rays after entering through the prism.
explanation: From the figure, angle of incidence for ray 1 is 45 0 . For ray 1, sin i = sin 45 0 = {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 1.414 }{/tex} For ray 1, {tex}\mu = 1.35{/tex} {tex}\mu = \frac { 1 } { \sin c } \Rightarrow \sin c = \frac { 1 } { \mu } = \frac { 1 } { 1.35 }{/tex} Here, {tex}\frac { 1 } { 1.414 } < \frac { 1 } { 1.35 }{/tex} i.e. sin i < sin c or i < c, so ray 1 will be refracted by the prism. For ray 2, angle of incidence, i = 45 0 sin i = sin 45 0 = {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 1.414 }{/tex} For ray 2, {tex}\mu = 1.45{/tex} {tex}\mu = \frac { 1 } { \sin c } \Rightarrow \sin c = \frac { 1 } { \mu } = \frac { 1 } { 1.45 }{/tex} Here, {tex}\frac{1}{{1.414}} > \frac{1}{{1.45}}{/tex} i.e. sin i > sin c or i > c, so, ray 2 will get total internally reflected.
solution: From the figure, angle of incidence for ray 1 is 45 0 . For ray 1, sin i = sin 45 0 = {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 1.414 }{/tex} For ray 1, {tex}\mu = 1.35{/tex} {tex}\mu = \frac { 1 } { \sin c } \Rightarrow \sin c = \frac { 1 } { \mu } = \frac { 1 } { 1.35 }{/tex} Here, {tex}\frac { 1 } { 1.414 } < \frac { 1 } { 1.35 }{/tex} i.e. sin i < sin c or i < c, so ray 1 will be refracted by the prism. For ray 2, angle of incidence, i = 45 0 sin i = sin 45 0 = {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 1.414 }{/tex} For ray 2, {tex}\mu = 1.45{/tex} {tex}\mu = \frac { 1 } { \sin c } \Rightarrow \sin c = \frac { 1 } { \mu } = \frac { 1 } { 1.45 }{/tex} Here, {tex}\frac{1}{{1.414}} > \frac{1}{{1.45}}{/tex} i.e. sin i > sin c or i > c, so, ray 2 will get total internally reflected.
solution_html:
From the figure, angle of incidence for ray 1 is 450 .
For ray 1, sin i = sin 450 = {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 1.414 }{/tex}
For ray 1,
{tex}\mu = 1.35{/tex}
{tex}\mu = \frac { 1 } { \sin c } \Rightarrow \sin c = \frac { 1 } { \mu } = \frac { 1 } { 1.35 }{/tex}
Here, {tex}\frac { 1 } { 1.414 } < \frac { 1 } { 1.35 }{/tex}
i.e. sin i < sin c or i < c, so ray 1 will be refracted by the prism.
For ray 2, angle of incidence, i = 450
sin i = sin 450 = {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 1.414 }{/tex}
For ray 2, {tex}\mu = 1.45{/tex}
{tex}\mu = \frac { 1 } { \sin c } \Rightarrow \sin c = \frac { 1 } { \mu } = \frac { 1 } { 1.45 }{/tex}
Here, {tex}\frac{1}{{1.414}} > \frac{1}{{1.45}}{/tex}
i.e. sin i > sin c or i > c, so, ray 2 will get total internally reflected.

question_html: A convex lens is placed in contact with a plane mirror. A point object at a distance of 20 cm on the axis of this combination has its image coinciding with itself. What is the focal length of the lens?
question_text: A convex lens is placed in contact with a plane mirror. A point object at a distance of 20 cm on the axis of this combination has its image coinciding with itself. What is the focal length of the lens?
explanation: L = Convex Lens in contact with a plane of Mirror P = The point where Object is kept in front of Lens and Mirror i.e LM at a distance of 20 cm from it. As per question, image is coinciding with the object itself. After refraction from the lens, the rays from the object falls normally on the Mirror M. So, the image will be formed at the focus of the lens. Hence, the focal length of the lens is 20 cm.
solution: L = Convex Lens in contact with a plane of Mirror P = The point where Object is kept in front of Lens and Mirror i.e LM at a distance of 20 cm from it. As per question, image is coinciding with the object itself. After refraction from the lens, the rays from the object falls normally on the Mirror M. So, the image will be formed at the focus of the lens. Hence, the focal length of the lens is 20 cm.
solution_html:
L = Convex Lens in contact with a plane of Mirror
P = The point where Object is kept in front of Lens and Mirror i.e LM at a distance of 20 cm from it.
As per question, image is coinciding with the object itself. After refraction from the lens, the rays from the object falls normally on the Mirror M. So, the image will be formed at the focus of the lens. Hence, the focal length of the lens is 20 cm.

explanation: ray diagram is given by; Magnifying power is given by , {tex}m=\frac{\tan \beta}{\tan \alpha} \cong \frac{\beta}{\alpha}{/tex} The angles are small Final image is formed at infinity when the image A'B' is formed by the objective lens at the focus of the eyepiece, {tex}m=\frac{h}{f_{e}} \times \frac{f_{0}}{h}{/tex} {tex}m=\frac{f_{0}}{f_{e}}{/tex} Given, f o + f e =105, f o = 20f e 20f e + f e = 105 {tex}f_{c}=\frac{105}{21}=5 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} f o =20 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 100 cm Magnification is given by, {tex}m=\frac{f_{0}}{f_{e}}=\frac{100}{5}=20{/tex}
solution: ray diagram is given by; Magnifying power is given by , {tex}m=\frac{\tan \beta}{\tan \alpha} \cong \frac{\beta}{\alpha}{/tex} The angles are small Final image is formed at infinity when the image A'B' is formed by the objective lens at the focus of the eyepiece, {tex}m=\frac{h}{f_{e}} \times \frac{f_{0}}{h}{/tex} {tex}m=\frac{f_{0}}{f_{e}}{/tex} Given, f o + f e =105, f o = 20f e 20f e + f e = 105 {tex}f_{c}=\frac{105}{21}=5 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} f o =20 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 100 cm Magnification is given by, {tex}m=\frac{f_{0}}{f_{e}}=\frac{100}{5}=20{/tex}

    {tex}f_{c}=\frac{105}{21}=5 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}

question_html: A convex lens of focal length f1 is kept in contact with a concave lens of focal length f2. Find the focal length of the combination.
question_text: A convex lens of focal length f 1 is kept in contact with a concave lens of focal length f 2 . Find the focal length of the combination.
explanation: Focal length for convex lens = f 1 Focal length for concave lens = - f 2 The equivalent focal length of a combination of a convex lens and concave lens is given by {tex}\frac { 1 } { F } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { - f _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow F = \frac { f _ { 1 } f _ { 2 } } { f _ { 2 } - f _ { 1 } }{/tex}
solution: Focal length for convex lens = f 1 Focal length for concave lens = - f 2 The equivalent focal length of a combination of a convex lens and concave lens is given by {tex}\frac { 1 } { F } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { - f _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow F = \frac { f _ { 1 } f _ { 2 } } { f _ { 2 } - f _ { 1 } }{/tex}
solution_html:
Focal length for convex lens = f1
Focal length for concave lens = - f2
The equivalent focal length of a combination of a convex lens and concave lens is given by
{tex}\frac { 1 } { F } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { - f _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow F = \frac { f _ { 1 } f _ { 2 } } { f _ { 2 } - f _ { 1 } }{/tex}

question_html: For the same value of angle of incidence, the angles of refraction in three media A, B and C are 15°, 25° and 35° respectively. In which medium, would the velocity of light be minimum?
question_text: For the same value of angle of incidence, the angles of refraction in three media A, B and C are 15°, 25° and 35° respectively. In which medium, would the velocity of light be minimum?
explanation: By using Snell's law, {tex}\mu = \frac {sin\; i}{sin \;r} = \frac cv{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v \propto \sin r{/tex} for given value of angle of incidence i. Thus, smaller the angle of refraction, smaller the velocity of light in medium. Hence, the velocity of light is minimum for A, as angle of refraction is minimum, i.e. 15°.
solution: By using Snell's law, {tex}\mu = \frac {sin\; i}{sin \;r} = \frac cv{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v \propto \sin r{/tex} for given value of angle of incidence i. Thus, smaller the angle of refraction, smaller the velocity of light in medium. Hence, the velocity of light is minimum for A, as angle of refraction is minimum, i.e. 15°.
solution_html:
By using Snell's law,
{tex}\mu = \frac {sin\; i}{sin \;r} = \frac cv{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad v \propto \sin r{/tex} for given value of angle of incidence i.
Thus, smaller the angle of refraction, smaller the velocity of light in medium. Hence, the velocity of light is minimum for A, as angle of refraction is minimum, i.e. 15°.

question_html: A ray of light incident on an equilateral glass prism {tex}\left( \mu _ { g } = \sqrt { 3 } \right){/tex} moves parallel to the base line of the prism inside it. Find the angle of incidence for this ray.
question_text: A ray of light incident on an equilateral glass prism {tex}\left( \mu _ { g } = \sqrt { 3 } \right){/tex} moves parallel to the base line of the prism inside it. Find the angle of incidence for this ray.
explanation: The reflection of light through a prism is shown as below. By geometry, angle of refraction, r = 30 0 . solution: The reflection of light through a prism is shown as below. By geometry, angle of refraction, r = 30 0 . question_html:
You are given three lenses L1, L2 and L3 each of focal length 15 cm. An object is kept at 20 cm in front of L1 as shown in Figure.

The real image is formed at the focus I of L3. Find the separations between L1, L2 and L3.
question_text: You are given three lenses L 1 , L 2 and L 3 each of focal length 15 cm. An object is kept at 20 cm in front of L 1 as shown in Figure. The real image is formed at the focus I of L 3 . Find the separations between L 1 , L 2 and L 3 .
explanation: As final image is formed at I, the focus of lens L 3 ; the rays of light from the lens L 2 must fall on the lens L 3 in the form of a parallel beam of light. For this to happen, the lens L 1 should form the image of the object at the focus of the lens L 2 . Therefore, separation between lenses L 1 and L 2 , x = v + f, where v is the distance of the image of O formed by L 1 from it. Now, {tex}-\frac{1}{u}+\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} Here, u = -20 cm, f = 15 cm {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{u}+\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{-20}+\frac{1}{15}=\frac{1}{60}{/tex} or v = 60 cm Hence, x = 60 + 15 = 75 cm As the rays of light from L 2 fall on the lens L 3 in the form of a parallel beam of light, the separation between lenses L 2 and L 3 can have any value.

x = v + f,

subject: Physics
question_html: When monochromatic light travels from one medium to another, its wavelength changes but frequency remains the same. Explain.
question_text: When monochromatic light travels from one medium to another, its wavelength changes but frequency remains the same. Explain.
explanation: Because the refractive index for a given pair of media depends on the ratio of wavelengths or velocity of light in two media and not on frequency (because the frequency of light depends on its source).
solution: Because the refractive index for a given pair of media depends on the ratio of wavelengths or velocity of light in two media and not on frequency (because the frequency of light depends on its source).
solution_html: Because the refractive index for a given pair of media depends on the ratio of wavelengths or velocity of light in two media and not on frequency (because the frequency of light depends on its source).

question_html: Draw a ray diagram to show the refraction of a ray of monochromatic light passing through a glass prism.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram to show the refraction of a ray of monochromatic light passing through a glass prism.
explanation: The rays PQ, QR and RS are called incident ray, refracted ray and emergent ray respectively. Also, {tex}\angle{/tex} PQN = i (angle of incidence), {tex}\angle{/tex} SRN ' = e (angle of emergence), {tex}\angle{/tex} RQO = r 1 (angle of refraction at face AB) and {tex}\angle{/tex} QRO = r 2 (angle of refraction at face AC). {tex}\delta{/tex} is called the angle of deviation and {tex}\angle{/tex} A is angle of prism.
solution: The rays PQ, QR and RS are called incident ray, refracted ray and emergent ray respectively. Also, {tex}\angle{/tex} PQN = i (angle of incidence), {tex}\angle{/tex} SRN ' = e (angle of emergence), {tex}\angle{/tex} RQO = r 1 (angle of refraction at face AB) and {tex}\angle{/tex} QRO = r 2 (angle of refraction at face AC). {tex}\delta{/tex} is called the angle of deviation and {tex}\angle{/tex} A is angle of prism.
solution_html:
The rays PQ, QR and RS are called incident ray, refracted ray and emergent ray respectively. 
Also, {tex}\angle{/tex}PQN = i (angle of incidence), {tex}\angle{/tex}SRN ' = e (angle of emergence), {tex}\angle{/tex}RQO = r1 (angle of refraction at face AB) and {tex}\angle{/tex}QRO = r2(angle of refraction at face AC).
{tex}\delta{/tex} is called the angle of deviation and {tex}\angle{/tex}A is angle of prism.

subject: Physics
question_html: A glass lens of refractive index 1.5 is placed in a trough of liquid. What must be the refractive index of the liquid in order to mark the lens disappear?
question_text: A glass lens of refractive index 1.5 is placed in a trough of liquid. What must be the refractive index of the liquid in order to mark the lens disappear?
explanation: The refractive index of liquid must be greater than or equal to 1.5. The lens will not be visible if no refraction occurs at the liquid-glass interface. This means that the incident ray should go through the glass without any deviation. For this condition to be fulfilled, the refractive index of the liquid must be equal to 1.5.
solution: The refractive index of liquid must be greater than or equal to 1.5. The lens will not be visible if no refraction occurs at the liquid-glass interface. This means that the incident ray should go through the glass without any deviation. For this condition to be fulfilled, the refractive index of the liquid must be equal to 1.5.
solution_html: The refractive index of liquid must be greater than or equal to 1.5. The lens will not be visible if no refraction occurs at the liquid-glass interface. This means that the incident ray should go through the glass without any deviation. For this condition to be fulfilled, the refractive index of the liquid must be equal to 1.5.

subject: Physics
question_html: Write the necessary conditions, for the phenomenon of total internal reflection to take place.
question_text: Write the necessary conditions, for the phenomenon of total internal reflection to take place.
explanation: Necessary conditions for the total internal reflection are Light rays must travel from the denser medium into the rarer medium. The angle of incidence in the denser medium must be greater than the critical angle for a given pair of optical media.
solution: Necessary conditions for the total internal reflection are Light rays must travel from the denser medium into the rarer medium. The angle of incidence in the denser medium must be greater than the critical angle for a given pair of optical media.
solution_html:
Necessary conditions for the total internal reflection are

  1. Light rays must travel from the denser medium into the rarer medium.
  2. The angle of incidence in the denser medium must be greater than the critical angle for a given pair of optical media.

subject: Physics
question_html: A converging lens axially is kept in contact with a diverging lens; both the lenses being of equal focal lengths. What is the focal length of the combination?
question_text: A converging lens axially is kept in contact with a diverging lens; both the lenses being of equal focal lengths. What is the focal length of the combination?
explanation: The combined focal length of a lens combination is given by: {tex}\frac { 1 } { f } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { f _ { 2 } }{/tex} (as for two thin lenses in contact) As, f 2 = - f 1 (as focal lengths are equal, one is convex and other is concave) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { f } = 0 \Rightarrow f = \infty{/tex} Thus, the focal length of the combination is infinite.
solution: The combined focal length of a lens combination is given by: {tex}\frac { 1 } { f } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { f _ { 2 } }{/tex} (as for two thin lenses in contact) As, f 2 = - f 1 (as focal lengths are equal, one is convex and other is concave) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { f } = 0 \Rightarrow f = \infty{/tex} Thus, the focal length of the combination is infinite.
solution_html:
The combined focal length of a lens combination is given by:
{tex}\frac { 1 } { f } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { f _ { 2 } }{/tex} (as for two thin lenses in contact)
As, f2 = - f1  (as focal lengths are equal, one is convex and other is concave)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { f } = 0 \Rightarrow f = \infty{/tex}
Thus, the focal length of the combination is infinite.

question_html: Write the relation for the refractive index {tex}\mu {/tex} of the prism in terms of the angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta_m{/tex} and the angle A of prism.
question_text: Write the relation for the refractive index {tex}\mu {/tex} of the prism in terms of the angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta_m{/tex} and the angle A of prism.
explanation: For a prism of the angle A, of refractive index n 2 placed in a medium of refractive index n 1 , {tex}n_{21} = \frac {n_2}{n_1}= \frac{{\sin \left( {\frac{{A + {\delta _m}}}{2}} \right)}}{{\sin \frac{A}{2}}}{/tex} where {tex}\delta_m{/tex} is the angle of minimum deviation.
solution: For a prism of the angle A, of refractive index n 2 placed in a medium of refractive index n 1 , {tex}n_{21} = \frac {n_2}{n_1}= \frac{{\sin \left( {\frac{{A + {\delta _m}}}{2}} \right)}}{{\sin \frac{A}{2}}}{/tex} where {tex}\delta_m{/tex} is the angle of minimum deviation.
solution_html:
For a prism of the angle A, of refractive index n2 placed in a medium of refractive index n1,
{tex}n_{21} = \frac {n_2}{n_1}= \frac{{\sin \left( {\frac{{A + {\delta _m}}}{2}} \right)}}{{\sin \frac{A}{2}}}{/tex}
where {tex}\delta_m{/tex} is the angle of minimum deviation.

question_html: Write two merits of a reflecting type telescope over refracting type telescope.
question_text: Write two merits of a reflecting type telescope over refracting type telescope.
explanation: As the objective is a spherical mirror, the reflecting type telescope is free from chromatic aberration. in reflecting type telescope, the defect of spherical aberration is reduced by using parabolic mirror as objective.
solution: As the objective is a spherical mirror, the reflecting type telescope is free from chromatic aberration. in reflecting type telescope, the defect of spherical aberration is reduced by using parabolic mirror as objective.
solution_html:
1. As the objective is a spherical mirror, the reflecting type telescope is free from chromatic aberration.
  2. in reflecting type telescope, the defect of spherical aberration is reduced by using parabolic mirror as objective.

question_html: For which colour, the refractive index of the material of prism is maximum and minimum?
question_text: For which colour, the refractive index of the material of prism is maximum and minimum?
explanation: The refractive index of the material of prism is maximum for violet light and minimum for red light.
solution: The refractive index of the material of prism is maximum for violet light and minimum for red light.
solution_html: The refractive index of the material of prism is maximum for violet light and minimum for red light.

question_html: When light travels from a rarer to denser medium, the speed decreases. Does the decrease in speed imply a decrease in the energy carried by the light wave? Justify your answer.
question_text: When light travels from a rarer to denser medium, the speed decreases. Does the decrease in speed imply a decrease in the energy carried by the light wave? Justify your answer.
explanation: When the light travels from a rarer to denser medium (refraction), its frequency remains unchanged. It is because, frequency is an inherent property of the light. Since energy of a photon of light is h {tex}\nu{/tex} , its energy will remain the same.
solution: When the light travels from a rarer to denser medium (refraction), its frequency remains unchanged. It is because, frequency is an inherent property of the light. Since energy of a photon of light is h {tex}\nu{/tex} , its energy will remain the same.
solution_html: When the light travels from a rarer to denser medium (refraction), its frequency remains unchanged. It is because, frequency is an inherent property of the light. Since energy of a photon of light is h{tex}\nu{/tex}, its energy will remain the same.

subject: Physics
question_html: Out of blue and red light, which is deviated more by a prism? Give reasons.
question_text: Out of blue and red light, which is deviated more by a prism? Give reasons.
explanation: For a small angled prism, deviation produced in the path of the light is given by {tex}\delta{/tex} = A( {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1) Since {tex}\mu_{b}>\mu_r{/tex} , it follows that {tex}\delta_{b}>\delta_{r}{/tex} Therefore, the blue light will suffer greater deviation than that suffered by the red light.
solution: For a small angled prism, deviation produced in the path of the light is given by {tex}\delta{/tex} = A( {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1) Since {tex}\mu_{b}>\mu_r{/tex} , it follows that {tex}\delta_{b}>\delta_{r}{/tex} Therefore, the blue light will suffer greater deviation than that suffered by the red light.
solution_html:
For a small angled prism, deviation produced in the path of the light is given by
{tex}\delta{/tex} = A({tex}\mu{/tex} - 1)
Since {tex}\mu_{b}>\mu_r{/tex}, it follows that
{tex}\delta_{b}>\delta_{r}{/tex}
Therefore, the blue light will suffer greater deviation than that suffered by the red light.

subject: Physics
question_html: Violet colour is seen at the bottom of the spectrum, when white light is dispersed by a prism. Why?
question_text: Violet colour is seen at the bottom of the spectrum, when white light is dispersed by a prism. Why?
explanation: For a small angled prism, deviation produced in the path of the light is given by {tex}\delta=\mathrm{A}(\mu-1){/tex} Since refractive index for the violet colour ( {tex}\mu_v{/tex} ) is maximum; of all the colours, it is deviated most and hence violet colour is seen at the bottom of the spectrum.
solution: For a small angled prism, deviation produced in the path of the light is given by {tex}\delta=\mathrm{A}(\mu-1){/tex} Since refractive index for the violet colour ( {tex}\mu_v{/tex} ) is maximum; of all the colours, it is deviated most and hence violet colour is seen at the bottom of the spectrum.
solution_html:
For a small angled prism, deviation produced in the path of the light is given by 
{tex}\delta=\mathrm{A}(\mu-1){/tex} 
Since refractive index for the violet colour ({tex}\mu_v{/tex}) is maximum; of all the colours, it is deviated most and hence violet colour is seen at the bottom of the spectrum.

subject: Physics
question_html: An astronomical telescope uses two lenses of powers 10 D and 1 D. What is its magnifying power in normal adjustment?
question_text: An astronomical telescope uses two lenses of powers 10 D and 1 D. What is its magnifying power in normal adjustment?
explanation: The magnifying power (in magnitude) of the astronomical telescope in normal adjustment, {tex}M = \frac{{{f_0}}}{{{f_e}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{{{f_e}}}}}{{\frac{1}{{{f_0}}}}} = \frac{{power\;of\;eye\;piece}}{{power\;of\;objective\;lens}}{/tex} The objective lens of a telescope is of large focal length (small power), while the eye piece is of small focal length (large power). {tex}\therefore \mathrm{M}=\frac{10}{1}=10{/tex}
solution: The magnifying power (in magnitude) of the astronomical telescope in normal adjustment, {tex}M = \frac{{{f_0}}}{{{f_e}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{{{f_e}}}}}{{\frac{1}{{{f_0}}}}} = \frac{{power\;of\;eye\;piece}}{{power\;of\;objective\;lens}}{/tex} The objective lens of a telescope is of large focal length (small power), while the eye piece is of small focal length (large power). {tex}\therefore \mathrm{M}=\frac{10}{1}=10{/tex}
solution_html:
The magnifying power (in magnitude) of the astronomical telescope in normal adjustment, {tex}M = \frac{{{f_0}}}{{{f_e}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{{{f_e}}}}}{{\frac{1}{{{f_0}}}}} = \frac{{power\;of\;eye\;piece}}{{power\;of\;objective\;lens}}{/tex}
The objective lens of a telescope is of large focal length (small power), while the eye piece is of small focal length (large power).
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{M}=\frac{10}{1}=10{/tex}

question_html: Why are convex mirrors used as side view mirrors in cars?
question_text: Why are convex mirrors used as side view mirrors in cars?
explanation: Because convex mirror produces a virtual, erect and smaller image of object irrespective of the relative position of the object from the mirror and it gives a view of large area and can see the vehicles coming from the back.
solution: Because convex mirror produces a virtual, erect and smaller image of object irrespective of the relative position of the object from the mirror and it gives a view of large area and can see the vehicles coming from the back.
solution_html: Because convex mirror produces a virtual, erect and smaller image of object irrespective of the relative position of the object from the mirror and it gives a view of large area and can see the vehicles coming from the back.

question_html:
You are given following three lenses. Which two lenses will you use an eyepiece and objective to construct an astronomical telescope?

Lens Power (P) Aperture (A)
L1 3 D 8 cm
L2 6 D 1 cm
L3 10 D 1 cm
question_text: You are given following three lenses. Which two lenses will you use an eyepiece and objective to construct an astronomical telescope? Lens Power (P) Aperture (A) L 1 3 D 8 cm L 2 6 D 1 cm L 3 10 D 1 cm
explanation: For greater magnifying power, the eyepiece of a telescope should have a small focal length (or large power). Therefore, lens L 3 should be used as the eyepiece. For greater magnifying power, the objective of a telescope should have large focal length (or small power). Further, in order to produce a bright image, the aperture of the objective should also be large. Therefore, lens L 1 should be used as the objective.
solution: For greater magnifying power, the eyepiece of a telescope should have a small focal length (or large power). Therefore, lens L 3 should be used as the eyepiece. For greater magnifying power, the objective of a telescope should have large focal length (or small power). Further, in order to produce a bright image, the aperture of the objective should also be large. Therefore, lens L 1 should be used as the objective.
solution_html:
For greater magnifying power, the eyepiece of a telescope should have a small focal length (or large power). Therefore, lens L3 should be used as the eyepiece.
For greater magnifying power, the objective of a telescope should have large focal length (or small power). Further, in order to produce a bright image, the aperture of the objective should also be large. Therefore, lens L1 should be used as the objective.

question_html: A converging lens of refractive index 1.5 is kept in a liquid medium having same refractive index. What would be the focal length of the lens in this medium?
question_text: A converging lens of refractive index 1.5 is kept in a liquid medium having same refractive index. What would be the focal length of the lens in this medium?
explanation: The lens in the liquid will act like a plane sheet of glass. Its focal length will be infinite {tex}(\infty ){/tex} . By using lens maker formula, {tex}\frac{1}{f} = (\mu - 1)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right){/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{f} = \left( {\frac{{{\mu _2}}}{{{\mu _1}}} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right){/tex} As, {tex}{\mu _1} = {\mu _2}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{1}{f} = 0{/tex} Thus, {tex}f = \infty {/tex}
solution: The lens in the liquid will act like a plane sheet of glass. Its focal length will be infinite {tex}(\infty ){/tex} . By using lens maker formula, {tex}\frac{1}{f} = (\mu - 1)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right){/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{f} = \left( {\frac{{{\mu _2}}}{{{\mu _1}}} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right){/tex} As, {tex}{\mu _1} = {\mu _2}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{1}{f} = 0{/tex} Thus, {tex}f = \infty {/tex}
solution_html:
The lens in the liquid will act like a plane sheet of glass. Its focal length will be infinite {tex}(\infty ){/tex}.
By using lens maker formula,
{tex}\frac{1}{f} = (\mu - 1)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right){/tex}
or {tex}\frac{1}{f} = \left( {\frac{{{\mu _2}}}{{{\mu _1}}} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right){/tex}
As, {tex}{\mu _1} = {\mu _2}{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{1}{f} = 0{/tex}
Thus, {tex}f = \infty {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: How does the power of a convex lens vary, if the incident red light is replaced by violet light?
question_text: How does the power of a convex lens vary, if the incident red light is replaced by violet light?
explanation: By the lens maker's formula {tex}\frac{1}{f} = (\mu - 1)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right){/tex} Power of lens, {tex}P = \frac{1}{f} = (\mu - 1)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right){/tex} {tex}\because {\mu _{violet}} > {\mu _{red}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Power of the lens will get increased.
solution: By the lens maker's formula {tex}\frac{1}{f} = (\mu - 1)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right){/tex} Power of lens, {tex}P = \frac{1}{f} = (\mu - 1)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right){/tex} {tex}\because {\mu _{violet}} > {\mu _{red}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Power of the lens will get increased.
solution_html:
By the lens maker's formula
{tex}\frac{1}{f} = (\mu - 1)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right){/tex}
Power of lens, {tex}P = \frac{1}{f} = (\mu - 1)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right){/tex}
{tex}\because {\mu _{violet}} > {\mu _{red}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Power of the lens will get increased.

question_html: When light is incident on a rarer medium from a denser medium, write the relation between the critical angle and refractive indices of the two media.
question_text: When light is incident on a rarer medium from a denser medium, write the relation between the critical angle and refractive indices of the two media.
explanation: If {tex}{}^{air}{\mu _{rarer}}{/tex} and {tex}{}^{air}{\mu _{denser}}{/tex} are refractive indices of the rarer and denser media w.r.t. air, then {tex}\frac{{{}^{air}{\mu _{denser}}}}{{{}^{air}{\mu _{rarer}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{sin C}{/tex}
solution: If {tex}{}^{air}{\mu _{rarer}}{/tex} and {tex}{}^{air}{\mu _{denser}}{/tex} are refractive indices of the rarer and denser media w.r.t. air, then {tex}\frac{{{}^{air}{\mu _{denser}}}}{{{}^{air}{\mu _{rarer}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{sin C}{/tex}
solution_html:
If {tex}{}^{air}{\mu _{rarer}}{/tex} and {tex}{}^{air}{\mu _{denser}}{/tex} are refractive indices of the rarer and denser media w.r.t. air, then
{tex}\frac{{{}^{air}{\mu _{denser}}}}{{{}^{air}{\mu _{rarer}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{sin C}{/tex}

question_html: A point object is placed at the centre of a glass sphere of radius 6 cm and refractive index 1.5. The distance of virtual image from the surface of sphere is
question_text: A point object is placed at the centre of a glass sphere of radius 6 cm and refractive index 1.5. The distance of virtual image from the surface of sphere is

options.explanation: The rays coming from the point object fall on the glass-air interface normally and hence pass undeviated. Therefore if we retrace the path of the refracted rays backwards, the image will be formed at the centre only.

explanation: The rays coming from the point object fall on the glass-air interface normally and hence pass undeviated. Therefore if we retrace the path of the refracted rays backwards, the image will be formed at the centre only.

question_html: How is the focal length of a spherical mirror affected, when the wavelength of the light used is increased?
question_text: How is the focal length of a spherical mirror affected, when the wavelength of the light used is increased?
explanation: There is no effect of change in wavelength of light on the focal length of a spherical mirror.
solution: There is no effect of change in wavelength of light on the focal length of a spherical mirror.
solution_html: There is no effect of change in wavelength of light on the focal length of a spherical mirror.

subject: Physics
question_html: The refractive index of water is {tex}1\cdot33{/tex}. What will be the speed of light in water
question_text: The refractive index of water is {tex}1\cdot33{/tex} . What will be the speed of light in water

options.explanation: The speed of light in water is given by {tex}v = \frac{c}{\mu}=\frac{3\times10^8}{1\cdot33}=2\cdot255\times10^8 m s^{-1}{/tex}

explanation: The speed of light in water is given by {tex}v = \frac{c}{\mu}=\frac{3\times10^8}{1\cdot33}=2\cdot255\times10^8 m s^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Which spherical mirror is called a divergent mirror- concave or convex?
question_text: Which spherical mirror is called a divergent mirror- concave or convex?
explanation: A convex mirror is called a divergent mirror.
solution: A convex mirror is called a divergent mirror.
solution_html: A convex mirror is called a divergent mirror.
